frontmatter.title: Introduction
frontmatter.description: Welcome to Schema Weaver — a PostgreSQL schema management platform with a visual SQL Editor, ER diagram, migration engine, Data Explorer, and AI assistant

introduction.content:
Schema Weaver Documentation¶

Welcome to the official documentation for Schema Weaver. This guide covers everything from connecting your first PostgreSQL database to running migrations, exploring your data, and collaborating with your team.

What is Schema Weaver?¶

Schema Weaver is a PostgreSQL schema management platform. It gives you a visual SQL Editor for writing and organizing your schema, an auto-generated ER diagram that updates as you type, a migration engine for safely pushing changes to your live database, and an AI-powered Data Explorer for querying and analyzing your data — all in one workspace.

The Two Product Surfaces¶

Surface What it's for Link
SQL Editor Write schema, analyze it with the compiler, visualize it as an ER diagram, and manage migrations against a live database sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com
Data Explorer Browse tables, run SQL queries, analyze columns, and use Resona AI to explore data in natural language data.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com

Both surfaces share the same database connections, projects, and team memberships. You connect a database once and it is available everywhere.

What You Can Do¶

Schema Development

  • Write PostgreSQL DDL across multiple files and folders in a structured project
  • See your schema graded and analyzed across 20 quality layers in real time
  • Visualize every table, view, function, and relationship as an interactive ER diagram
  • Use Resona AI to generate SQL, explain schema decisions, and run surgical edits across files

Database Sync

  • Pull the live schema from any PostgreSQL database into your project files
  • Diff your project SQL against the live database before pushing
  • Apply migrations safely — with advisory locking, drift detection, and automatic rollback support
  • Detect when someone changed the database outside of Schema Weaver

Data Exploration

  • Browse any table with a high-performance, filterable data grid
  • Run custom SQL queries with syntax highlighting and autocomplete
  • Analyze columns — null rates, distinct counts, value distributions
  • Use Resona AI to write queries, generate charts, and build reports from your data

Team Collaboration

  • Invite teammates to projects with owner or member roles
  • Share database connections without sharing credentials
  • Track every file edit and migration by author and timestamp

Quick Start¶

  1. Open the SQL Editor and create a new project
  2. Add a .sql file and write your first CREATE TABLE statement
  3. Watch the ER Diagram and Schema Compiler update automatically
  4. Connect a PostgreSQL database via the Connect button in the footer
  5. Run sw pull in the terminal to import the live schema, or sw push --apply to push your changes

Explore the Documentation¶

  • Database — add connections, pull, diff, push migrations, detect drift
  • SQL Editor — files, schema compiler, ER diagram, AI assistant
  • Data Explorer — browse tables, run queries, AI-assisted analysis
  • Resona AI — models, credits, and how the AI assistant works
  • Team Collaboration — invite members, roles, and shared access

schemaweaver.vivekmind.com · SQL Editor · Data Explorer

# [Schema Weaver](https://schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) Documentation

Welcome to the official documentation for Schema Weaver. This guide covers everything from connecting your first PostgreSQL database to running migrations, exploring your data, and collaborating with your team.

---

## What is Schema Weaver?

Schema Weaver is a PostgreSQL schema management platform. It gives you a visual SQL Editor for writing and organizing your schema, an auto-generated ER diagram that updates as you type, a migration engine for safely pushing changes to your live database, and an AI-powered Data Explorer for querying and analyzing your data — all in one workspace.

---

## The Two Product Surfaces

| Surface | What it's for | Link |
|---|---|---|
| **SQL Editor** | Write schema, analyze it with the compiler, visualize it as an ER diagram, and manage migrations against a live database | [sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) |
| **Data Explorer** | Browse tables, run SQL queries, analyze columns, and use Resona AI to explore data in natural language | [data.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com](https://data.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) |

Both surfaces share the same database connections, projects, and team memberships. You connect a database once and it is available everywhere.

---

## What You Can Do

**Schema Development**
- Write PostgreSQL DDL across multiple files and folders in a structured project
- See your schema graded and analyzed across 20 quality layers in real time
- Visualize every table, view, function, and relationship as an interactive ER diagram
- Use Resona AI to generate SQL, explain schema decisions, and run surgical edits across files

**Database Sync**
- Pull the live schema from any PostgreSQL database into your project files
- Diff your project SQL against the live database before pushing
- Apply migrations safely — with advisory locking, drift detection, and automatic rollback support
- Detect when someone changed the database outside of Schema Weaver

**Data Exploration**
- Browse any table with a high-performance, filterable data grid
- Run custom SQL queries with syntax highlighting and autocomplete
- Analyze columns — null rates, distinct counts, value distributions
- Use Resona AI to write queries, generate charts, and build reports from your data

**Team Collaboration**
- Invite teammates to projects with owner or member roles
- Share database connections without sharing credentials
- Track every file edit and migration by author and timestamp

---

## Quick Start

1. Open the **[SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/)** and create a new project
2. Add a `.sql` file and write your first `CREATE TABLE` statement
3. Watch the **ER Diagram** and **Schema Compiler** update automatically
4. Connect a PostgreSQL database via the **Connect** button in the footer
5. Run `sw pull` in the terminal to import the live schema, or `sw push --apply` to push your changes

---

## Explore the Documentation

- [Database](/database) — add connections, pull, diff, push migrations, detect drift
- [SQL Editor](/sql-editor) — files, schema compiler, ER diagram, AI assistant
- [Data Explorer](/data-explorer) — browse tables, run queries, AI-assisted analysis
- [Resona AI](/resona-ai) — models, credits, and how the AI assistant works
- [Team Collaboration](/team-collaboration) — invite members, roles, and shared access

---

frontmatter.title: Adding a Connection
frontmatter.description: Add a PostgreSQL connection to Schema Weaver — fill in credentials, choose an SSL mode, test the connection, and understand how credentials are stored securely

database/adding-a-connection.content:
Adding a Connection¶

All database connections in Schema Weaver are added from the SQL Editor. Once saved, a connection is available across both the SQL Editor (for schema migrations) and the Data Explorer (for browsing and querying your data).

Quick links: Database Overview · Connection Dashboard

How to Add a Connection¶

  1. Open the SQL Editor
  2. Click Connect in the footer status bar at the bottom of the editor
  3. The connection panel opens — click + Add Connection
  4. Fill in your PostgreSQL credentials and click Test Connection
  5. Click Save once the test passes

Connection Fields¶

Field Default Description
Name — A label for this connection (e.g. production, staging, local)
Host — Hostname or IP address of your PostgreSQL server (e.g. db.example.com)
Port 5432 PostgreSQL port — almost always 5432
Database — The database name (e.g. postgres, myapp_production)
Username — PostgreSQL role to connect as
Password — Password for that role
SSL Mode Require Encryption and certificate verification level (see below)

SSL Mode¶

SSL mode controls how Schema Weaver encrypts the connection to your database.

Option When to use
Disable Local databases only (localhost, Docker containers). No encryption. Do not use for cloud or remote databases.
Require Cloud-hosted databases — Supabase, AWS RDS, Railway, Render, Neon, Google Cloud SQL, and similar. Encrypts the connection but does not verify the server certificate. This is the right choice for most databases.
Verify Full High-security production environments where certificate validation is strictly required. Verifies the server certificate and hostname against a configured CA. Only works if your database server has a valid CA certificate configured.

Recommendation: Use Require for any cloud-hosted database. Use Disable only for local development. Use Verify Full only when your database server is configured with a valid CA and you need strict certificate validation.

Testing the Connection¶

Click Test Connection before saving. The test:

  • Confirms the host is reachable from Schema Weaver's servers
  • Verifies the credentials are valid
  • Checks that the user has sufficient permissions to introspect the database
  • Reports the PostgreSQL version and current latency

If the test fails, the error message will tell you whether the issue is connectivity (host/port wrong, firewall block), credentials (username/password wrong), or permissions (role lacks required access).

Required PostgreSQL Permissions¶

The PostgreSQL role you connect as needs the following permissions for full functionality:

Permission Required for
CONNECT on the database Any operation at all
USAGE on schemas Reading schema structure and running queries
SELECT on tables Pull, diff, drift detection, Data Explorer browsing
CREATE Creating new tables, indexes, functions, views
ALTER Modifying existing columns, constraints, and types
DROP Destructive migrations (sw push --force only)

For read-only use in the Data Explorer, CONNECT + USAGE + SELECT is sufficient. For schema migrations in the SQL Editor, the role needs CREATE and ALTER at minimum.

Connection Security¶

Credentials are stored encrypted in Schema Weaver's backend:

  • Encryption: AES-256 at rest
  • Never in the browser: Credentials are sent to the server at save time and never returned to the client again
  • Team access: Team members with access to your project can use the connection to run operations, but they cannot see or export the password
  • Server-side only: Credentials are only used when a backend operation (pull, diff, migration, query) is triggered — they are never returned by any API endpoint

Supported PostgreSQL Versions¶

Version Status
PostgreSQL 12 ✅ Full support
PostgreSQL 13 ✅ Full support
PostgreSQL 14 ✅ Full support — ALTER TYPE … ADD VALUE works inside transactions
PostgreSQL 15 ✅ Full support
PostgreSQL 16 ✅ Full support
PostgreSQL 17 ✅ Full support

Common Connection Setups¶

Supabase¶

  • Host: Your Supabase project's pooler host (e.g. aws-0-us-east-1.pooler.supabase.com)
  • Port: 6543 (transaction mode) or 5432 (session mode)
  • Database: postgres
  • Username: postgres.[project-ref]
  • SSL Mode: Require

AWS RDS / Aurora¶

  • Host: Your RDS endpoint (e.g. mydb.cluster-xyz.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com)
  • Port: 5432
  • SSL Mode: Require

Neon¶

  • Host: Your Neon host (e.g. ep-xyz.us-east-2.aws.neon.tech)
  • SSL Mode: Require

Railway¶

  • Host: Your Railway PostgreSQL host
  • SSL Mode: Require

Local / Docker¶

  • Host: localhost or 127.0.0.1
  • Port: 5432 (or whichever port you've mapped)
  • SSL Mode: Disable

Next Steps¶

  • Connection Dashboard — managing your connections list and linking to projects
  • Pull, Diff & Push — start syncing your schema once a connection is active

# Adding a Connection

All database connections in Schema Weaver are added from the SQL Editor. Once saved, a connection is available across both the SQL Editor (for schema migrations) and the Data Explorer (for browsing and querying your data).

> Quick links: [Database Overview](/database) · [Connection Dashboard](/database/connection-dashboard)

---

## How to Add a Connection

1. Open the [SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/)
2. Click **Connect** in the footer status bar at the bottom of the editor
3. The connection panel opens — click **+ Add Connection**
4. Fill in your PostgreSQL credentials and click **Test Connection**
5. Click **Save** once the test passes

![The Add Connection form showing Host, Port, Database, Username, Password, and SSL Mode fields](/images/sql-editor/connect-live-database.png)

---

## Connection Fields

| Field | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Name** | — | A label for this connection (e.g. `production`, `staging`, `local`) |
| **Host** | — | Hostname or IP address of your PostgreSQL server (e.g. `db.example.com`) |
| **Port** | `5432` | PostgreSQL port — almost always 5432 |
| **Database** | — | The database name (e.g. `postgres`, `myapp_production`) |
| **Username** | — | PostgreSQL role to connect as |
| **Password** | — | Password for that role |
| **SSL Mode** | Require | Encryption and certificate verification level (see below) |

---

## SSL Mode

SSL mode controls how Schema Weaver encrypts the connection to your database.

![The SSL Mode dropdown showing Disable, Require, and Verify Full options with descriptions](/images/sql-editor/connect-databse-with-ssl-mode.png)

| Option | When to use |
|---|---|
| **Disable** | Local databases only (`localhost`, Docker containers). No encryption. Do not use for cloud or remote databases. |
| **Require** | Cloud-hosted databases — Supabase, AWS RDS, Railway, Render, Neon, Google Cloud SQL, and similar. Encrypts the connection but does not verify the server certificate. This is the right choice for most databases. |
| **Verify Full** | High-security production environments where certificate validation is strictly required. Verifies the server certificate and hostname against a configured CA. Only works if your database server has a valid CA certificate configured. |

> **Recommendation:** Use **Require** for any cloud-hosted database. Use **Disable** only for local development. Use **Verify Full** only when your database server is configured with a valid CA and you need strict certificate validation.

---

## Testing the Connection

Click **Test Connection** before saving. The test:

- Confirms the host is reachable from Schema Weaver's servers
- Verifies the credentials are valid
- Checks that the user has sufficient permissions to introspect the database
- Reports the PostgreSQL version and current latency

If the test fails, the error message will tell you whether the issue is connectivity (host/port wrong, firewall block), credentials (username/password wrong), or permissions (role lacks required access).

---

## Required PostgreSQL Permissions

The PostgreSQL role you connect as needs the following permissions for full functionality:

| Permission | Required for |
|---|---|
| `CONNECT` on the database | Any operation at all |
| `USAGE` on schemas | Reading schema structure and running queries |
| `SELECT` on tables | Pull, diff, drift detection, Data Explorer browsing |
| `CREATE` | Creating new tables, indexes, functions, views |
| `ALTER` | Modifying existing columns, constraints, and types |
| `DROP` | Destructive migrations (`sw push --force` only) |

For read-only use in the Data Explorer, `CONNECT + USAGE + SELECT` is sufficient. For schema migrations in the SQL Editor, the role needs `CREATE` and `ALTER` at minimum.

---

## Connection Security

Credentials are stored encrypted in Schema Weaver's backend:

- **Encryption:** AES-256 at rest
- **Never in the browser:** Credentials are sent to the server at save time and never returned to the client again
- **Team access:** Team members with access to your project can use the connection to run operations, but they cannot see or export the password
- **Server-side only:** Credentials are only used when a backend operation (pull, diff, migration, query) is triggered — they are never returned by any API endpoint

---

## Supported PostgreSQL Versions

| Version | Status |
|---|---|
| PostgreSQL 12 | ✅ Full support |
| PostgreSQL 13 | ✅ Full support |
| PostgreSQL 14 | ✅ Full support — `ALTER TYPE … ADD VALUE` works inside transactions |
| PostgreSQL 15 | ✅ Full support |
| PostgreSQL 16 | ✅ Full support |
| PostgreSQL 17 | ✅ Full support |

---

## Common Connection Setups

### Supabase
- **Host:** Your Supabase project's pooler host (e.g. `aws-0-us-east-1.pooler.supabase.com`)
- **Port:** `6543` (transaction mode) or `5432` (session mode)
- **Database:** `postgres`
- **Username:** `postgres.[project-ref]`
- **SSL Mode:** Require

### AWS RDS / Aurora
- **Host:** Your RDS endpoint (e.g. `mydb.cluster-xyz.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com`)
- **Port:** `5432`
- **SSL Mode:** Require

### Neon
- **Host:** Your Neon host (e.g. `ep-xyz.us-east-2.aws.neon.tech`)
- **SSL Mode:** Require

### Railway
- **Host:** Your Railway PostgreSQL host
- **SSL Mode:** Require

### Local / Docker
- **Host:** `localhost` or `127.0.0.1`
- **Port:** `5432` (or whichever port you've mapped)
- **SSL Mode:** Disable

---

## Next Steps

- [Connection Dashboard](/database/connection-dashboard) — managing your connections list and linking to projects
- [Pull, Diff & Push](/database/pull-diff-push) — start syncing your schema once a connection is active

database/connection-dashboard.content:
Connection Dashboard¶

Once you've added one or more connections, Schema Weaver gives you several views for managing and using them — a connections list, a sidebar panel while you're working in the SQL Editor, and a full connection dashboard for each connection.

Quick links: Adding a Connection · Pull, Diff & Push

Connections List¶

After adding connections they appear in the connection panel. Open the panel from the SQL Editor by clicking Connect in the footer status bar.

Each row in the list shows:

Element Description
Connection name The label you gave when creating the connection
Green dot Connection is healthy — last test passed
Gray dot Connection is offline or has not been tested recently
Ping time Latency from the last successful health check (e.g. 241ms)
Database name The PostgreSQL database this connection points to
Linked file The SQL file currently associated with this connection
LINKED badge This connection is currently linked to the open project
Link button Link this connection to the current project
Dashboard button Open the full connection dashboard

Connection Sidebar Panel¶

When a connection is linked to your current project, a panel appears in the left sidebar of the SQL Editor showing the live connection state.

The sidebar panel shows:

  • Connection name and live ping — latency is measured and displayed in real time
  • Database name and PostgreSQL version — read directly from the server
  • SSL mode in use
  • Linked project — which project this connection is attached to, with an Unlink button
  • Pull / Push / Diff / Health — the four primary migration actions (see Pull, Diff & Push)
  • Recent Events — a timestamped log of recent operations: pulls, pushes, diffs, links, and connects

Full Connection Dashboard¶

Click Dashboard on any connection in the list to open its full-width view.

The dashboard has four sections:

Section What it shows
Project Connection Which project is currently linked to this connection, with an Unlink button
Linked Files The SQL file(s) linked to this connection — the CURRENT badge marks the active file, and a timestamp shows when it was last updated
Migrations The full migration history — every push applied through Schema Weaver with its status, timestamp, and who ran it
Live Activity A real-time event log on the right — PULL, PUSH, DIFF, LINK, CONNECT events with success/failure and metadata

Linking a Connection to a Project¶

Each project can have one active connection. The linked connection is used for all migration and schema sync operations — drift detection, pull, diff, push, and rollback all run against it.

To link from the connections list:

  1. Open the connection panel (Connect in the footer)
  2. Find the connection you want to use
  3. Click Link — it is now attached to the currently open project
  4. The LINKED badge appears on that connection

To link from the dashboard:

  1. Open the Dashboard for the connection
  2. Under Project Connection, select the project from the dropdown
  3. Click Link

Once linked, every team member with access to the project can use the connection to run operations — no one needs to re-enter credentials.

One Connection, Both Tools¶

Connections you add in the SQL Editor are visible in the Data Explorer automatically. There is no separate connection setup for the Data Explorer — it reads from the same connection list.

When you open the Data Explorer and select a connection, you are using the same saved credentials and the same connection pool. Changes to a connection (e.g. updating the password) take effect immediately across both tools.

Next Steps¶

  • Pull, Diff & Push — start syncing your schema
  • Migrations — apply and track schema changes
  • Terminal & sw Commands — run operations from the built-in terminal

# Connection Dashboard

Once you've added one or more connections, Schema Weaver gives you several views for managing and using them — a connections list, a sidebar panel while you're working in the SQL Editor, and a full connection dashboard for each connection.

> Quick links: [Adding a Connection](/database/adding-a-connection) · [Pull, Diff & Push](/database/pull-diff-push)

---

## Connections List

After adding connections they appear in the connection panel. Open the panel from the SQL Editor by clicking **Connect** in the footer status bar.

![The connections list showing multiple connections with their ping times, linked files, and Link/Dashboard action buttons](/images/sql-editor/after-connection.png)

Each row in the list shows:

| Element | Description |
|---|---|
| **Connection name** | The label you gave when creating the connection |
| **Green dot** | Connection is healthy — last test passed |
| **Gray dot** | Connection is offline or has not been tested recently |
| **Ping time** | Latency from the last successful health check (e.g. `241ms`) |
| **Database name** | The PostgreSQL database this connection points to |
| **Linked file** | The SQL file currently associated with this connection |
| **LINKED badge** | This connection is currently linked to the open project |
| **Link button** | Link this connection to the current project |
| **Dashboard button** | Open the full connection dashboard |

---

## Connection Sidebar Panel

When a connection is linked to your current project, a panel appears in the left sidebar of the SQL Editor showing the live connection state.

![The connection panel in the SQL Editor left sidebar showing connection name, ping, database info, linked project, and Pull/Push/Diff/Health action buttons](/images/sql-editor/connection-panle-leftsidebar.png)

The sidebar panel shows:

- **Connection name and live ping** — latency is measured and displayed in real time
- **Database name and PostgreSQL version** — read directly from the server
- **SSL mode** in use
- **Linked project** — which project this connection is attached to, with an Unlink button
- **Pull / Push / Diff / Health** — the four primary migration actions (see [Pull, Diff & Push](/database/pull-diff-push))
- **Recent Events** — a timestamped log of recent operations: pulls, pushes, diffs, links, and connects

---

## Full Connection Dashboard

Click **Dashboard** on any connection in the list to open its full-width view.

![The connection dashboard showing Project Connection, Linked Files, Migration History, and a Live Activity panel](/images/sql-editor/connection-dashbaord.png)

The dashboard has four sections:

| Section | What it shows |
|---|---|
| **Project Connection** | Which project is currently linked to this connection, with an Unlink button |
| **Linked Files** | The SQL file(s) linked to this connection — the CURRENT badge marks the active file, and a timestamp shows when it was last updated |
| **Migrations** | The full migration history — every push applied through Schema Weaver with its status, timestamp, and who ran it |
| **Live Activity** | A real-time event log on the right — PULL, PUSH, DIFF, LINK, CONNECT events with success/failure and metadata |

---

## Linking a Connection to a Project

Each project can have one active connection. The linked connection is used for all migration and schema sync operations — drift detection, pull, diff, push, and rollback all run against it.

**To link from the connections list:**
1. Open the connection panel (**Connect** in the footer)
2. Find the connection you want to use
3. Click **Link** — it is now attached to the currently open project
4. The **LINKED** badge appears on that connection

**To link from the dashboard:**
1. Open the Dashboard for the connection
2. Under **Project Connection**, select the project from the dropdown
3. Click **Link**

Once linked, every team member with access to the project can use the connection to run operations — no one needs to re-enter credentials.

---

## One Connection, Both Tools

Connections you add in the SQL Editor are visible in the **Data Explorer** automatically. There is no separate connection setup for the Data Explorer — it reads from the same connection list.

When you open the Data Explorer and select a connection, you are using the same saved credentials and the same connection pool. Changes to a connection (e.g. updating the password) take effect immediately across both tools.

---

## Next Steps

- [Pull, Diff & Push](/database/pull-diff-push) — start syncing your schema
- [Migrations](/database/migrations) — apply and track schema changes
- [Terminal & sw Commands](/database/terminal) — run operations from the built-in terminal

frontmatter.title: Drift Detection
frontmatter.description: Detect when your live PostgreSQL database has drifted from your project SQL — understand what causes drift, how Schema Weaver detects it, and how to resolve it

headings.title: Keep the external changes (they are intentional)
Discard the external changes (they should not have been made)

database/drift-detection.content:
Drift Detection¶

Drift happens when someone (or something) changes your live database's schema outside of Schema Weaver. Your project SQL says one thing, but the actual database looks different. Schema Weaver detects this automatically and prevents you from overwriting those changes unknowingly.

Quick links: Pull, Diff & Push · Migrations

What Causes Drift¶

Drift can be introduced by:

  • A developer running ALTER TABLE or CREATE INDEX directly on the database
  • An ORM or framework auto-migrating the database on startup (e.g. Django makemigrations, Prisma db push)
  • A database restore that overwrote part of the schema with an older state
  • A PostgreSQL extension upgrade that modified catalog entries
  • Emergency hotfixes applied manually during an incident

Drift is not a sign that something went wrong — it is a normal part of working with shared databases. Schema Weaver's job is to make sure you know about it before you accidentally overwrite it.

How Drift Detection Works¶

Every time you run sw pull or sw push --apply, Schema Weaver saves a snapshot of the live schema. The snapshot includes a fingerprint — a hash derived from the database's structural identity (including PostgreSQL's internal pg_control_system metadata and the database OID).

When you later run sw push --apply, Schema Weaver checks the current live schema fingerprint against the stored snapshot before executing any DDL. If the fingerprints do not match, it means the live schema has changed since your last operation.

⚠ Drift detected since last snapshot. Run `sw pull` to sync before pushing.

The migration is aborted. The database is not touched.

Checking for Drift Manually¶

You can check for drift at any time without starting a migration:

cloop sw > sw drift
Checking for external schema changes...
  ⚠ Drift detected (2 changes):
    + Column: users.temp_migration_flag (added directly)
    ~ Index: idx_orders_status (modified outside Schema Weaver)

sw drift tells you:

  • How many changes were detected
  • Which objects were added, modified, or removed
  • Whether those changes are in conflict with your project SQL

If no drift is detected:

cloop sw > sw drift
✓ No drift detected. Live schema matches last snapshot.

Drift vs Your Project SQL¶

There are two distinct comparisons you can make:

Comparison Command What it tells you
Live DB vs last snapshot sw drift Changes made to the database since your last pull or push
Live DB vs project SQL sw diff Difference between what you've written and what's deployed

Use sw drift to detect external changes. Use sw diff to plan your next migration.

Resolving Drift¶

When drift is detected before a push, the recommended resolution depends on what you want to keep:

Keep the external changes (they are intentional)¶

sw pull

Pull imports the current live schema — including the externally made changes — into your project SQL. Your SQL file is now up to date with the live state. From here, make any additional changes you want and push normally.

Discard the external changes (they should not have been made)¶

Use sw diff to see exactly what the external changes are. If you push your project SQL with sw push --apply, the diff engine will generate the statements needed to undo those changes and restore your intended state.

Before discarding external changes, make sure you understand what they were and whether any data depends on them. Dropping a column that was added externally will drop the data in that column.

When you are unsure¶

Use sw drift to list the changes, then sw diff to see how your project SQL compares to the current live state. You can also view the live schema directly by running sw pull to a temporary copy and reviewing it before deciding.

Automatic Drift Check During Push¶

You do not need to run sw drift manually before every push. The drift check runs automatically as part of sw push --apply (Step 5 of the migration pipeline). If drift is detected at that point, the migration is aborted before any statements execute.

This means the database is always protected against accidental overwrites, even if you forget to check for drift manually.

Next Steps¶

  • Pull, Diff & Push — resolving drift with pull, and planning your next migration with diff
  • Migrations — the full migration pipeline including drift check timing
  • History & Rollback — if a migration applied changes you want to reverse
database/drift-detection.raw:
---
title: Drift Detection
description: Detect when your live PostgreSQL database has drifted from your project SQL — understand what causes drift, how Schema Weaver detects it, and how to resolve it
tags: [database, drift, detection, schema, sync, migration]
order: 5
sidebar_group: "Database"
related:
  - title: Pull, Diff & Push
    path: /database/pull-diff-push
  - title: Migrations
    path: /database/migrations
  - title: History & Rollback
    path: /database/history-rollback
---

# Drift Detection

Drift happens when someone (or something) changes your live database's schema outside of Schema Weaver. Your project SQL says one thing, but the actual database looks different. Schema Weaver detects this automatically and prevents you from overwriting those changes unknowingly.

> Quick links: [Pull, Diff & Push](/database/pull-diff-push) · [Migrations](/database/migrations)

---

## What Causes Drift

Drift can be introduced by:

- A developer running `ALTER TABLE` or `CREATE INDEX` directly on the database
- An ORM or framework auto-migrating the database on startup (e.g. Django `makemigrations`, Prisma `db push`)
- A database restore that overwrote part of the schema with an older state
- A PostgreSQL extension upgrade that modified catalog entries
- Emergency hotfixes applied manually during an incident

Drift is not a sign that something went wrong — it is a normal part of working with shared databases. Schema Weaver's job is to make sure you know about it before you accidentally overwrite it.

---

## How Drift Detection Works

Every time you run `sw pull` or `sw push --apply`, Schema Weaver saves a **snapshot** of the live schema. The snapshot includes a **fingerprint** — a hash derived from the database's structural identity (including PostgreSQL's internal `pg_control_system` metadata and the database OID).

When you later run `sw push --apply`, Schema Weaver checks the current live schema fingerprint against the stored snapshot **before executing any DDL**. If the fingerprints do not match, it means the live schema has changed since your last operation.

```
⚠ Drift detected since last snapshot. Run `sw pull` to sync before pushing.
```

The migration is aborted. The database is not touched.

---

## Checking for Drift Manually

You can check for drift at any time without starting a migration:

```
cloop sw > sw drift
Checking for external schema changes...
  ⚠ Drift detected (2 changes):
    + Column: users.temp_migration_flag (added directly)
    ~ Index: idx_orders_status (modified outside Schema Weaver)
```

`sw drift` tells you:
- How many changes were detected
- Which objects were added, modified, or removed
- Whether those changes are in conflict with your project SQL

If no drift is detected:

```
cloop sw > sw drift
✓ No drift detected. Live schema matches last snapshot.
```

---

## Drift vs Your Project SQL

There are two distinct comparisons you can make:

| Comparison | Command | What it tells you |
|---|---|---|
| Live DB vs last snapshot | `sw drift` | Changes made to the database since your last pull or push |
| Live DB vs project SQL | `sw diff` | Difference between what you've written and what's deployed |

Use `sw drift` to detect external changes. Use `sw diff` to plan your next migration.

---

## Resolving Drift

When drift is detected before a push, the recommended resolution depends on what you want to keep:

### Keep the external changes (they are intentional)

```
sw pull
```

Pull imports the current live schema — including the externally made changes — into your project SQL. Your SQL file is now up to date with the live state. From here, make any additional changes you want and push normally.

### Discard the external changes (they should not have been made)

Use `sw diff` to see exactly what the external changes are. If you push your project SQL with `sw push --apply`, the diff engine will generate the statements needed to undo those changes and restore your intended state.

> Before discarding external changes, make sure you understand what they were and whether any data depends on them. Dropping a column that was added externally will drop the data in that column.

### When you are unsure

Use `sw drift` to list the changes, then `sw diff` to see how your project SQL compares to the current live state. You can also view the live schema directly by running `sw pull` to a temporary copy and reviewing it before deciding.

---

## Automatic Drift Check During Push

You do not need to run `sw drift` manually before every push. The drift check runs **automatically as part of `sw push --apply`** (Step 5 of the migration pipeline). If drift is detected at that point, the migration is aborted before any statements execute.

This means the database is always protected against accidental overwrites, even if you forget to check for drift manually.

---

## Next Steps

- [Pull, Diff & Push](/database/pull-diff-push) — resolving drift with pull, and planning your next migration with diff
- [Migrations](/database/migrations) — the full migration pipeline including drift check timing
- [History & Rollback](/database/history-rollback) — if a migration applied changes you want to reverse

frontmatter.title: History & Rollback
frontmatter.description: View the full audit trail of schema migrations for each connection, and roll back applied migrations with automatically generated reverse SQL

headings.title: What Rollback Can and Cannot Undo

database/history-rollback.content:
History & Rollback¶

Every migration applied through Schema Weaver is recorded in a persistent history log — who ran it, when, and what the outcome was. If a migration causes a problem, you can roll it back with a single command. The reverse SQL is generated automatically at apply time, so rollback is always available.

Quick links: Migrations · Terminal & sw Commands

Migration History¶

sw history lists all migrations applied through Schema Weaver for the active connection, in reverse chronological order.

cloop sw > sw history
Migration History — cloop
────────────────────────────────────────────────
  #12   2026-04-01   success        alice@company.com
  #11   2026-03-20   success        you@company.com
  #10   2026-03-15   success        you@company.com
  #9    2026-03-01   rolled_back
  #8    2026-02-15   success        you@company.com
  ...

Each entry records:

Field Description
Migration number A sequence number, incremented per connection
Date When the migration was applied
Status success, rolled_back, or failed
Applied by The user who triggered the migration — email address for team accountability

The same history is visible in the Connection Dashboard under the Migrations section, where you can also see timestamps and status at a glance.

Hash Chain Integrity¶

Each migration record includes a parent_hash — a cryptographic link to the previous migration. This forms a tamper-evident chain: if any record is modified or deleted, the chain breaks and Schema Weaver can detect it.

This audit trail is useful for:

  • Compliance reviews and change audits
  • Debugging unexpected schema state
  • Identifying which migration introduced a particular change

Rollback¶

sw rollback reverses the last applied migration. It executes the reverse SQL that was computed and stored when the migration was originally applied.

cloop sw > sw rollback
Rolling back migration #12...
  Executing reverse SQL...
  ✓ DROP TABLE notifications
  ✓ ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN last_login
  ✓ DROP INDEX idx_users_last_login
Rollback complete. Schema restored to migration #11.

How Reverse SQL Is Generated¶

When sw push --apply succeeds, Schema Weaver immediately runs the diff in reverse — comparing the post-migration state against the pre-migration state — and stores the resulting SQL as the migration's down_sql. This happens at apply time, while Schema Weaver has full context of both states.

This means rollback does not require a separate command to generate the reverse SQL — it is always ready.

Rolling Back Multiple Migrations¶

To roll back more than one migration, pass the number of steps:

sw rollback 3

This rolls back migrations #12, #11, and #10, in that order. Each migration is reversed individually so that partial progress is recorded if a rollback fails mid-sequence.

What Rollback Can and Cannot Undo¶

Rollback restores the schema structure as it was before the migration. However, it cannot restore data that was deleted.

Situation What rollback does
A table was created ✅ Drops the table
A column was added ✅ Drops the column
An index was created ✅ Drops the index
A column was dropped (destructive migration) ⚠️ Recreates the column definition — but the data rows are gone
A table was dropped (destructive migration) ⚠️ Recreates the table structure — but all rows are gone
An enum value was added ✅ Can remove the enum value if no rows use it
An enum value was removed ❌ PostgreSQL cannot remove enum values that have been in use

For destructive migrations, the best protection is reviewing sw push --force output carefully before confirming. Once a column or table is dropped and the migration commits, the data cannot be recovered through rollback.

Viewing History in the Dashboard¶

The Connection Dashboard shows the full migration history in the Migrations section. Each entry shows:

  • Migration number and timestamp
  • Status badge (success / rolled_back / failed)
  • Who applied it (for team connections)

You can view the history without opening the terminal by clicking Dashboard on the connection in the connections list.

Next Steps¶

  • Migrations — the full apply pipeline and how reverse SQL is generated
  • Drift Detection — preventing the need for rollback by catching external changes early
  • Terminal & sw Commands — complete sw history and sw rollback command reference

## Rollback

```
Rolling back migration #12...

  ✓ ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN last_login

Rollback complete. Schema restored to migration #11.
```

This means rollback does not require a separate command to generate the reverse SQL — it is always ready.

### Rolling Back Multiple Migrations

---

## What Rollback Can and Cannot Undo

| Situation | What rollback does |
|---|---|
| A table was created | ✅ Drops the table |
| A column was added | ✅ Drops the column |
| An index was created | ✅ Drops the index |
| A column was dropped (destructive migration) | ⚠️ Recreates the column definition — but the data rows are gone |
| A table was dropped (destructive migration) | ⚠️ Recreates the table structure — but all rows are gone |
| An enum value was added | ✅ Can remove the enum value if no rows use it |
| An enum value was removed | ❌ PostgreSQL cannot remove enum values that have been in use |

> For destructive migrations, the best protection is reviewing `sw push --force` output carefully before confirming. Once a column or table is dropped and the migration commits, the data cannot be recovered through rollback.

---

## Viewing History in the Dashboard

The Connection Dashboard shows the full migration history in the **Migrations** section. Each entry shows:

- Migration number and timestamp
- Status badge (success / rolled_back / failed)
- Who applied it (for team connections)

You can view the history without opening the terminal by clicking **Dashboard** on the connection in the connections list.

---

## Next Steps

- [Migrations](/database/migrations) — the full apply pipeline and how reverse SQL is generated
- [Drift Detection](/database/drift-detection) — preventing the need for rollback by catching external changes early
- [Terminal & sw Commands](/database/terminal) — complete `sw history` and `sw rollback` command reference

frontmatter.title: Database
frontmatter.description: Connect Schema Weaver to your live PostgreSQL databases — manage connections, sync schemas, run migrations, detect drift, and explore your data across the SQL Editor and Data Explorer

headings.title: What You Can Do With a Connection
What Schema Weaver Reads From Your Database

database.content:
Database¶

Schema Weaver is built for PostgreSQL. Every live-database feature — schema sync, migration, drift detection, data exploration — works exclusively with PostgreSQL. This focus means every part of the system is purpose-built for PostgreSQL's type system, DDL model, catalog structure, and extension ecosystem.

Quick links: SQL Editor · Data Explorer

Where Connections Live¶

All database connections are created and managed from the SQL Editor. Open the SQL Editor, click Connect in the footer status bar, and add your PostgreSQL credentials. Once a connection is saved, it is available everywhere in your Schema Weaver workspace — including the Data Explorer.

You do not manage separate connections for the SQL Editor and the Data Explorer. One connection, used across both tools.

What You Can Do With a Connection¶

In the SQL Editor¶

Capability What it means
Pull Introspect the live database and import its full schema into your project SQL files
Diff Compare your project SQL against the live database and see exactly what DDL would change
Push Preview, apply, and track schema migrations against the live database
Drift detection Find schema changes that were made directly on the database outside Schema Weaver
Migration history Full audit trail of every applied migration, with rollback support
Terminal Run sw commands directly from the built-in terminal

In the Data Explorer¶

Capability What it means
Browse tables View all tables, schemas, and row counts from the live database
Run queries Execute SQL queries and see results in a high-performance grid
Column analysis Statistics, distributions, and null rates for any column
AI queries Ask Resona AI questions in plain English and get SQL back

PostgreSQL Versions Supported¶

Version Support
PostgreSQL 12 Full
PostgreSQL 13 Full
PostgreSQL 14 Full — ALTER TYPE … ADD VALUE works inside transactions
PostgreSQL 15 Full
PostgreSQL 16 Full
PostgreSQL 17 Full

What Schema Weaver Reads From Your Database¶

When you pull a schema or run a diff, Schema Weaver reads from two layers:

Structural layer (tables, indexes, constraints):

  • Tables, columns, data types, defaults, generated columns
  • Primary keys, foreign keys, unique constraints, check constraints
  • Indexes — including partial indexes and expression indexes
  • Sequences and identity columns
  • Enums and composite types
  • Extensions

Behavioral layer (procedural objects via PostgreSQL catalog):

  • Views and materialized views
  • Functions and stored procedures
  • Triggers
  • Row-Level Security policies

Not currently pulled: Collations, custom operators, foreign data wrappers, tablespaces, and event triggers are not included in pull output.

Security¶

Connection credentials are stored encrypted at rest (AES-256) in Schema Weaver's backend. They are:

  • Never stored in your browser
  • Never visible to other team members after the connection is saved
  • Never logged or returned by the API
  • Only used server-side when a migration, pull, or diff operation is triggered

Team members can use a shared connection to run migrations and explore data, but they cannot see or extract the password.

Next Steps¶

  • Adding a Connection — fill in credentials, choose SSL mode, test the connection
  • Connection Dashboard — manage your connections list, link to projects, view live activity
  • Pull, Diff & Push — the core workflow for syncing your schema
  • Migrations — applying changes, smart migration, and destructive change protection
  • Drift Detection — detect and resolve schema changes made outside Schema Weaver
  • History & Rollback — audit trail and rollback mechanics
  • Terminal & sw Commands — run sw commands from the built-in terminal

# Database

Schema Weaver is built for PostgreSQL. Every live-database feature — schema sync, migration, drift detection, data exploration — works exclusively with PostgreSQL. This focus means every part of the system is purpose-built for PostgreSQL's type system, DDL model, catalog structure, and extension ecosystem.

> Quick links: [SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [Data Explorer](https://data.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/)

---

## Where Connections Live

All database connections are created and managed from the **SQL Editor**. Open the SQL Editor, click **Connect** in the footer status bar, and add your PostgreSQL credentials. Once a connection is saved, it is available everywhere in your Schema Weaver workspace — including the **Data Explorer**.

You do not manage separate connections for the SQL Editor and the Data Explorer. One connection, used across both tools.

---

## What You Can Do With a Connection

### In the SQL Editor

| Capability | What it means |
|---|---|
| **Pull** | Introspect the live database and import its full schema into your project SQL files |
| **Diff** | Compare your project SQL against the live database and see exactly what DDL would change |
| **Push** | Preview, apply, and track schema migrations against the live database |
| **Drift detection** | Find schema changes that were made directly on the database outside Schema Weaver |
| **Migration history** | Full audit trail of every applied migration, with rollback support |
| **Terminal** | Run `sw` commands directly from the built-in terminal |

### In the Data Explorer

| Capability | What it means |
|---|---|

| **Run queries** | Execute SQL queries and see results in a high-performance grid |
| **Column analysis** | Statistics, distributions, and null rates for any column |
| **AI queries** | Ask Resona AI questions in plain English and get SQL back |

---

## PostgreSQL Versions Supported

| Version | Support |
|---|---|
| PostgreSQL 12 | Full |
| PostgreSQL 13 | Full |
| PostgreSQL 14 | Full — `ALTER TYPE … ADD VALUE` works inside transactions |
| PostgreSQL 15 | Full |
| PostgreSQL 16 | Full |
| PostgreSQL 17 | Full |

---

## What Schema Weaver Reads From Your Database

When you pull a schema or run a diff, Schema Weaver reads from two layers:

**Structural layer** (tables, indexes, constraints):
- Tables, columns, data types, defaults, generated columns
- Primary keys, foreign keys, unique constraints, check constraints
- Indexes — including partial indexes and expression indexes
- Sequences and identity columns
- Enums and composite types
- Extensions

**Behavioral layer** (procedural objects via PostgreSQL catalog):
- Views and materialized views
- Functions and stored procedures
- Triggers
- Row-Level Security policies

**Not currently pulled:**
Collations, custom operators, foreign data wrappers, tablespaces, and event triggers are not included in pull output.

---

## Security

Connection credentials are stored **encrypted at rest** (AES-256) in Schema Weaver's backend. They are:

- Never stored in your browser
- Never visible to other team members after the connection is saved
- Never logged or returned by the API
- Only used server-side when a migration, pull, or diff operation is triggered

Team members can use a shared connection to run migrations and explore data, but they cannot see or extract the password.

---

## Next Steps

frontmatter.title: Migrations
frontmatter.description: Apply schema changes to your live PostgreSQL database — understand the migration pipeline, use safe mode for complex type changes, and control destructive operations

database/migrations.content:
Migrations¶

sw push --apply applies your schema changes to the live database. Schema Weaver wraps every migration in a multi-step pipeline designed for correctness and safety — advisory locking to prevent concurrent changes, automatic drift checking before execution, and full transaction wrapping so partial failures roll back cleanly.

Quick links: Pull, Diff & Push · Drift Detection · History & Rollback

The Migration Pipeline¶

When you run sw push --apply, Schema Weaver executes eight steps in sequence:

Step 1 — Schema Diff¶

The structural diff runs outside any transaction. Atlas compares your project SQL against the live database and generates the DDL statements needed to bring the database in line with your project. At this stage, nothing is written to the database.

Step 2 — Validation¶

The generated DDL is analyzed for common issues and automatically corrected where possible (e.g. statement ordering, dependency resolution). If a critical issue is found that cannot be auto-corrected, the migration is halted before anything executes.

Step 3 — Destructive Change Check¶

The DDL is scanned for destructive operations — DROP TABLE, DROP COLUMN, incompatible type casts. If any are found, the migration is blocked unless you pass --force. See Destructive Change Protection below.

Step 4 — Advisory Lock¶

An exclusive advisory lock is acquired on your connection before any DDL runs. The lock key is derived from your connection ID, so two simultaneous migrations against the same connection are impossible. If the lock cannot be acquired, the operation waits briefly and retries before failing with a clear message.

Step 5 — Drift Check¶

Immediately before execution, Schema Weaver compares the current live schema fingerprint against the stored snapshot from your last pull or push. If someone has modified the database since your last operation, the migration is aborted with a SCHEMA_DRIFTED error. You must run sw pull to re-sync before pushing. See Drift Detection.

Step 6 — Structural DDL¶

Tables, columns, indexes, constraints, enums, and sequences are applied via Atlas, wrapped in a transaction. If any statement fails, the entire structural DDL block is rolled back.

Two special cases are handled outside the main transaction:

  • Enum ADD VALUE on PostgreSQL 13 and earlier: PostgreSQL does not allow ALTER TYPE ... ADD VALUE inside a transaction on PG ≤ 13. This statement is extracted and run before the transaction opens. On PG 14+, it runs inside the transaction normally.
  • CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY: PostgreSQL requires concurrent index builds to run outside a transaction. These are extracted and run after the transaction commits. If a concurrent build is interrupted, the rest of the migration is unaffected.

Step 7 — Behavioral DDL¶

After structural changes are committed, Schema Weaver syncs behavioral objects: functions, views, triggers, and RLS policies. These are applied using idempotent patterns — CREATE OR REPLACE for functions and views, and DROP + CREATE for triggers and policies. Materialized views with a unique index are refreshed with REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY to avoid blocking reads.

Step 8 — Snapshot and Audit¶

After all DDL is applied, Schema Weaver:

  • Re-introspects the live schema and saves a new snapshot
  • Generates reverse DDL (the SQL needed to undo this migration) and stores it for rollback
  • Records the migration in history with a timestamp, the applying user, and a parent_hash linking this migration to the previous one in an audit chain

Safe Mode — Complex Type Changes¶

Some ALTER COLUMN TYPE operations cannot be done with a plain ALTER TABLE because the existing data cannot be automatically cast to the new type. For example: changing a text column to integer, or changing numeric to bigint when precision would be lost.

sw push --safe detects these situations and applies a multi-phase migration that preserves all existing data.

cloop sw > sw push --safe
Analyzing migration for unsafe operations...
  ⚠ Column type change detected: orders.amount (numeric → bigint)
  Smart migration plan:
    Phase 1: Add shadow column
    Phase 2: Install sync trigger
    Phase 3: Backfill existing data (5,000 rows/batch)
    Phase 4: Validate constraints
    Phase 5: Atomic swap
    Phase 6: Recreate FK constraints
  Proceed? [y/n]

The Six Phases¶

Phase What happens
1 — Shadow column A new column with the target type is added alongside the existing one (orders.amount_new bigint)
2 — Sync trigger A BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE trigger is installed so any new writes are immediately mirrored to the shadow column
3 — Backfill Existing rows are updated in batches of 5,000 to convert the data, with a 50ms pause between batches to avoid locking pressure
4 — Validate Constraints and nullability are verified on the shadow column before the swap proceeds
5 — Swap In a single transaction: triggers are dropped, the original column is renamed away, and the shadow column takes its place
6 — FK cleanup Any foreign keys that referenced the original column are recreated pointing to the new column

This approach keeps the table available for reads and writes throughout — there are no long locks.

When Safe Mode Is Blocked¶

Not every type change can be handled with safe mode. Schema Weaver blocks smart migration and reports the reason when:

Situation Why it's blocked
The column is a primary key A PK type change requires restructuring the entire table's FK graph; this cannot be done safely column-by-column
The column is referenced by a foreign key in another table The FK relationship must be resolved before the type can change
The column has a UNIQUE constraint The shadow column approach cannot preserve uniqueness guarantees during backfill

When blocked, Schema Weaver describes the dependency chain so you can resolve it manually before retrying.

Impossible Casts¶

Some type changes have no valid cast expression at all. Schema Weaver detects these and throws an UNSAFE_CAST error before executing anything:

  • uuid ↔ integer, bigint, smallint
  • text / varchar → integer, bigint (lossy — text may not be numeric)
  • boolean ↔ integer
  • jsonb ↔ integer
  • bigint → integer, smallint
  • integer → smallint
  • numeric, real, double precision → integer, bigint, smallint

For these casts, there is no automatic path — you need to backfill and convert the data manually before changing the column type.

Force Mode — Destructive Changes¶

sw push --force bypasses the destructive change protection. It is required when your migration includes:

  • DROP TABLE
  • DROP COLUMN
  • A type cast that cannot be performed automatically (when not using safe mode)
cloop sw > sw push --force
⚠ WARNING: Destructive changes detected:
  DROP TABLE public.old_payments
  DROP COLUMN users.legacy_field
Type "confirm" to proceed:

You must type confirm explicitly. There is no flag to skip the confirmation prompt.

Data loss warning: Force mode drops data permanently. Schema Weaver does not make a backup before applying a force migration. Verify that the data being dropped is safe to remove before proceeding.

Destructive Change Protection¶

The following operations are blocked by default and require --force:

Operation Protected
DROP TABLE ✅ Yes
DROP COLUMN ✅ Yes
ALTER COLUMN TYPE with an incompatible cast ✅ Yes
DROP INDEX No — indexes hold no row data
DROP VIEW No
DROP FUNCTION No
DROP CONSTRAINT No

Concurrent Migration Protection¶

The advisory lock (Step 4 above) ensures that two migrations cannot run simultaneously against the same connection.

If you attempt to push while another migration is in progress:

⚠ Another migration is in progress for this connection. Try again in a moment.

The lock is released automatically when the migration completes or fails. If a migration was interrupted ungracefully (e.g. a server restart), the advisory lock is released when the PostgreSQL session that held it terminates.

Migration Commands¶

Command What it does
sw push Preview the migration — no changes applied
sw push --apply Apply the migration to the live database
sw push --safe Apply using multi-phase safe mode for complex type changes
sw push --force Apply including destructive operations (confirmation required)

Next Steps¶

  • Drift Detection — what happens when the database changes between your push commands
  • History & Rollback — reverse a migration you've applied
  • Terminal & sw Commands — full command reference
database/migrations.raw:
---
title: Migrations
description: Apply schema changes to your live PostgreSQL database — understand the migration pipeline, use safe mode for complex type changes, and control destructive operations
tags: [database, migration, push, apply, safe, force, pipeline, advisory-lock, transactions]
order: 4
sidebar_group: "Database"
related:
  - title: Pull, Diff & Push
    path: /database/pull-diff-push
  - title: Drift Detection
    path: /database/drift-detection
  - title: History & Rollback
    path: /database/history-rollback
---

# Migrations

`sw push --apply` applies your schema changes to the live database. Schema Weaver wraps every migration in a multi-step pipeline designed for correctness and safety — advisory locking to prevent concurrent changes, automatic drift checking before execution, and full transaction wrapping so partial failures roll back cleanly.

> Quick links: [Pull, Diff & Push](/database/pull-diff-push) · [Drift Detection](/database/drift-detection) · [History & Rollback](/database/history-rollback)

---

## The Migration Pipeline

When you run `sw push --apply`, Schema Weaver executes eight steps in sequence:

### Step 1 — Schema Diff
The structural diff runs outside any transaction. Atlas compares your project SQL against the live database and generates the DDL statements needed to bring the database in line with your project. At this stage, nothing is written to the database.

### Step 2 — Validation
The generated DDL is analyzed for common issues and automatically corrected where possible (e.g. statement ordering, dependency resolution). If a critical issue is found that cannot be auto-corrected, the migration is halted before anything executes.

### Step 3 — Destructive Change Check
The DDL is scanned for destructive operations — `DROP TABLE`, `DROP COLUMN`, incompatible type casts. If any are found, the migration is blocked unless you pass `--force`. See [Destructive Change Protection](#destructive-change-protection) below.

### Step 4 — Advisory Lock
An exclusive advisory lock is acquired on your connection before any DDL runs. The lock key is derived from your connection ID, so two simultaneous migrations against the same connection are impossible. If the lock cannot be acquired, the operation waits briefly and retries before failing with a clear message.

### Step 5 — Drift Check
Immediately before execution, Schema Weaver compares the current live schema fingerprint against the stored snapshot from your last pull or push. If someone has modified the database since your last operation, the migration is aborted with a `SCHEMA_DRIFTED` error. You must run `sw pull` to re-sync before pushing. See [Drift Detection](/database/drift-detection).

### Step 6 — Structural DDL
Tables, columns, indexes, constraints, enums, and sequences are applied via Atlas, wrapped in a transaction. If any statement fails, the entire structural DDL block is rolled back.

Two special cases are handled outside the main transaction:
- **Enum `ADD VALUE` on PostgreSQL 13 and earlier:** PostgreSQL does not allow `ALTER TYPE ... ADD VALUE` inside a transaction on PG ≤ 13. This statement is extracted and run before the transaction opens. On PG 14+, it runs inside the transaction normally.
- **`CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY`:** PostgreSQL requires concurrent index builds to run outside a transaction. These are extracted and run after the transaction commits. If a concurrent build is interrupted, the rest of the migration is unaffected.

### Step 7 — Behavioral DDL
After structural changes are committed, Schema Weaver syncs behavioral objects: functions, views, triggers, and RLS policies. These are applied using idempotent patterns — `CREATE OR REPLACE` for functions and views, and `DROP + CREATE` for triggers and policies. Materialized views with a unique index are refreshed with `REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY` to avoid blocking reads.

### Step 8 — Snapshot and Audit
After all DDL is applied, Schema Weaver:
- Re-introspects the live schema and saves a new snapshot
- Generates reverse DDL (the SQL needed to undo this migration) and stores it for rollback
- Records the migration in history with a timestamp, the applying user, and a `parent_hash` linking this migration to the previous one in an audit chain

---

## Safe Mode — Complex Type Changes

Some `ALTER COLUMN TYPE` operations cannot be done with a plain `ALTER TABLE` because the existing data cannot be automatically cast to the new type. For example: changing a `text` column to `integer`, or changing `numeric` to `bigint` when precision would be lost.

`sw push --safe` detects these situations and applies a **multi-phase migration** that preserves all existing data.

```
cloop sw > sw push --safe
Analyzing migration for unsafe operations...
  ⚠ Column type change detected: orders.amount (numeric → bigint)
  Smart migration plan:
    Phase 1: Add shadow column
    Phase 2: Install sync trigger
    Phase 3: Backfill existing data (5,000 rows/batch)
    Phase 4: Validate constraints
    Phase 5: Atomic swap
    Phase 6: Recreate FK constraints
  Proceed? [y/n]
```

### The Six Phases

| Phase | What happens |
|---|---|
| **1 — Shadow column** | A new column with the target type is added alongside the existing one (`orders.amount_new bigint`) |
| **2 — Sync trigger** | A `BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE` trigger is installed so any new writes are immediately mirrored to the shadow column |
| **3 — Backfill** | Existing rows are updated in batches of 5,000 to convert the data, with a 50ms pause between batches to avoid locking pressure |
| **4 — Validate** | Constraints and nullability are verified on the shadow column before the swap proceeds |
| **5 — Swap** | In a single transaction: triggers are dropped, the original column is renamed away, and the shadow column takes its place |
| **6 — FK cleanup** | Any foreign keys that referenced the original column are recreated pointing to the new column |

This approach keeps the table available for reads and writes throughout — there are no long locks.

### When Safe Mode Is Blocked

Not every type change can be handled with safe mode. Schema Weaver blocks smart migration and reports the reason when:

| Situation | Why it's blocked |
|---|---|
| The column is a **primary key** | A PK type change requires restructuring the entire table's FK graph; this cannot be done safely column-by-column |
| The column is **referenced by a foreign key** in another table | The FK relationship must be resolved before the type can change |
| The column has a **UNIQUE constraint** | The shadow column approach cannot preserve uniqueness guarantees during backfill |

When blocked, Schema Weaver describes the dependency chain so you can resolve it manually before retrying.

### Impossible Casts

Some type changes have no valid cast expression at all. Schema Weaver detects these and throws an `UNSAFE_CAST` error before executing anything:

- `uuid` ↔ `integer`, `bigint`, `smallint`
- `text` / `varchar` → `integer`, `bigint` (lossy — text may not be numeric)
- `boolean` ↔ `integer`
- `jsonb` ↔ `integer`
- `bigint` → `integer`, `smallint`
- `integer` → `smallint`
- `numeric`, `real`, `double precision` → `integer`, `bigint`, `smallint`

For these casts, there is no automatic path — you need to backfill and convert the data manually before changing the column type.

---

## Force Mode — Destructive Changes

`sw push --force` bypasses the destructive change protection. It is required when your migration includes:

- `DROP TABLE`
- `DROP COLUMN`
- A type cast that cannot be performed automatically (when not using safe mode)

```
cloop sw > sw push --force
⚠ WARNING: Destructive changes detected:
  DROP TABLE public.old_payments
  DROP COLUMN users.legacy_field
Type "confirm" to proceed:
```

You must type `confirm` explicitly. There is no flag to skip the confirmation prompt.

> **Data loss warning:** Force mode drops data permanently. Schema Weaver does not make a backup before applying a force migration. Verify that the data being dropped is safe to remove before proceeding.

---

## Destructive Change Protection

The following operations are blocked by default and require `--force`:

| Operation | Protected |
|---|---|
| `DROP TABLE` | ✅ Yes |
| `DROP COLUMN` | ✅ Yes |
| `ALTER COLUMN TYPE` with an incompatible cast | ✅ Yes |
| `DROP INDEX` | No — indexes hold no row data |
| `DROP VIEW` | No |
| `DROP FUNCTION` | No |
| `DROP CONSTRAINT` | No |

---

## Concurrent Migration Protection

The advisory lock (Step 4 above) ensures that two migrations cannot run simultaneously against the same connection.

If you attempt to push while another migration is in progress:

```
⚠ Another migration is in progress for this connection. Try again in a moment.
```

The lock is released automatically when the migration completes or fails. If a migration was interrupted ungracefully (e.g. a server restart), the advisory lock is released when the PostgreSQL session that held it terminates.

---

## Migration Commands

| Command | What it does |
|---|---|
| `sw push` | Preview the migration — no changes applied |
| `sw push --apply` | Apply the migration to the live database |
| `sw push --safe` | Apply using multi-phase safe mode for complex type changes |
| `sw push --force` | Apply including destructive operations (confirmation required) |

---

## Next Steps

- [Drift Detection](/database/drift-detection) — what happens when the database changes between your push commands
- [History & Rollback](/database/history-rollback) — reverse a migration you've applied
- [Terminal & sw Commands](/database/terminal) — full command reference

frontmatter.title: Pull, Diff & Push
frontmatter.description: Sync your project SQL with your live PostgreSQL database — import the live schema with pull, compare differences with diff, and preview or apply changes with push

database/pull-diff-push.content:
Pull, Diff & Push¶

The core database workflow in Schema Weaver is three steps: pull the live schema into your project files, diff to see what has changed, and push to apply your changes back. These three operations keep your project SQL and your live PostgreSQL database in sync.

Quick links: Migrations · Drift Detection · Terminal & sw Commands

The Workflow¶

First time connecting to an existing database:
  sw pull → your SQL files now reflect the live schema

Making changes:
  Edit SQL in the editor
  sw diff  → see exactly what DDL will run
  sw push  → preview the migration summary
  sw push --apply → execute it against the live database

You can also start from scratch — write your schema from scratch in the editor and push it to an empty database. sw pull is only needed when the database already has a schema you want to import.

Pull — Import the Live Schema¶

sw pull introspects the connected database and writes its current schema to the linked SQL file in your project. It reads from two sources:

Structural objects (via Atlas DDL engine):

  • Tables — columns, data types, nullability, defaults, generated columns
  • Primary keys, foreign keys, unique constraints, check constraints
  • Indexes — including partial indexes and expression indexes
  • Sequences and identity columns
  • Enums and composite types
  • Extensions enabled in the database

Behavioral objects (via PostgreSQL catalog queries):

  • Views and materialized views
  • Functions and stored procedures
  • Triggers
  • Row-Level Security (RLS) policies

After introspection, the schema is written as canonical DDL into your linked SQL file. Schema Weaver also saves a snapshot of the current state — a fingerprint of the live schema that is used later for drift detection.

cloop sw > sw pull
Connecting to database (pull mode: single)...
✓ Introspected schema: 20 tables
  Snapshot saved (0f33295b…)
Schema pulled and written to file.

When to use pull:

  • First time connecting to an existing database with an existing schema
  • After someone made DDL changes directly on the database
  • After a database restore or import
  • To reset your project SQL to match the current live state

Heads up: Pull overwrites the linked SQL file with what is currently in the database. Any edits you made to that file that are not yet pushed to the database will be lost. Make sure you have pushed your changes first, or use sw diff to check what would be overwritten.

Diff — See What Would Change¶

sw diff compares your project SQL against the live database and shows the exact DDL statements that would be executed if you pushed. No changes are made — it is purely informational.

cloop sw > sw diff
--- Live database
+++ Project SQL

+ CREATE TABLE notifications (
+   id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
+   user_id uuid NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
+   message text NOT NULL,
+   read_at timestamptz
+ );
+ ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN last_login timestamptz;
+ CREATE INDEX idx_users_last_login ON users (last_login);

Lines prefixed with + exist in your project SQL but not in the database — they will be created on push. Lines prefixed with - exist in the database but not in your project SQL — they would be dropped (protected by destructive change detection).

When to use diff: Before any migration, especially in production. Use sw diff when you want the exact raw DDL. Use sw push (without --apply) for a plain-English summary.

Push — Preview the Migration¶

Running sw push without any flags generates a human-readable summary of what would change. Nothing is applied.

cloop sw > sw push
Diffing project SQL against live schema...
  2 tables to create
  1 column to add
  1 index to create

  Preview:
  CREATE TABLE notifications (...);
  ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN last_login timestamptz;
  CREATE INDEX idx_users_last_login ON users (last_login);

Run `sw push --apply` to execute, or `sw diff` for the raw DDL.

This is the recommended step before every migration. Review the summary, check that the changes are what you expect, and then run sw push --apply.

Push Apply — Execute the Migration¶

sw push --apply executes the migration. Every statement runs inside a transaction — if anything fails, the entire migration is rolled back and the database is left unchanged.

cloop sw > sw push --apply
Applying migration...
  ✓ CREATE TABLE notifications
  ✓ ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN last_login
  ✓ CREATE INDEX idx_users_last_login
Migration applied. Snapshot saved (a1b2c3d4…)

After a successful migration, Schema Weaver:

  • Saves a new snapshot of the live schema
  • Generates reverse DDL (for rollback)
  • Records the migration in history with a timestamp and the user who applied it
  • Saves the parent hash for audit chain integrity

For the full details of what happens during sw push --apply — advisory locking, behavioral DDL, enum handling, and more — see Migrations.

How Diff Works¶

Schema Weaver uses a two-track diff engine:

Track 1 — Structural diff (Atlas): Tables, columns, indexes, constraints, and sequences are diffed using Atlas, an open-source schema migration tool. Atlas compares the canonical DDL representations of your project SQL and the live database and generates the minimal set of ALTER TABLE, CREATE INDEX, and similar statements needed to bring them in sync.

Track 2 — Behavioral diff (custom): Functions, views, triggers, and RLS policies are diffed by Schema Weaver's own behavioral layer. These objects are not handled by Atlas — they are extracted from your project SQL and compared against what is currently deployed in pg_catalog, then applied using CREATE OR REPLACE (functions and views) and DROP + CREATE (triggers and policies).

The result is a complete, accurate diff of everything in your schema — not just tables.

Quick Reference¶

Command Effect
sw pull Import live schema → overwrite linked SQL file
sw diff Show raw DDL diff (no changes made)
sw push Preview migration summary (no changes made)
sw push --apply Execute the migration

Next Steps¶

  • Migrations — full migration pipeline, safe mode, force mode, and destructive change protection
  • Drift Detection — what happens when someone changes the database outside Schema Weaver
  • History & Rollback — view migration history and reverse a migration
  • Terminal & sw Commands — complete command reference
