# Pull, Diff & Push

The core database workflow in Schema Weaver is three steps: **pull** the live schema into your project files, **diff** to see what has changed, and **push** to apply your changes back. These three operations keep your project SQL and your live PostgreSQL database in sync.

> Quick links: [Migrations](/database/migrations) · [Drift Detection](/database/drift-detection) · [Terminal & sw Commands](/database/terminal)

---

## The Workflow

```
First time connecting to an existing database:
  sw pull → your SQL files now reflect the live schema

Making changes:
  Edit SQL in the editor
  sw diff  → see exactly what DDL will run
  sw push  → preview the migration summary
  sw push --apply → execute it against the live database
```

You can also start from scratch — write your schema from scratch in the editor and push it to an empty database. `sw pull` is only needed when the database already has a schema you want to import.

---

## Pull — Import the Live Schema

`sw pull` introspects the connected database and writes its current schema to the linked SQL file in your project. It reads from two sources:

**Structural objects** (via Atlas DDL engine):
- Tables — columns, data types, nullability, defaults, generated columns
- Primary keys, foreign keys, unique constraints, check constraints
- Indexes — including partial indexes and expression indexes
- Sequences and identity columns
- Enums and composite types
- Extensions enabled in the database

**Behavioral objects** (via PostgreSQL catalog queries):
- Views and materialized views
- Functions and stored procedures
- Triggers
- Row-Level Security (RLS) policies

After introspection, the schema is written as canonical DDL into your linked SQL file. Schema Weaver also saves a **snapshot** of the current state — a fingerprint of the live schema that is used later for drift detection.

```
cloop sw > sw pull
Connecting to database (pull mode: single)...
✓ Introspected schema: 20 tables
  Snapshot saved (0f33295b…)
Schema pulled and written to file.
```

**When to use pull:**
- First time connecting to an existing database with an existing schema
- After someone made DDL changes directly on the database
- After a database restore or import
- To reset your project SQL to match the current live state

> **Heads up:** Pull overwrites the linked SQL file with what is currently in the database. Any edits you made to that file that are not yet pushed to the database will be lost. Make sure you have pushed your changes first, or use `sw diff` to check what would be overwritten.

---

## Diff — See What Would Change

`sw diff` compares your project SQL against the live database and shows the exact DDL statements that would be executed if you pushed. No changes are made — it is purely informational.

```
cloop sw > sw diff
--- Live database
+++ Project SQL

+ CREATE TABLE notifications (
+   id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
+   user_id uuid NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
+   message text NOT NULL,
+   read_at timestamptz
+ );
+ ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN last_login timestamptz;
+ CREATE INDEX idx_users_last_login ON users (last_login);
```

Lines prefixed with `+` exist in your project SQL but not in the database — they will be created on push. Lines prefixed with `-` exist in the database but not in your project SQL — they would be dropped (protected by [destructive change detection](/database/migrations#destructive-change-protection)).

**When to use diff:** Before any migration, especially in production. Use `sw diff` when you want the exact raw DDL. Use `sw push` (without `--apply`) for a plain-English summary.

---

## Push — Preview the Migration

Running `sw push` without any flags generates a human-readable summary of what would change. Nothing is applied.

```
cloop sw > sw push
Diffing project SQL against live schema...
  2 tables to create
  1 column to add
  1 index to create

  Preview:
  CREATE TABLE notifications (...);
  ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN last_login timestamptz;
  CREATE INDEX idx_users_last_login ON users (last_login);

Run `sw push --apply` to execute, or `sw diff` for the raw DDL.
```

This is the recommended step before every migration. Review the summary, check that the changes are what you expect, and then run `sw push --apply`.

---

## Push Apply — Execute the Migration

`sw push --apply` executes the migration. Every statement runs inside a transaction — if anything fails, the entire migration is rolled back and the database is left unchanged.

```
cloop sw > sw push --apply
Applying migration...
  ✓ CREATE TABLE notifications
  ✓ ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN last_login
  ✓ CREATE INDEX idx_users_last_login
Migration applied. Snapshot saved (a1b2c3d4…)
```

After a successful migration, Schema Weaver:
- Saves a new snapshot of the live schema
- Generates reverse DDL (for rollback)
- Records the migration in history with a timestamp and the user who applied it
- Saves the parent hash for audit chain integrity

For the full details of what happens during `sw push --apply` — advisory locking, behavioral DDL, enum handling, and more — see [Migrations](/database/migrations).

---

## How Diff Works

Schema Weaver uses a **two-track diff engine**:

**Track 1 — Structural diff (Atlas):**
Tables, columns, indexes, constraints, and sequences are diffed using Atlas, an open-source schema migration tool. Atlas compares the canonical DDL representations of your project SQL and the live database and generates the minimal set of `ALTER TABLE`, `CREATE INDEX`, and similar statements needed to bring them in sync.

**Track 2 — Behavioral diff (custom):**
Functions, views, triggers, and RLS policies are diffed by Schema Weaver's own behavioral layer. These objects are not handled by Atlas — they are extracted from your project SQL and compared against what is currently deployed in `pg_catalog`, then applied using `CREATE OR REPLACE` (functions and views) and `DROP + CREATE` (triggers and policies).

The result is a complete, accurate diff of everything in your schema — not just tables.

---

## Quick Reference

| Command | Effect |
|---|---|
| `sw pull` | Import live schema → overwrite linked SQL file |
| `sw diff` | Show raw DDL diff (no changes made) |
| `sw push` | Preview migration summary (no changes made) |
| `sw push --apply` | Execute the migration |

---

## Next Steps

- [Migrations](/database/migrations) — full migration pipeline, safe mode, force mode, and destructive change protection
- [Drift Detection](/database/drift-detection) — what happens when someone changes the database outside Schema Weaver
- [History & Rollback](/database/history-rollback) — view migration history and reverse a migration
- [Terminal & sw Commands](/database/terminal) — complete command reference

frontmatter.title: Terminal & sw Commands
frontmatter.description: Run database operations directly from the built-in sw terminal in the SQL Editor — complete reference for all sw commands

database/terminal.content:
Terminal & sw Commands¶

The SQL Editor has a built-in terminal for running database operations directly without leaving the editor. Once a connection is active, open the terminal from the connection panel and run sw commands — a CLI for interacting with your connected PostgreSQL database.

Quick links: Pull, Diff & Push · Migrations · Drift Detection

Opening the Terminal¶

  1. Make sure a database connection is linked to the current project
  2. Click the terminal icon in the connection sidebar panel, or click the terminal button in the connection dashboard
  3. The terminal opens with your connection name and current file as context

The prompt format is:

<connection-name> sw >

Everything you type runs against that connection.

Command Reference¶

sw status¶

Checks whether the connection is reachable and reports latency.

cloop sw > sw status
Checking connection health...
Connected • Latency: 2698ms

Use it when: You want a quick sanity check before running a migration.

sw pull¶

Introspects the live database and writes the current schema to the linked SQL file. Saves a snapshot for future drift detection.

cloop sw > sw pull
Connecting to database (pull mode: single)...
✓ Introspected schema: 20 tables
  Snapshot saved (0f33295b…)
Schema pulled and written to file.

Use it when: You want to import the live schema into your project, or sync after external changes were made to the database.

See Pull, Diff & Push for full details.

sw push¶

Previews the migration — shows what would change without applying anything.

cloop sw > sw push
Diffing project SQL against live schema...
  2 tables to create
  1 column to add
  1 index to create

  Preview:
  CREATE TABLE notifications (...);
  ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN last_login timestamptz;
  CREATE INDEX idx_users_last_login ON users (last_login);

Run `sw push --apply` to execute, or `sw diff` for the raw DDL.

Use it when: You want to review the migration summary before committing.

sw push --apply¶

Applies the migration to the live database. Runs inside a transaction with an advisory lock. If any statement fails, the entire migration rolls back.

cloop sw > sw push --apply
Applying migration...
  ✓ CREATE TABLE notifications
  ✓ ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN last_login
  ✓ CREATE INDEX idx_users_last_login
Migration applied. Snapshot saved (a1b2c3d4…)

Use it when: You have reviewed the preview and are ready to push your changes.

See Migrations for the full 8-step pipeline.

sw push --safe¶

Applies the migration using smart migration mode — a multi-phase strategy for type changes that cannot be done with a single ALTER TABLE.

cloop sw > sw push --safe
Analyzing migration for unsafe operations...
  ⚠ Column type change detected: orders.amount (numeric → bigint)
  Smart migration plan:
    Phase 1: Add shadow column
    Phase 2: Install sync trigger
    Phase 3: Backfill existing data (5,000 rows/batch)
    Phase 4: Validate constraints
    Phase 5: Atomic swap
    Phase 6: Recreate FK constraints
  Proceed? [y/n]

Use it when: Your diff includes column type changes that require data to be preserved during a structural change.

See Migrations — Safe Mode for details on when this mode is blocked.

sw push --force¶

Bypasses destructive change protection and applies the migration including DROP TABLE, DROP COLUMN, or unsafe type cast operations.

cloop sw > sw push --force
⚠ WARNING: Destructive changes detected:
  DROP TABLE public.old_payments
  DROP COLUMN users.legacy_field
Type "confirm" to proceed:

Requires typing confirm explicitly. Use only after verifying the data can be safely removed.

sw diff¶

Shows the raw DDL difference between your project SQL and the live database — the exact SQL statements that would be executed by sw push --apply.

cloop sw > sw diff
--- Live database
+++ Project SQL

+ CREATE TABLE notifications (
+   id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
+   user_id uuid NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
+   message text NOT NULL,
+   read_at timestamptz
+ );
+ ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN last_login timestamptz;

Use it when: You want to see the exact DDL that will run, rather than the summary from sw push.

sw health¶

Runs a full connection health check — verifies credentials, checks permissions, and reports the PostgreSQL version and latency.

cloop sw > sw health
Connection:   ✓ reachable
Credentials:  ✓ valid
Permissions:  ✓ CREATE, ALTER, DROP granted
PostgreSQL:   16.3 (Ubuntu 16.3-1.pgdg22.04+1)
Latency:      175ms

If permissions are insufficient, the output lists exactly what is missing.

sw drift¶

Detects changes made directly to the live database since the last pull or push.

cloop sw > sw drift
Checking for external schema changes...
  ⚠ Drift detected (2 changes):
    + Column: users.temp_migration_flag (added directly)
    ~ Index: idx_orders_status (modified outside Schema Weaver)

Use it when: You suspect someone ran DDL directly on the database, or after a restore or import.

See Drift Detection for how to resolve drift.

sw history¶

Lists all migrations applied through Schema Weaver for this connection.

cloop sw > sw history
Migration History — cloop
────────────────────────────────────────────────
  #12   2026-04-01   success   alice@company.com
  #11   2026-03-20   success   you@company.com
  #10   2026-03-15   success   you@company.com
  #9    2026-03-01   rolled_back
  ...

Each entry shows the migration number, date, status, and who applied it.

sw rollback¶

Rolls back the last applied migration using the stored reverse SQL.

cloop sw > sw rollback
Rolling back migration #12...
  Executing reverse SQL...
  ✓ DROP TABLE notifications
  ✓ ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN last_login
  ✓ DROP INDEX idx_users_last_login
Rollback complete. Schema restored to migration #11.

To roll back multiple migrations: sw rollback 3 rolls back migrations #12, #11, and #10 in sequence.

Use it when: A migration caused a problem and you need to restore the previous schema state.

See History & Rollback for what rollback can and cannot undo.

Command Summary¶

Command What it does
sw status Quick connection check — reachable and latency
sw pull Import live schema → write to SQL file + save snapshot
sw push Preview the migration (no changes applied)
sw push --apply Apply the migration to the live database
sw push --safe Apply using smart migration for complex type changes

sw diff Show raw DDL diff between project SQL and live database
sw health Full health check — connectivity, credentials, permissions, version
sw drift Detect schema changes made outside Schema Weaver
sw history List all applied migrations with status and author
sw rollback Roll back the last migration
sw rollback N Roll back the last N migrations in sequence
database/terminal.raw:
---
title: Terminal & sw Commands
description: Run database operations directly from the built-in sw terminal in the SQL Editor — complete reference for all sw commands
tags: [database, terminal, sw, cli, commands, pull, push, diff, migration, health, drift, history, rollback]
order: 7
sidebar_group: "Database"
related:
  - title: Pull, Diff & Push
    path: /database/pull-diff-push
  - title: Migrations
    path: /database/migrations
  - title: Drift Detection
    path: /database/drift-detection
  - title: History & Rollback
    path: /database/history-rollback
---

# Terminal & sw Commands

The SQL Editor has a built-in terminal for running database operations directly without leaving the editor. Once a connection is active, open the terminal from the connection panel and run `sw` commands — a CLI for interacting with your connected PostgreSQL database.

> Quick links: [Pull, Diff & Push](/database/pull-diff-push) · [Migrations](/database/migrations) · [Drift Detection](/database/drift-detection)

![The built-in terminal showing sw status and sw pull commands with their output](/images/sql-editor/terminal-dashbaord.png)

---

## Opening the Terminal

1. Make sure a database connection is linked to the current project
2. Click the **terminal icon** in the connection sidebar panel, or click the terminal button in the connection dashboard
3. The terminal opens with your connection name and current file as context

The prompt format is:

```
<connection-name> sw >
```

Everything you type runs against that connection.

---

## Command Reference

### `sw status`

Checks whether the connection is reachable and reports latency.

```
cloop sw > sw status
Checking connection health...
Connected • Latency: 2698ms
```

**Use it when:** You want a quick sanity check before running a migration.

---

### `sw pull`

Introspects the live database and writes the current schema to the linked SQL file. Saves a snapshot for future drift detection.

```
cloop sw > sw pull
Connecting to database (pull mode: single)...
✓ Introspected schema: 20 tables
  Snapshot saved (0f33295b…)
Schema pulled and written to file.
```

**Use it when:** You want to import the live schema into your project, or sync after external changes were made to the database.

See [Pull, Diff & Push](/database/pull-diff-push) for full details.

---

### `sw push`

Previews the migration — shows what would change without applying anything.

```
cloop sw > sw push
Diffing project SQL against live schema...
  2 tables to create
  1 column to add
  1 index to create

  Preview:
  CREATE TABLE notifications (...);
  ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN last_login timestamptz;
  CREATE INDEX idx_users_last_login ON users (last_login);

Run `sw push --apply` to execute, or `sw diff` for the raw DDL.
```

**Use it when:** You want to review the migration summary before committing.

---

### `sw push --apply`

Applies the migration to the live database. Runs inside a transaction with an advisory lock. If any statement fails, the entire migration rolls back.

```
cloop sw > sw push --apply
Applying migration...
  ✓ CREATE TABLE notifications
  ✓ ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN last_login
  ✓ CREATE INDEX idx_users_last_login
Migration applied. Snapshot saved (a1b2c3d4…)
```

**Use it when:** You have reviewed the preview and are ready to push your changes.

See [Migrations](/database/migrations) for the full 8-step pipeline.

---

### `sw push --safe`

Applies the migration using smart migration mode — a multi-phase strategy for type changes that cannot be done with a single `ALTER TABLE`.

```
cloop sw > sw push --safe
Analyzing migration for unsafe operations...
  ⚠ Column type change detected: orders.amount (numeric → bigint)
  Smart migration plan:
    Phase 1: Add shadow column
    Phase 2: Install sync trigger
    Phase 3: Backfill existing data (5,000 rows/batch)
    Phase 4: Validate constraints
    Phase 5: Atomic swap
    Phase 6: Recreate FK constraints
  Proceed? [y/n]
```

**Use it when:** Your diff includes column type changes that require data to be preserved during a structural change.

See [Migrations — Safe Mode](/database/migrations#safe-mode--complex-type-changes) for details on when this mode is blocked.

---

### `sw push --force`

Bypasses destructive change protection and applies the migration including `DROP TABLE`, `DROP COLUMN`, or unsafe type cast operations.

```
cloop sw > sw push --force
⚠ WARNING: Destructive changes detected:
  DROP TABLE public.old_payments
  DROP COLUMN users.legacy_field
Type "confirm" to proceed:
```

Requires typing `confirm` explicitly. Use only after verifying the data can be safely removed.

---

### `sw diff`

Shows the raw DDL difference between your project SQL and the live database — the exact SQL statements that would be executed by `sw push --apply`.

```
cloop sw > sw diff
--- Live database
+++ Project SQL

+ CREATE TABLE notifications (
+   id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
+   user_id uuid NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
+   message text NOT NULL,
+   read_at timestamptz
+ );
+ ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN last_login timestamptz;
```

**Use it when:** You want to see the exact DDL that will run, rather than the summary from `sw push`.

---

### `sw health`

Runs a full connection health check — verifies credentials, checks permissions, and reports the PostgreSQL version and latency.

```
cloop sw > sw health
Connection:   ✓ reachable
Credentials:  ✓ valid
Permissions:  ✓ CREATE, ALTER, DROP granted
PostgreSQL:   16.3 (Ubuntu 16.3-1.pgdg22.04+1)
Latency:      175ms
```

If permissions are insufficient, the output lists exactly what is missing.

---

### `sw drift`

Detects changes made directly to the live database since the last pull or push.

```
cloop sw > sw drift
Checking for external schema changes...
  ⚠ Drift detected (2 changes):
    + Column: users.temp_migration_flag (added directly)
    ~ Index: idx_orders_status (modified outside Schema Weaver)
```

**Use it when:** You suspect someone ran DDL directly on the database, or after a restore or import.

See [Drift Detection](/database/drift-detection) for how to resolve drift.

---

### `sw history`

Lists all migrations applied through Schema Weaver for this connection.

```
cloop sw > sw history
Migration History — cloop
────────────────────────────────────────────────
  #12   2026-04-01   success   alice@company.com
  #11   2026-03-20   success   you@company.com
  #10   2026-03-15   success   you@company.com
  #9    2026-03-01   rolled_back
  ...
```

Each entry shows the migration number, date, status, and who applied it.

---

### `sw rollback`

Rolls back the last applied migration using the stored reverse SQL.

```
cloop sw > sw rollback
Rolling back migration #12...
  Executing reverse SQL...
  ✓ DROP TABLE notifications
  ✓ ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN last_login
  ✓ DROP INDEX idx_users_last_login
Rollback complete. Schema restored to migration #11.
```

To roll back multiple migrations: `sw rollback 3` rolls back migrations #12, #11, and #10 in sequence.

**Use it when:** A migration caused a problem and you need to restore the previous schema state.

See [History & Rollback](/database/history-rollback) for what rollback can and cannot undo.

---

## Command Summary

| Command | What it does |
|---|---|
| `sw status` | Quick connection check — reachable and latency |
| `sw pull` | Import live schema → write to SQL file + save snapshot |
| `sw push` | Preview the migration (no changes applied) |
| `sw push --apply` | Apply the migration to the live database |
| `sw push --safe` | Apply using smart migration for complex type changes |
| `sw push --force` | Apply despite destructive changes (confirm required) |
| `sw diff` | Show raw DDL diff between project SQL and live database |
| `sw health` | Full health check — connectivity, credentials, permissions, version |
| `sw drift` | Detect schema changes made outside Schema Weaver |
| `sw history` | List all applied migrations with status and author |
| `sw rollback` | Roll back the last migration |
| `sw rollback N` | Roll back the last N migrations in sequence |

frontmatter.title: AI Tools Reference
frontmatter.description: Complete reference for all 55 tools available to Resona AI in the SQL Editor — schema analysis, file management, database migrations, graph analysis, audits, and more

sql-editor/ai-assistant-tools.content:
AI Tools Reference¶

Resona AI has access to 55 purpose-built tools it can call autonomously when answering your questions. You never call these tools directly — Resona decides which ones to use based on your intent. This page is a complete reference so you know exactly what Resona is capable of.

Quick links: Resona AI Overview · Workspace Editing

How Tools Work¶

When you send a message, Resona's agentic loop plans which tools to call, executes them (up to 10 in parallel per iteration), reads the results, and uses them to form its answer. You see each tool call and its one-line summary in the chat stream.

Context injection — some values are automatically provided to tools without Resona needing to ask. These include your user_id, the active project_id, and the linked connection_id. You never have to re-specify your project or connection in every message.

Safety gates — two tools require your explicit "yes" before executing: db_apply_migration and db_rollback. Resona will always describe the operation and ask for confirmation first.

Preview mode — all file-editing tools support a preview_only flag. Resona will often preview changes and show you a diff before committing anything to your files.

Schema Discovery¶

These tools let Resona read and understand the live structure of a connected database.

Tool What it does
sql_list_tables Lists all tables in a connected database with row estimates, column counts, and index counts. Can be scoped to a specific schema.
sql_get_schema Returns the full schema definition for a specific table — columns, types, nullability, defaults, primary key, foreign keys, and indexes.
sql_get_column_info Returns statistics for a column — distinct count, null count, and sample values from the live database.

Schema Compiler¶

These tools give Resona access to the full output of the Schema Compiler — the same 20-layer analysis that grades your schema A–F. Results are cached for 5 minutes per session.

Tool What it does
sql_compile_schema Runs a full 20-layer compilation of your project SQL. Returns the overall grade, per-layer grades, every issue with severity and suggested SQL fix, and object counts (tables, views, functions, policies, etc.).
sql_get_issues Queries the compilation result for issues. Can be filtered by layer (e.g., "security", "normalization") or severity ("error", "warning", "info").
sql_get_table_report Returns a structural report for all tables — constraints, column stats, and quality flags.
sql_get_layer_report Returns the detailed report for a single compiler layer — layer grade, issues list, and score. Accepts any of the 20 layer names.

Graph & Relationship Analysis¶

These tools operate on the ER Diagram's compiled relationship graph — the same data that drives node and edge rendering on the canvas.

Tool What it does
sql_get_graph_summary Returns a topology summary — node count, edge count, connectivity stats, and isolated table count.
sql_get_connections Returns all relationship edges. Can be filtered by type: "foreign_key", "view_dependency", "trigger_target", "inferred".
sql_get_path Finds the shortest path between two tables. Returns the chain of tables and edges (same as Path Analysis in the diagram).
sql_get_domain_subgraph Extracts the subgraph for a specific schema namespace — all nodes and edges that belong to that schema.
sql_get_cascade_impact Impact analysis for dropping a table or column — returns all objects that would be affected by cascading deletes or drops.
sql_get_centrality Centrality scores (betweenness and degree) for all tables — identifies the most critical "hub" tables in your schema.

Dependency Analysis¶

Tool What it does
sql_analyze_dependencies Builds a dependency graph from your SQL files — shows which views depend on tables, which triggers reference columns, and which functions are used by other objects. Can be scoped to a specific table or column.
sql_check_column_impact Answers "what breaks if I change this column?" — returns all views, functions, triggers, and constraints that reference the specified column.

File & Workspace Management¶

These tools let Resona read, create, modify, move, and delete files in your project. All writes create a new immutable version — nothing is ever truly overwritten.

Tool What it does
workspace_list_files Lists the full project file tree with human-readable paths, node IDs, and types.
workspace_read_file Reads a file by node ID or filename (e.g., "schema.sql" or "migrations/001_init.sql"). Returns the content and version number.
workspace_search_files Grep-style search across all SQL files. Accepts a regex pattern and returns matching lines with file name, line number, and context.
workspace_create_file Creates a new file in the project. Accepts a path-style name (e.g., "migrations/002_add_index.sql") and content.
workspace_update_file Replaces the entire content of a file. Creates a new version in history.
workspace_create_folder Creates a new folder at any level of the project tree.
workspace_delete_node Soft-deletes a file or folder (moves to trash — recoverable).
workspace_move_node Moves a file or folder to a different parent location.
workspace_get_versions Lists up to 20 recent versions of a file — version number, timestamp, content preview, and size.
workspace_revert_to_version Reverts a file to a previous version. Creates a new version (the history is always preserved, nothing is destroyed).
workspace_patch_file Surgical edit — finds an exact text block in a file and replaces it. Supports preview_only mode. See Workspace Editing.
workspace_apply_diff Applies an ordered list of changes to a single file in one operation — each change specifies the exact text to find and what to replace it with. See Workspace Editing.
workspace_batch_edit Applies changes across multiple files in a single call — if any change fails, all previous changes in the same call are rolled back. See Workspace Editing.

Database & Migration¶

These tools connect Resona to your live PostgreSQL database for schema pull, diff, drift detection, and migration apply/rollback.

Tool What it does Safety gate
db_list_connections Lists all saved database connections — name, host, database, SSL mode, last used. —
db_test_connection Tests credentials without saving — returns success and PostgreSQL version. —
db_save_connection Saves a new connection (credentials stored encrypted at rest). —
db_get_project_connection Returns the connection currently linked to this project. —
db_link_project Links a saved connection to the active project. —
db_pull_schema Introspects the live database and returns full DDL — tables, indexes, constraints, enums, views, functions, triggers, policies. —
db_diff_schema Diffs the project SQL against the live database. Returns migration_sql, an isEmpty flag, a plain-English summary, and any destructive change warnings. —
db_detect_drift Detects whether the live database has changed externally since the last pull (compares fingerprints). —
db_preview_migration Dry-run of a migration — shows exactly what SQL would be applied without touching the database. —
db_apply_migration Applies migration to live database. Requires confirmed: true. Resona must ask you before setting this flag. ✅ Confirm required
db_rollback Rolls back N migrations. Requires confirmed: true. ✅ Confirm required
db_migration_history Lists past migrations — timestamp, SQL snippet, applied_by, status. —
db_migration_status Shows the current sync state — pending changes count, last applied migration. —
db_analyze_destructive Analyzes a migration SQL block for destructive operations (DROP TABLE, DROP COLUMN, ALTER TYPE, etc.) before applying. —

Terminal¶

Tool What it does
terminal_run_sw Mirrors the sw CLI commands: pull, push, diff, status, history, drift, health, rollback. push and rollback are preview-only through this tool — they do not auto-confirm.

Quality Audits¶

These tools run deep analysis passes on your schema beyond the standard compiler layers.

Tool What it does
sql_security_audit Full security audit — RLS coverage, over-privileged roles, SECURITY DEFINER functions, public schema exposure, extension risks, and an overall exposure score.
sql_performance_audit Performance audit — missing indexes on FK columns, table bloat estimates, inefficient query patterns, query plan analysis hints.
sql_naming_audit Naming convention audit — checks table names, column names, constraint names, and index names for consistency. Returns violations with suggested corrections.
sql_get_constraint_report Complete constraint report — PKs, FKs, CHECK constraints, and UNIQUE constraints with validation status.
sql_get_view_report Report on all views — dependencies, materialization status, potential circular references.
sql_get_function_report Report on all functions and procedures — parameter signatures, return types, security context (SECURITY DEFINER vs INVOKER).
sql_get_trigger_report Report on all triggers — timing (BEFORE/AFTER/INSTEAD OF), events (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/TRUNCATE), and referenced function.
sql_get_partition_report Report on partitioned tables — partition strategy (RANGE/LIST/HASH), partition statistics, and orphaned partition detection.

SQL Formatting & Linting¶

Tool What it does
sql_format Formats SQL for consistent style — uppercases keywords, normalizes indentation, aligns column definitions.
sql_lint Lints SQL for style violations and best-practice deviations — returns findings with line numbers and severity.
sql_detect_antipatterns Detects SQL anti-patterns: SELECT *, missing LIMIT, N+1 patterns, functions used on indexed columns, correlated subqueries, implicit casts, DISTINCT misuse. Returns findings with severity and recommended alternatives.

Migration Planning¶

Tool What it does
sql_plan_migration Generates a full migration plan from the current project schema to a provided target schema. Handles the diff, ordering, and destructive change detection.
sql_compare_schemas Side-by-side comparison of the project schema against an externally provided schema SQL. Returns the diff summary and detailed changes.

Tool Summary¶

Category Tool count
Schema Discovery 3
Schema Compiler 4
Graph & Relationship Analysis 6
Dependency Analysis 2
File & Workspace Management 13
Database & Migration 9
Terminal 1
Quality Audits 8
SQL Formatting & Linting 3
Migration Planning 2
Total 55

Next Steps¶

  • Resona AI Overview — how the agentic loop works, intent classification, memory, and streaming
  • Workspace Editing — deep dive into surgical file editing with workspace_patch_file, workspace_apply_diff, and workspace_batch_edit
  • Database — link a live database to unlock the migration and drift detection tools

| Tool | What it does | Safety gate |
|---|---|---|
| `db_list_connections` | Lists all saved database connections — name, host, database, SSL mode, last used. | — |
| `db_test_connection` | Tests credentials without saving — returns success and PostgreSQL version. | — |
| `db_save_connection` | Saves a new connection (credentials stored encrypted at rest). | — |

---

## Terminal

| Tool | What it does |
|---|---|
| `terminal_run_sw` | Mirrors the `sw` CLI commands: `pull`, `push`, `diff`, `status`, `history`, `drift`, `health`, `rollback`. `push` and `rollback` are preview-only through this tool — they do not auto-confirm. |

---

## Quality Audits

These tools run deep analysis passes on your schema beyond the standard compiler layers.

| Tool | What it does |
|---|---|
| `sql_security_audit` | Full security audit — RLS coverage, over-privileged roles, `SECURITY DEFINER` functions, public schema exposure, extension risks, and an overall exposure score. |
| `sql_performance_audit` | Performance audit — missing indexes on FK columns, table bloat estimates, inefficient query patterns, query plan analysis hints. |
| `sql_naming_audit` | Naming convention audit — checks table names, column names, constraint names, and index names for consistency. Returns violations with suggested corrections. |
| `sql_get_constraint_report` | Complete constraint report — PKs, FKs, CHECK constraints, and UNIQUE constraints with validation status. |
| `sql_get_view_report` | Report on all views — dependencies, materialization status, potential circular references. |

| `sql_get_partition_report` | Report on partitioned tables — partition strategy (RANGE/LIST/HASH), partition statistics, and orphaned partition detection. |

---

## SQL Formatting & Linting

| Tool | What it does |
|---|---|
| `sql_format` | Formats SQL for consistent style — uppercases keywords, normalizes indentation, aligns column definitions. |
| `sql_lint` | Lints SQL for style violations and best-practice deviations — returns findings with line numbers and severity. |
| `sql_detect_antipatterns` | Detects SQL anti-patterns: `SELECT *`, missing `LIMIT`, N+1 patterns, functions used on indexed columns, correlated subqueries, implicit casts, `DISTINCT` misuse. Returns findings with severity and recommended alternatives. |

---

## Migration Planning

| Tool | What it does |
|---|---|
| `sql_plan_migration` | Generates a full migration plan from the current project schema to a provided target schema. Handles the diff, ordering, and destructive change detection. |
| `sql_compare_schemas` | Side-by-side comparison of the project schema against an externally provided schema SQL. Returns the diff summary and detailed changes. |

---

## Tool Summary

| Category | Tool count |
|---|---|
| Schema Discovery | 3 |
| Schema Compiler | 4 |
| Graph & Relationship Analysis | 6 |
| Dependency Analysis | 2 |
| File & Workspace Management | 13 |
| Database & Migration | 9 |
| Terminal | 1 |
| Quality Audits | 8 |
| SQL Formatting & Linting | 3 |
| Migration Planning | 2 |
| **Total** | **55** |

---

## Next Steps

- [Resona AI Overview](/sql-editor/ai-assistant) — how the agentic loop works, intent classification, memory, and streaming
- [Workspace Editing](/sql-editor/ai-assistant-workspace) — deep dive into surgical file editing with `workspace_patch_file`, `workspace_apply_diff`, and `workspace_batch_edit`
- [Database](/database) — link a live database to unlock the migration and drift detection tools

frontmatter.title: Workspace Editing
frontmatter.description: Resona AI makes targeted, surgical edits to your SQL project files — single-block patches, multi-change file edits, and cross-file refactoring with full preview and rollback

sql-editor/ai-assistant-workspace.content:
Workspace Editing¶

When Resona AI edits your SQL files it does not regenerate entire files from scratch. It finds the exact block that needs to change and replaces only that — leaving everything else untouched. It can make multiple changes to a single file in one go, or apply edits across several files at once, with full rollback if anything goes wrong.

Quick links: Resona AI Overview · AI Tools Reference · Version History

Three Editing Tools¶

Resona has three editing tools, each suited to a different scope:

Tool Scope When Resona uses it
workspace_patch_file One change in one file Add a column, fix a constraint, insert an index
workspace_apply_diff Multiple changes in one file Add several indexes and update a view in the same file
workspace_batch_edit Changes across multiple files Rename a column that appears across 4 files

All three support preview mode — Resona can show you a diff before committing anything to your files.

workspace_patch_file — Single Block Edit¶

The most precise tool. Resona finds an exact text block in a file and replaces it. The rest of the file is untouched.

Example: You ask "Add a deleted_at timestamp to the users table."

Resona locates this block in auth.sql:

CREATE TABLE users (
  id         uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  email      text NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

And replaces it with:

CREATE TABLE users (
  id         uuid        PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  email      text        NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  deleted_at timestamptz
);

Only those five lines change. Everything else in auth.sql stays exactly as it was.

After applying, Resona reports the new version number and how many lines changed.

Preview before applying¶

Resona often runs a preview first — showing you the diff in the chat and asking for confirmation before writing. Only after you confirm (or Resona determines the change is safe) does the file update.

workspace_apply_diff — Multiple Changes in One File¶

When several changes need to happen in the same file, this tool applies all of them together. Resona provides an ordered list of find-and-replace pairs.

Example: You ask "Add indexes for all FK columns in billing.sql."

Resona identifies three missing indexes and applies all three to billing.sql in one write:

Change 1: Add index after the orders table definition
Change 2: Add FK index after order_items
Change 3: Add index for invoices.customer_id

All changes are validated before any are written. If one find-and-replace fails to match (e.g. the file was edited since the preview), none of the changes are applied — the file stays clean.

After applying: a single new file version is created containing all three changes.

workspace_batch_edit — Changes Across Multiple Files¶

The broadest editing tool — changes across several files in one call. Each entry in the batch specifies which file to update and what to change (either a full file replacement or a targeted find-and-replace).

Example: You ask "Rename user_id to owner_id across all files."

Resona searches all project files, finds four occurrences, and applies them together:

orders.sql       — column definition
order_items.sql  — view referencing the column
auth.sql         — FK definition in another table
policies.sql     — RLS USING clause

If any change fails, all previous changes in the same batch are rolled back — you never end up with a half-renamed schema.

Mixed operations¶

A batch can combine different operation types:

  • Some files get a targeted find-and-replace (e.g. schema files where one column changes)
  • Other files get a full replacement (e.g. a generated migration file being rebuilt)

If a Change Fails¶

Scenario What happens
workspace_patch_file — text not found Error reported, no changes made
workspace_apply_diff — one pair fails No changes applied to the file
workspace_batch_edit — one file fails All successfully-applied changes from that batch are rolled back

In all cases, your files are left in a consistent state — either fully updated or unchanged.

Preview Workflow¶

You can ask Resona to preview before applying anything:

You: Rename full_name to display_name across all files — show me first

Resona: Here are the proposed changes across 3 files:

  auth.sql (1 change):
  - full_name text,
  + display_name text,

  views.sql (2 changes):
  - users.full_name AS author_name
  + users.display_name AS author_name
  ...

  Apply these changes?

You: Yes

Resona: ✓ Applied. New versions created for 2 files.

Version History Integration¶

Every edit creates a new immutable version in Version History. This means:

  • Every AI-applied change is tracked with a timestamp
  • You can always revert to the state before any AI edit
  • The full audit trail is available for any file

If an edit isn't what you wanted, ask Resona to revert:

Revert auth.sql to the version before you renamed the column

Search Before Editing¶

Before any multi-file edit, Resona searches across your project files using workspace_search_files — a regex-capable search. This tells Resona exactly which files are affected before building its edit plan, so it doesn't guess.

Tips¶

  • Be specific about scope: "Add a deleted_at column to @orders only" gives a tighter result than "add soft delete"
  • Ask for preview on risky changes: "Rename this column — show me the diff first"
  • Use version history as your safety net: every pre-edit state is preserved

Next Steps¶

  • Resona AI Overview — the full agentic loop, intent classification, streaming, and memory
  • AI Tools Reference — complete catalog of all 55 tools
  • Version History — browse and restore every version created by AI edits

---

## If a Change Fails

Resona: Here are the proposed changes across 3 files:

sql-editor/ai-assistant.content:
Resona AI — SQL Editor¶

Resona AI is an agentic AI assistant built directly into the SQL Editor. It is not a simple chat interface that generates a code block for you to copy — it is a full agentic system with 55 purpose-built tools that can read your files, reason about your schema graph, run the compiler, analyze security and performance, write surgical multi-file edits, diff your live database, and apply migrations — all from a single conversation.

Quick links: Open SQL Editor · Tools Reference · Workspace Editing

Opening Resona AI¶

Resona AI is available in two places:

Left Sidebar Panel¶

Click Resona AI in the left sidebar of the SQL Editor to open the full-height chat panel. This is the primary AI interface — it stays open as you navigate files and shows the full conversation history.

ER Diagram Nodes¶

Resona AI is also embedded directly in the ER Diagram as floating chat nodes anchored to individual tables, schema groups, or the full canvas. See Resona AI in the ER Diagram for details.

How It Works — Agentic Loop¶

Unlike a simple prompt → response system, Resona AI uses a ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) agentic loop:

Your message
     ↓
Intent classification
(write SQL / debug / analyze / file action / migrate / …)
     ↓
Context assembly
(schema graph + compiler result + @ mentions + history)
     ↓
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  THINK: Stream LLM response                     │
  │      ↓                                          │
  │  DETECT tool calls in response                  │
  │      ↓                                          │
  │  EXECUTE tools (up to 10 in parallel)           │
  │      ↓                                          │
  │  OBSERVE results → feed back to LLM             │
  │      ↓                                          │
  │  LOOP (up to 10 iterations)                     │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
     ↓
Validate final SQL (if generated)
     ↓
Stream final answer + usage stats

You see this loop play out in real time: as Resona works, each tool call is shown with its name and a one-line result summary. You can follow along as it reads files, runs the compiler, checks indexes, and builds its answer — step by step.

Intent Classification¶

Before executing any tools, Resona classifies your message into one of ten intent categories:

Intent What triggers it Tools made available
write_query "Create a table…", "Add a column…" Schema tools, file tools, compiler
debug_query "Why is this FK failing?", "Fix this syntax" Compiler, dependency tools, debug tools
optimize_query "Improve performance", "Missing indexes?" Performance audit, graph tools, compiler
explain_query "What does this trigger do?", "Explain…" Schema tools, compiler, graph tools
refactor_query "Rename this column across files", "Clean up…" Workspace tools, batch edit, compiler
schema_question "How are users and orders connected?" Graph tools, dependency tools
plan_query "How should I model a…", "What's the best approach…" Compiler, graph tools, analysis tools
workspace_action "Create a file", "Show me the file tree" Workspace file tools
database_action "Pull live schema", "Apply migration", "Diff" Migration tools, terminal
conversational General questions, small talk Minimal toolset

Each intent activates a curated subset of tools — Resona doesn't get all 55 tools for every message, only the ones relevant to what you asked. This keeps responses focused and fast.

Context Awareness¶

Resona automatically receives rich context before every response:

Context What it includes
Full schema graph Every table, view, function, relationship — from the ER Diagram's compiled output
Compiler result The 20-layer compilation: grades, every issue with severity and suggested fix
Project file tree All files and folders in the active project
Conversation history Compressed and summarized history across sessions
@ mentions Specific tables, files, or folders you reference explicitly
Active file The currently open file and cursor context

You do not need to paste your schema into the prompt. Resona already knows it.

@ Mentions¶

Type @ to reference a specific table, file, or folder by name. An autocomplete dropdown filters your project's objects as you type:

What indexes are missing on @orders?
Add a foreign key on @order_items.product_id referencing @products.id
Read @migrations/001_init.sql and explain the changes

What Resona Can Do¶

Schema Generation¶

Task Example prompt
Create a table "Create a users table with email, hashed password, and created/updated timestamps"
Add columns "Add soft delete support to @orders with deleted_at and deleted_by"
Create an enum "Create an enum for payment methods: card, bank_transfer, crypto, invoice"
Generate RLS "Write RLS policies so users can only see their own rows in @documents"
Create functions "Write a trigger function that updates updated_at on every row change"
Write migrations "Write a migration to rename full_name to display_name across @users and @profiles"
Multi-table design "Design a RBAC system with roles, permissions, and a junction table"

Schema Analysis¶

Task Example prompt
Security audit "Run a security audit on my schema — show RLS coverage and privilege risks"
Performance audit "Find missing indexes and table bloat estimates"
Normalization check "Is my schema in 3NF? Flag any denormalization"
Naming audit "Check naming consistency across all tables and columns"
Dependency analysis "What breaks if I drop the @products.category_id column?"
Cascade impact "Show the cascade chain from @orders — what gets deleted when an order is deleted?"
Centrality analysis "Which tables are the most critical hubs in the schema?"

Database Operations¶

Task Example prompt
Pull live schema "Pull the current schema from the connected database"
Diff "Show me the diff between my project SQL and the live database"
Preview migration "Preview what SQL would be applied if I push now"
Apply migration "Apply the pending changes to the database"
Rollback "Roll back the last migration"
Migration history "Show me the last 10 migrations"

Safety gates: Apply migration and Rollback require your explicit confirmation before executing. Resona will always describe what it is about to do and ask "confirm?" before touching your live database.

File Operations¶

Task Example prompt
Read a file "Read @auth.sql and summarize what's in it"
Create a file "Create a new file called triggers.sql with the audit trigger we just discussed"
Edit a file (surgical) "Add the missing index to @orders.sql — just the index, nothing else"
Multi-file edit "Rename user_id to owner_id in @orders.sql, @order_items.sql, and @views.sql"
Revert a file "Revert @billing.sql to the version from two saves ago"
Search across files "Find all tables that reference the users table across all files"

Streaming — Watch It Think¶

Resona streams its reasoning in real time. As it works through your request, you see:

  • Tool calls: the name of each tool being executed and a one-line result summary
  • Thinking: the LLM's reasoning between tool calls
  • Final answer: the response streamed word by word

This transparency means you always know what Resona is doing — you can see it read a file, run the compiler, check a dependency, and then form its answer. If it takes an unexpected path, you can interrupt and redirect.

Memory — Remembers Across Sessions¶

Resona maintains a persistent memory of your schema conversations. This is a four-layer compression model:

Layer What it stores When it's used
Recent messages Last 20 messages verbatim Always in context
Schema changes DDL operations and migrations applied Recent history
Conversation summaries LLM-generated summaries of older message clusters Older sessions
Global facts Key facts extracted from the full conversation history Long-term memory

When your conversation history grows long, Resona automatically compresses older messages into summaries — so it never loses the thread of what you've been working on, even across sessions or days. The next time you open the SQL Editor, Resona remembers what you were building.

Applying AI-Generated SQL¶

When Resona generates SQL, you have three options:

Button Action
Apply to file Inserts (or replaces) the generated SQL into the currently active file
Apply to new file Creates a new file in the project and writes the SQL there
Copy Copies the SQL to clipboard

Preview Before Applying¶

Before any change is written to a file, a diff overlay shows exactly what will be added or removed — green lines are additions, red are removals. Click Accept to apply or Reject to discard.

Resona can also make surgical edits that target only the specific section of a file that needs changing, rather than replacing the whole file. See Workspace Editing for how this works.

Iterative Refinement¶

Resona maintains full conversation context across turns. You can refine your schema iteratively:

You:   Create a products table with name, price, and stock_qty

AI:    [generates CREATE TABLE products ...]

You:   Add a category_id FK referencing @categories

AI:    [adds column + FK constraint, updates the table]

You:   Now add a partial index for products where stock_qty = 0

AI:    [adds CREATE INDEX ... WHERE stock_qty = 0]

You:   Run the compiler and tell me what issues are left

AI:    [calls sql_compile_schema tool, reports 2 warnings]

You:   Fix both warnings

AI:    [patches the file to resolve both issues]

Each step builds on the last. Resona keeps the full schema state and conversation in context throughout.

Model Selection¶

Click the model selector in the AI panel header:

Model Best for
Claude Sonnet Default — fast, accurate schema generation, most tasks
Claude Opus Complex multi-table design, deep analysis, very large schemas

Usage and Credits¶

Each AI response consumes credits from your Schema Weaver plan. The footer of the AI panel shows current session usage.

Plan Daily AI credits
Free 50
Pro 500
Team 2,000 per seat

Credits reset at midnight UTC.

Keyboard Shortcuts¶

Shortcut Action
Ctrl+K / Cmd+K Open AI panel (with selected SQL as context if text is highlighted)
Enter Send message
Shift+Enter New line in the input
↑ Navigate to previous message in input history
Escape Close the AI panel

Next Steps¶

  • AI Tools Reference — complete catalog of all 55 AI tools with parameters and capabilities
  • Workspace Editing — surgical file edits, multi-file diffs, atomic batch operations
  • Resona AI in the ER Diagram — AI anchored to specific table nodes and schema groups
  • Schema Compiler — the 20-layer analysis engine Resona uses to assess your schema
  • Database — connect a live database so Resona can pull, diff, and migrate

| **@ mentions** | Specific tables, files, or folders you reference explicitly |
| **Active file** | The currently open file and cursor context |

You do not need to paste your schema into the prompt. Resona already knows it.

### @ Mentions

Type `@` to reference a specific table, file, or folder by name. An autocomplete dropdown filters your project's objects as you type:

```
What indexes are missing on @orders?
Add a foreign key on @order_items.product_id referencing @products.id
Read @migrations/001_init.sql and explain the changes
```

---

## What Resona Can Do

### Schema Generation

| Task | Example prompt |
|---|---|
| Create a table | "Create a users table with email, hashed password, and created/updated timestamps" |
| Add columns | "Add soft delete support to @orders with deleted_at and deleted_by" |
| Create an enum | "Create an enum for payment methods: card, bank_transfer, crypto, invoice" |
| Generate RLS | "Write RLS policies so users can only see their own rows in @documents" |
| Create functions | "Write a trigger function that updates updated_at on every row change" |
| Write migrations | "Write a migration to rename full_name to display_name across @users and @profiles" |
| Multi-table design | "Design a RBAC system with roles, permissions, and a junction table" |

### Schema Analysis

| Task | Example prompt |
|---|---|
| Security audit | "Run a security audit on my schema — show RLS coverage and privilege risks" |
| Performance audit | "Find missing indexes and table bloat estimates" |
| Normalization check | "Is my schema in 3NF? Flag any denormalization" |
| Naming audit | "Check naming consistency across all tables and columns" |
| Dependency analysis | "What breaks if I drop the @products.category_id column?" |
| Cascade impact | "Show the cascade chain from @orders — what gets deleted when an order is deleted?" |
| Centrality analysis | "Which tables are the most critical hubs in the schema?" |

### Database Operations

| Task | Example prompt |
|---|---|
| Pull live schema | "Pull the current schema from the connected database" |
| Diff | "Show me the diff between my project SQL and the live database" |
| Preview migration | "Preview what SQL would be applied if I push now" |
| Apply migration | "Apply the pending changes to the database" |
| Rollback | "Roll back the last migration" |
| Migration history | "Show me the last 10 migrations" |

> **Safety gates:** `Apply migration` and `Rollback` require your explicit confirmation before executing. Resona will always describe what it is about to do and ask "confirm?" before touching your live database.

### File Operations

| Task | Example prompt |
|---|---|
| Read a file | "Read @auth.sql and summarize what's in it" |
| Create a file | "Create a new file called triggers.sql with the audit trigger we just discussed" |
| Edit a file (surgical) | "Add the missing index to @orders.sql — just the index, nothing else" |
| Multi-file edit | "Rename user_id to owner_id in @orders.sql, @order_items.sql, and @views.sql" |
| Revert a file | "Revert @billing.sql to the version from two saves ago" |
| Search across files | "Find all tables that reference the users table across all files" |

---

## Streaming — Watch It Think

Resona streams its reasoning in real time. As it works through your request, you see:

- **Tool calls**: the name of each tool being executed and a one-line result summary
- **Thinking**: the LLM's reasoning between tool calls
- **Final answer**: the response streamed word by word

This transparency means you always know what Resona is doing — you can see it read a file, run the compiler, check a dependency, and then form its answer. If it takes an unexpected path, you can interrupt and redirect.

---

## Memory — Remembers Across Sessions

Resona maintains a persistent memory of your schema conversations. This is a **four-layer compression model**:

| Layer | What it stores | When it's used |
|---|---|---|
| **Recent messages** | Last 20 messages verbatim | Always in context |
| **Schema changes** | DDL operations and migrations applied | Recent history |
| **Conversation summaries** | LLM-generated summaries of older message clusters | Older sessions |
| **Global facts** | Key facts extracted from the full conversation history | Long-term memory |

When your conversation history grows long, Resona automatically compresses older messages into summaries — so it never loses the thread of what you've been working on, even across sessions or days. The next time you open the SQL Editor, Resona remembers what you were building.

---

## Applying AI-Generated SQL

When Resona generates SQL, you have three options:

| Button | Action |
|---|---|
| **Apply to file** | Inserts (or replaces) the generated SQL into the currently active file |
| **Apply to new file** | Creates a new file in the project and writes the SQL there |
| **Copy** | Copies the SQL to clipboard |

### Preview Before Applying

Before any change is written to a file, a **diff overlay** shows exactly what will be added or removed — green lines are additions, red are removals. Click **Accept** to apply or **Reject** to discard.

Resona can also make **surgical edits** that target only the specific section of a file that needs changing, rather than replacing the whole file. See [Workspace Editing](/sql-editor/ai-assistant-workspace) for how this works.

---

## Iterative Refinement

Resona maintains full conversation context across turns. You can refine your schema iteratively:

```
You:   Create a products table with name, price, and stock_qty

AI:    [generates CREATE TABLE products ...]

You:   Add a category_id FK referencing @categories

AI:    [adds column + FK constraint, updates the table]

You:   Now add a partial index for products where stock_qty = 0

AI:    [adds CREATE INDEX ... WHERE stock_qty = 0]

You:   Run the compiler and tell me what issues are left

AI:    [calls sql_compile_schema tool, reports 2 warnings]

You:   Fix both warnings

AI:    [patches the file to resolve both issues]
```

Each step builds on the last. Resona keeps the full schema state and conversation in context throughout.

---

## Model Selection

Click the model selector in the AI panel header:

| Model | Best for |
|---|---|
| **Claude Sonnet** | Default — fast, accurate schema generation, most tasks |
| **Claude Opus** | Complex multi-table design, deep analysis, very large schemas |

---

## Usage and Credits

Each AI response consumes credits from your Schema Weaver plan. The footer of the AI panel shows current session usage.

| Plan | Daily AI credits |
|---|---|
| Free | 50 |
| Pro | 500 |
| Team | 2,000 per seat |

Credits reset at midnight UTC.

---

## Keyboard Shortcuts

| Shortcut | Action |
|---|---|
| `Ctrl+K` / `Cmd+K` | Open AI panel (with selected SQL as context if text is highlighted) |
| `Enter` | Send message |
| `Shift+Enter` | New line in the input |
| `↑` | Navigate to previous message in input history |
| `Escape` | Close the AI panel |

---

## Next Steps

- [AI Tools Reference](/sql-editor/ai-assistant-tools) — complete catalog of all 55 AI tools with parameters and capabilities
- [Workspace Editing](/sql-editor/ai-assistant-workspace) — surgical file edits, multi-file diffs, atomic batch operations
- [Resona AI in the ER Diagram](/sql-editor/er-diagram-ai) — AI anchored to specific table nodes and schema groups
- [Schema Compiler](/sql-editor/schema-compiler) — the 20-layer analysis engine Resona uses to assess your schema
- [Database](/database) — connect a live database so Resona can pull, diff, and migrate

sql-editor/er-diagram-ai.content:
Resona AI in the ER Diagram¶

Resona AI is embedded directly into the ER Diagram at three levels: individual tables, schema groups, and the full schema. Each level opens a chat panel anchored by a dashed blue edge to its subject — you can ask questions, request SQL, and apply generated changes without leaving the diagram.

Quick links: Open SQL Editor · ER Diagram Overview · Resona AI Assistant

Three AI Entry Points¶

Mode How to open What it knows
Table AI Click the ✦ Sparkles icon on any table node The selected table: columns, types, constraints, indexes, triggers, policies
Group AI Click the ✦ AI icon on a schema group header (when grouping is enabled) All tables, views, and objects in that schema group
Global AI Click Resona AI in the toolbar The entire merged schema — all tables, views, functions, relationships across all files

Each mode opens a separate floating chat node on the canvas, connected to its subject via a dashed blue edge. You can have multiple AI nodes open simultaneously (one per table, one per group, and one global) and move them freely around the canvas.

Table AI¶

Table AI opens a chat panel anchored to a specific table. The AI has full context of:

  • All columns, types, defaults, and nullability
  • Primary key and foreign key definitions
  • All indexes on the table
  • All triggers (timing, events, function)
  • All RLS policies (command, roles, USING, WITH CHECK)
  • All check constraints
  • Inbound and outbound FK relationships (which tables reference this one, and which tables this one references)

Opening Table AI¶

Option A — From the table node:

  1. Hover over any table node
  2. Click the ✦ (Sparkles) icon that appears in the node header
  3. The AI chat panel spawns to the right of the table node, connected with a dashed blue line

Option B — Keyboard shortcut:

  1. Select a table node (click it)
  2. Press Ctrl+K / Cmd+K
  3. Table AI opens for the selected table

Suggested Prompts for Table AI¶

When Table AI opens, it shows quick-start prompts:

  • "Analyze structure" — reviews the table for common design issues (missing PKs, unbounded text columns, nullable FKs without indexes, etc.)
  • "Suggest indexes" — recommends indexes based on the FK columns, common query patterns, and current index coverage
  • "Check normalization" — evaluates whether the table is in 3NF, flags denormalization, and suggests improvements
  • "Generate seed data" — generates realistic INSERT statements for development/testing
  • "Write a migration" — generates the ALTER TABLE DDL to implement a described change safely

Closing Table AI¶

Click the × button on the AI chat node, or click the ✦ Sparkles icon on the same table again to toggle it off.

Group AI¶

Group AI opens when schema grouping is enabled and you click the AI icon on a group container header. The AI has context of every object inside that schema group — tables, views, functions, and their relationships.

Group AI is useful for:

  • Schema-level review — "Are there any design issues across the billing schema?"
  • Cross-table analysis — "How do orders, order_items, and products relate? Is this normalized correctly?"
  • Domain documentation — "Write a markdown summary of what the auth schema contains and how it works"
  • Refactoring suggestions — "Which tables in this schema are candidates for partitioning?"

Global AI¶

Global AI opens by clicking Resona AI in the toolbar. It is anchored in the top-left of the canvas and has context of the entire merged schema — every table, view, function, relationship, and constraint across all files in the project.

Global AI is best for:

  • Full-schema review — "What are the most critical issues in this schema?"
  • Cross-domain questions — "How does the auth schema connect to the billing schema?"
  • Schema generation — "Add a notifications table that can target both users and organizations"
  • Documentation — "Generate a README explaining this database schema"
  • Migration planning — "Plan a safe migration to add multi-tenancy to this schema"

Applying AI-Generated SQL¶

When Resona suggests SQL changes (new tables, ALTER TABLE, new indexes, etc.), you can review and apply them directly from the chat panel:

  1. The AI generates a SQL block with a Preview button
  2. Click Preview to see a diff of how the change would affect your current SQL files
  3. Click Apply to insert the generated SQL into your active file
  4. The ER Diagram updates immediately — new nodes and edges appear as soon as the SQL is parsed

AI-generated SQL is appended to your current file with a -- AI Generated: comment marker so you can find and review it easily.

AI Chat Context¶

Each AI node maintains its own conversation history. You can:

  • Continue the conversation — ask follow-up questions that build on the previous exchange
  • Start a new chat — click New Chat in the panel to reset the conversation while keeping the panel open
  • View history — scroll up in the panel to see earlier messages in the session

The AI automatically receives the latest version of your schema each time you send a message — if you edit SQL between messages, the AI sees the updated schema.

Working with Multiple AI Nodes¶

You can have multiple AI nodes open simultaneously:

  • One Global AI node
  • One AI node per table (open as many tables as you want)
  • One AI node per schema group

Each node is independent — conversations don't cross-contaminate. This lets you, for example, ask Global AI about the overall architecture while simultaneously asking Table AI on orders about a specific index strategy.

Move any AI node by dragging it. Nodes stay connected to their subject via the dashed blue edge regardless of where you drag them.

Keyboard Shortcuts¶

Shortcut Action
Ctrl+K / Cmd+K Open Table AI for the currently selected table node
Escape Close the most recently opened AI node

Next Steps¶

  • ER Diagram Overview — full feature overview including navigation, export, and layout
  • Resona AI Assistant — the full SQL Editor AI assistant (same AI, different surface)
  • Path Analysis — find the shortest FK join path, then ask AI to generate the JOIN SQL
  • Grouping & Schema Clusters — enable schema groups to unlock Group AI

# Resona AI in the ER Diagram

Resona AI is embedded directly into the ER Diagram at three levels: individual tables, schema groups, and the full schema. Each level opens a chat panel anchored by a dashed blue edge to its subject — you can ask questions, request SQL, and apply generated changes without leaving the diagram.

> Quick links: [Open SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [ER Diagram Overview](/sql-editor/er-diagram) · [Resona AI Assistant](/sql-editor/ai-assistant)

---

## Three AI Entry Points

| Mode | How to open | What it knows |
|---|---|---|
| **Table AI** | Click the ✦ Sparkles icon on any table node | The selected table: columns, types, constraints, indexes, triggers, policies |
| **Group AI** | Click the ✦ AI icon on a schema group header (when grouping is enabled) | All tables, views, and objects in that schema group |
| **Global AI** | Click **Resona AI** in the toolbar | The entire merged schema — all tables, views, functions, relationships across all files |

Each mode opens a separate floating chat node on the canvas, connected to its subject via a dashed blue edge. You can have multiple AI nodes open simultaneously (one per table, one per group, and one global) and move them freely around the canvas.

![Resona AI global chat node open on the ER Diagram canvas, connected to the diagram with a dashed blue edge](/images/sql-editor/resona-ai-global.png)

---

## Table AI

![Resona AI table chat node anchored to a table node in the ER Diagram with a dashed blue connection edge](/images/sql-editor/resona-ai-table.png)

**Table AI** opens a chat panel anchored to a specific table. The AI has full context of:

- All columns, types, defaults, and nullability
- Primary key and foreign key definitions
- All indexes on the table
- All triggers (timing, events, function)
- All RLS policies (command, roles, USING, WITH CHECK)
- All check constraints
- Inbound and outbound FK relationships (which tables reference this one, and which tables this one references)

### Opening Table AI

**Option A — From the table node:**
1. Hover over any table node
2. Click the ✦ (Sparkles) icon that appears in the node header
3. The AI chat panel spawns to the right of the table node, connected with a dashed blue line

**Option B — Keyboard shortcut:**
1. Select a table node (click it)
2. Press `Ctrl+K` / `Cmd+K`
3. Table AI opens for the selected table

### Suggested Prompts for Table AI

When Table AI opens, it shows quick-start prompts:

- **"Analyze structure"** — reviews the table for common design issues (missing PKs, unbounded text columns, nullable FKs without indexes, etc.)
- **"Suggest indexes"** — recommends indexes based on the FK columns, common query patterns, and current index coverage
- **"Check normalization"** — evaluates whether the table is in 3NF, flags denormalization, and suggests improvements
- **"Generate seed data"** — generates realistic `INSERT` statements for development/testing
- **"Write a migration"** — generates the `ALTER TABLE` DDL to implement a described change safely

### Closing Table AI

Click the × button on the AI chat node, or click the ✦ Sparkles icon on the same table again to toggle it off.

---

## Group AI

![Resona AI group chat node anchored to a schema group container in the ER Diagram](/images/sql-editor/resona-ai-gropu-table.png)

**Group AI** opens when schema grouping is enabled and you click the AI icon on a group container header. The AI has context of every object inside that schema group — tables, views, functions, and their relationships.

Group AI is useful for:

- **Schema-level review** — "Are there any design issues across the billing schema?"
- **Cross-table analysis** — "How do orders, order_items, and products relate? Is this normalized correctly?"
- **Domain documentation** — "Write a markdown summary of what the auth schema contains and how it works"
- **Refactoring suggestions** — "Which tables in this schema are candidates for partitioning?"

---

## Global AI

**Global AI** opens by clicking **Resona AI** in the toolbar. It is anchored in the top-left of the canvas and has context of the entire merged schema — every table, view, function, relationship, and constraint across all files in the project.

Global AI is best for:

- **Full-schema review** — "What are the most critical issues in this schema?"
- **Cross-domain questions** — "How does the auth schema connect to the billing schema?"
- **Schema generation** — "Add a notifications table that can target both users and organizations"
- **Documentation** — "Generate a README explaining this database schema"
- **Migration planning** — "Plan a safe migration to add multi-tenancy to this schema"

---

## Applying AI-Generated SQL

When Resona suggests SQL changes (new tables, `ALTER TABLE`, new indexes, etc.), you can review and apply them directly from the chat panel:

1. The AI generates a SQL block with a **Preview** button
2. Click **Preview** to see a diff of how the change would affect your current SQL files
3. Click **Apply** to insert the generated SQL into your active file
4. The ER Diagram updates immediately — new nodes and edges appear as soon as the SQL is parsed

AI-generated SQL is appended to your current file with a `-- AI Generated:` comment marker so you can find and review it easily.

---

## AI Chat Context

Each AI node maintains its own conversation history. You can:

- **Continue the conversation** — ask follow-up questions that build on the previous exchange
- **Start a new chat** — click **New Chat** in the panel to reset the conversation while keeping the panel open
- **View history** — scroll up in the panel to see earlier messages in the session

The AI automatically receives the latest version of your schema each time you send a message — if you edit SQL between messages, the AI sees the updated schema.

---

## Working with Multiple AI Nodes

You can have multiple AI nodes open simultaneously:

- One Global AI node
- One AI node per table (open as many tables as you want)
- One AI node per schema group

Each node is independent — conversations don't cross-contaminate. This lets you, for example, ask Global AI about the overall architecture while simultaneously asking Table AI on `orders` about a specific index strategy.

Move any AI node by dragging it. Nodes stay connected to their subject via the dashed blue edge regardless of where you drag them.

---

## Keyboard Shortcuts

| Shortcut | Action |
|---|---|
| `Ctrl+K` / `Cmd+K` | Open Table AI for the currently selected table node |
| `Escape` | Close the most recently opened AI node |

---

## Next Steps

- [ER Diagram Overview](/sql-editor/er-diagram) — full feature overview including navigation, export, and layout

sql-editor/er-diagram-grouping.content:
Grouping & Schema Clusters¶

When Cluster by Schema is enabled, the ER Diagram groups all nodes by their PostgreSQL schema namespace into visually distinct containers. Tables, views, functions, and other objects that belong to auth, billing, public, inventory — or any other schema in your project — are enclosed in their own labeled group. This makes it immediately clear which domain each object belongs to and eliminates the visual noise of a flat diagram with hundreds of nodes.

Quick links: Open SQL Editor · ER Diagram Overview

Enabling Grouping¶

  1. In the diagram footer, click the Group button
  2. Select Cluster by Schema
  3. The diagram re-runs ELK layout in two-level hierarchical mode — groups are positioned first, then their contents are laid out inside each group

To disable grouping, click Group → Cluster by Schema again. The diagram returns to a flat layout.

Group Containers¶

Each schema group is rendered as a resizable container with a colored header:

Header¶

Element Description
Schema name The PostgreSQL schema name (e.g. auth, billing, public)
Color bar Each schema gets a consistent color derived from its name — the same color is used everywhere that schema appears in the UI
Tables badge Count of table nodes inside the group
Views badge Count of view/materialized view nodes inside the group
Relations badge Count of foreign-key edges whose both endpoints are inside the group

Resizing Groups¶

Drag the resize handle at the corner or edge of a group container to make it larger or smaller. This is useful when a group has many tables and the auto-layout makes it too compact to read comfortably.

Click Auto-fit (available via right-click on the group header) to automatically resize the container to snugly fit all its children.

Moving Groups¶

Drag the group header to reposition the entire group — all nodes inside it move together. When groups are repositioned, the cross-group FK edges update automatically to connect through the new positions.

Locking Groups¶

Once you've arranged your group containers in a useful layout, enable Lock Groups to prevent accidental repositioning:

  1. Click Group in the footer
  2. Select Lock Groups

With groups locked:

  • Group containers cannot be dragged
  • Individual nodes inside a group can still be dragged within their container
  • Re-running auto-layout will reposition nodes inside groups but respect group positions

Cross-Schema Relationships¶

Foreign keys that cross schema boundaries (e.g. billing.orders.user_id → auth.users.id) are drawn as edges that exit one group container and enter another. These cross-group edges are particularly useful for understanding which schemas are tightly coupled.

The edge passes through the group borders with routing that avoids overlapping node labels.

Schema Color Coding¶

Each schema gets a persistent color that is used consistently:

  • In the group container header background
  • On the schema badge shown on table node headers when a table's schema is not public
  • In the Visibility panel schema group list

The color is derived deterministically from the schema name, so auth always gets the same color regardless of project.

Two-Level ELK Layout¶

When grouping is enabled, the ELK layout engine runs a two-level hierarchical algorithm:

  1. Intra-group layout: nodes within each schema are laid out relative to each other, respecting FK directions
  2. Inter-group layout: groups are positioned on the canvas to minimize cross-group edge crossings

This two-level approach means that within-schema FK chains are readable (parent tables above child tables), while across-schema dependencies flow in the overall chosen direction (LR, TB, etc.).

Note: Two-level hierarchical layout has an 8-second timeout. For very large schemas (hundreds of tables across many schemas), the layout may fall back to a flat arrangement. Use Visibility Controls to hide non-essential object types and reduce node count.

Tips for Working with Groups¶

  • Name your schemas intentionally — the group header shows the raw PostgreSQL schema name, so billing, auth, inventory produce cleaner diagrams than schema1, schema2
  • Use locking after initial layout — run auto-layout once, fine-tune group positions, then lock to prevent accidents
  • Use the Visibility panel to declutter — if a schema group has many auxiliary objects (sequences, policies, roles), hide those node types to reduce group density
  • Cross-group edges reveal coupling — a schema group with many cross-group edges is tightly coupled to other schemas, which is useful to know before refactoring
  • Resize before exporting — auto-fit each group before exporting as PNG/SVG to ensure nothing is clipped

Next Steps¶

  • ER Diagram Overview — full feature overview including navigation, layout, and export
  • Visibility & Node Types — control which schemas and object types are shown inside groups
  • Resona AI in the ER Diagram — open AI chat anchored to a specific schema group for domain-level analysis
  • Path Analysis — trace the shortest FK path across schema boundaries
