# Grouping & Schema Clusters

When **Cluster by Schema** is enabled, the ER Diagram groups all nodes by their PostgreSQL schema namespace into visually distinct containers. Tables, views, functions, and other objects that belong to `auth`, `billing`, `public`, `inventory` — or any other schema in your project — are enclosed in their own labeled group. This makes it immediately clear which domain each object belongs to and eliminates the visual noise of a flat diagram with hundreds of nodes.

> Quick links: [Open SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [ER Diagram Overview](/sql-editor/er-diagram)

![The ER Diagram in grouping mode showing tables clustered into colored schema containers for auth, billing, inventory, and public schemas](/images/sql-editor/er-diagram-grouping.png)

---

## Enabling Grouping

1. In the diagram footer, click the **Group** button
2. Select **Cluster by Schema**
3. The diagram re-runs ELK layout in two-level hierarchical mode — groups are positioned first, then their contents are laid out inside each group

To disable grouping, click **Group → Cluster by Schema** again. The diagram returns to a flat layout.

---

## Group Containers

Each schema group is rendered as a resizable container with a colored header:

### Header

| Element | Description |
|---|---|
| **Schema name** | The PostgreSQL schema name (e.g. `auth`, `billing`, `public`) |
| **Color bar** | Each schema gets a consistent color derived from its name — the same color is used everywhere that schema appears in the UI |
| **Tables badge** | Count of table nodes inside the group |
| **Views badge** | Count of view/materialized view nodes inside the group |
| **Relations badge** | Count of foreign-key edges whose both endpoints are inside the group |

### Resizing Groups

Drag the resize handle at the corner or edge of a group container to make it larger or smaller. This is useful when a group has many tables and the auto-layout makes it too compact to read comfortably.

Click **Auto-fit** (available via right-click on the group header) to automatically resize the container to snugly fit all its children.

### Moving Groups

Drag the group header to reposition the entire group — all nodes inside it move together. When groups are repositioned, the cross-group FK edges update automatically to connect through the new positions.

---

## Locking Groups

Once you've arranged your group containers in a useful layout, enable **Lock Groups** to prevent accidental repositioning:

1. Click **Group** in the footer
2. Select **Lock Groups**

With groups locked:
- Group containers cannot be dragged
- Individual nodes inside a group can still be dragged within their container
- Re-running auto-layout will reposition nodes inside groups but respect group positions

---

## Cross-Schema Relationships

Foreign keys that cross schema boundaries (e.g. `billing.orders.user_id` → `auth.users.id`) are drawn as edges that exit one group container and enter another. These cross-group edges are particularly useful for understanding which schemas are tightly coupled.

The edge passes through the group borders with routing that avoids overlapping node labels.

---

## Schema Color Coding

Each schema gets a persistent color that is used consistently:
- In the **group container** header background
- On the **schema badge** shown on table node headers when a table's schema is not `public`
- In the **Visibility panel** schema group list

The color is derived deterministically from the schema name, so `auth` always gets the same color regardless of project.

---

## Two-Level ELK Layout

When grouping is enabled, the ELK layout engine runs a **two-level hierarchical algorithm**:

1. **Intra-group layout**: nodes within each schema are laid out relative to each other, respecting FK directions
2. **Inter-group layout**: groups are positioned on the canvas to minimize cross-group edge crossings

This two-level approach means that within-schema FK chains are readable (parent tables above child tables), while across-schema dependencies flow in the overall chosen direction (LR, TB, etc.).

> **Note:** Two-level hierarchical layout has an 8-second timeout. For very large schemas (hundreds of tables across many schemas), the layout may fall back to a flat arrangement. Use [Visibility Controls](/sql-editor/er-diagram-visibility) to hide non-essential object types and reduce node count.

---

## Tips for Working with Groups

- **Name your schemas intentionally** — the group header shows the raw PostgreSQL schema name, so `billing`, `auth`, `inventory` produce cleaner diagrams than `schema1`, `schema2`
- **Use locking after initial layout** — run auto-layout once, fine-tune group positions, then lock to prevent accidents
- **Use the Visibility panel to declutter** — if a schema group has many auxiliary objects (sequences, policies, roles), hide those node types to reduce group density
- **Cross-group edges reveal coupling** — a schema group with many cross-group edges is tightly coupled to other schemas, which is useful to know before refactoring
- **Resize before exporting** — auto-fit each group before exporting as PNG/SVG to ensure nothing is clipped

---

## Next Steps

- [ER Diagram Overview](/sql-editor/er-diagram) — full feature overview including navigation, layout, and export
- [Visibility & Node Types](/sql-editor/er-diagram-visibility) — control which schemas and object types are shown inside groups
- [Resona AI in the ER Diagram](/sql-editor/er-diagram-ai) — open AI chat anchored to a specific schema group for domain-level analysis
- [Path Analysis](/sql-editor/er-diagram-path-analysis) — trace the shortest FK path across schema boundaries

frontmatter.title: Path Analysis
frontmatter.description: Find the shortest foreign-key join path between any two tables in your schema — with BFS and Dijkstra algorithms, visual highlighting, and confidence filtering

sql-editor/er-diagram-path-analysis.content:
Path Analysis¶

Path Analysis finds the shortest foreign-key relationship chain between any two tables in your schema. Instead of manually tracing JOIN paths through a dense diagram, you select a start table and an end table — the diagram highlights every intermediate table and edge you would need to traverse to connect them.

Quick links: Open SQL Editor · ER Diagram Overview

When to Use Path Analysis¶

  • Writing multi-table JOINs — when you know the start and end tables but need to figure out which intermediate tables to join through
  • Understanding a legacy schema — quickly visualize how two distant parts of a schema are connected
  • Reviewing migrations — check whether a new table is connected to the rest of the graph as expected
  • Onboarding — help new team members understand how the data model hangs together

Activating Path Analysis¶

  1. Click the Path button in the diagram toolbar (footprints icon)
    • The toolbar button turns highlighted to indicate path mode is active
    • A draggable Path Analysis overlay appears on the canvas
  2. Click any table node to set it as the start table
  3. Click a second table node to set it as the end table
  4. The path is calculated and highlighted immediately

Reading the Result¶

When a path is found, the diagram:

  • Highlights every node in the path with a distinct color
  • Highlights every edge in the path
  • Dims all nodes and edges that are not part of the path
  • Shows the full step-by-step path in the overlay panel

Each step in the overlay shows:

Field Description
Source The table at the start of this hop
Target The table at the end of this hop
Relationship The foreign key or relationship connecting them
Direction Whether the FK points from source → target or target → source

Path Algorithms¶

The overlay lets you choose between two algorithms:

BFS — Breadth-First Search¶

Best for most cases. BFS finds the path with the fewest hops — it treats all FK relationships as equal regardless of confidence, and returns the shortest chain by number of intermediate tables.

Use BFS when:

  • You want the simplest possible JOIN path
  • All relationships in your schema are explicit FKs (not inferred)
  • You need a fast result on a large schema

Dijkstra — Weighted Shortest Path¶

Best for inferred or mixed-confidence schemas. Dijkstra treats each relationship edge as having a weight based on confidence. Higher-confidence relationships (explicit FKs) are preferred over lower-confidence inferred relationships.

Use Dijkstra when:

  • Your schema contains inferred relationships (e.g. matching column names without explicit FKs)
  • You want the most reliable path, not just the shortest by hop count
  • You're working with a schema that was imported or reverse-engineered

Filter Options¶

Click the settings gear icon in the Path Analysis overlay to open filter controls:

Filter Description
Exclude inferred relationships Skips edges of type VIEW_DEPENDENCY, TRIGGER_TARGET, and INFERRED — only uses explicit FK edges
Exclude low-confidence edges Skips any relationship with confidence below 0.8

These filters affect both the path calculation and the diagram highlighting. Using both filters together gives you a path built entirely from explicit, high-confidence FK relationships.

Path Cost¶

When using the Dijkstra algorithm, the overlay shows a path cost — the cumulative weight of all edges in the path. Lower cost means higher overall confidence. This is useful for comparing alternative paths between the same two tables.

No Path Found¶

If no FK chain connects the two selected tables, the overlay shows "No path found." This can mean:

  • The tables are in completely separate parts of the schema (no FK chain at all)
  • The path exists only through inferred/low-confidence relationships, and those are currently filtered out — try disabling the filters
  • One or both tables are views or non-table objects that don't participate in FK relationships

Resetting Path Analysis¶

Action How
Clear selection Click Reset in the overlay, or click the same table again
Exit path mode Click the Path button in the toolbar again — the overlay closes and the diagram returns to normal
Change start or end Click a different table while in path mode — the selection updates automatically

Next Steps¶

  • ER Diagram Overview — full feature overview including navigation, layout, and export
  • Grouping & Schema Clusters — cluster your schema by PostgreSQL namespace
  • Resona AI in the ER Diagram — ask AI to explain the join path or generate the JOIN SQL
  • Visibility & Node Types — hide irrelevant object types before running path analysis on a large schema

# Path Analysis

Path Analysis finds the **shortest foreign-key relationship chain** between any two tables in your schema. Instead of manually tracing JOIN paths through a dense diagram, you select a start table and an end table — the diagram highlights every intermediate table and edge you would need to traverse to connect them.

> Quick links: [Open SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [ER Diagram Overview](/sql-editor/er-diagram)

![Path Analysis active in the ER Diagram showing the highlighted shortest path between two selected tables](/images/sql-editor/path-analysis.png)

---

## When to Use Path Analysis

- **Writing multi-table JOINs** — when you know the start and end tables but need to figure out which intermediate tables to join through
- **Understanding a legacy schema** — quickly visualize how two distant parts of a schema are connected
- **Reviewing migrations** — check whether a new table is connected to the rest of the graph as expected
- **Onboarding** — help new team members understand how the data model hangs together

---

## Activating Path Analysis

1. Click the **Path** button in the diagram toolbar (footprints icon)
   - The toolbar button turns highlighted to indicate path mode is active
   - A draggable **Path Analysis overlay** appears on the canvas
2. Click any table node to set it as the **start table**
3. Click a second table node to set it as the **end table**
4. The path is calculated and highlighted immediately

![The Path Analysis overlay showing the algorithm selector, start and end table fields, and filter options](/images/sql-editor/path-analysis-algorithm-selecting.png)

---

## Reading the Result

When a path is found, the diagram:

- **Highlights** every node in the path with a distinct color
- **Highlights** every edge in the path
- **Dims** all nodes and edges that are not part of the path
- Shows the full **step-by-step path** in the overlay panel

Each step in the overlay shows:

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| **Source** | The table at the start of this hop |
| **Target** | The table at the end of this hop |
| **Relationship** | The foreign key or relationship connecting them |
| **Direction** | Whether the FK points from source → target or target → source |

![The ER Diagram with the path highlighted — tables and edges along the shortest path are emphasized, everything else is dimmed](/images/sql-editor/path-analysis-selecting-path.png)

---

## Path Algorithms

The overlay lets you choose between two algorithms:

### BFS — Breadth-First Search

**Best for most cases.** BFS finds the path with the **fewest hops** — it treats all FK relationships as equal regardless of confidence, and returns the shortest chain by number of intermediate tables.

Use BFS when:
- You want the simplest possible JOIN path
- All relationships in your schema are explicit FKs (not inferred)
- You need a fast result on a large schema

### Dijkstra — Weighted Shortest Path

**Best for inferred or mixed-confidence schemas.** Dijkstra treats each relationship edge as having a **weight based on confidence**. Higher-confidence relationships (explicit FKs) are preferred over lower-confidence inferred relationships.

Use Dijkstra when:
- Your schema contains inferred relationships (e.g. matching column names without explicit FKs)
- You want the most reliable path, not just the shortest by hop count
- You're working with a schema that was imported or reverse-engineered

---

## Filter Options

Click the settings gear icon in the Path Analysis overlay to open filter controls:

| Filter | Description |
|---|---|
| **Exclude inferred relationships** | Skips edges of type VIEW_DEPENDENCY, TRIGGER_TARGET, and INFERRED — only uses explicit FK edges |
| **Exclude low-confidence edges** | Skips any relationship with confidence below 0.8 |

These filters affect both the path calculation and the diagram highlighting. Using both filters together gives you a path built entirely from explicit, high-confidence FK relationships.

---

## Path Cost

When using the Dijkstra algorithm, the overlay shows a **path cost** — the cumulative weight of all edges in the path. Lower cost means higher overall confidence. This is useful for comparing alternative paths between the same two tables.

---

## No Path Found

If no FK chain connects the two selected tables, the overlay shows "No path found." This can mean:

- The tables are in completely separate parts of the schema (no FK chain at all)
- The path exists only through inferred/low-confidence relationships, and those are currently filtered out — try disabling the filters
- One or both tables are views or non-table objects that don't participate in FK relationships

---

## Resetting Path Analysis

| Action | How |
|---|---|
| **Clear selection** | Click **Reset** in the overlay, or click the same table again |
| **Exit path mode** | Click the **Path** button in the toolbar again — the overlay closes and the diagram returns to normal |
| **Change start or end** | Click a different table while in path mode — the selection updates automatically |

---

## Next Steps

- [ER Diagram Overview](/sql-editor/er-diagram) — full feature overview including navigation, layout, and export
- [Grouping & Schema Clusters](/sql-editor/er-diagram-grouping) — cluster your schema by PostgreSQL namespace
- [Resona AI in the ER Diagram](/sql-editor/er-diagram-ai) — ask AI to explain the join path or generate the JOIN SQL
- [Visibility & Node Types](/sql-editor/er-diagram-visibility) — hide irrelevant object types before running path analysis on a large schema

frontmatter.title: Visibility & Node Types
frontmatter.description: Control exactly which PostgreSQL object types and which schema namespaces are shown in the ER Diagram — declutter large schemas and focus on what matters

sql-editor/er-diagram-visibility.content:
Visibility & Node Types¶

The ER Diagram supports every PostgreSQL object type — tables, views, materialized views, functions, enums, domains, roles, sequences, extensions, and RLS policies. On a real production schema this can mean dozens or hundreds of extra nodes beyond your core tables. The Visibility controls let you show only what you need, making large diagrams readable and layout faster.

Quick links: Open SQL Editor · ER Diagram Overview

Opening Visibility Controls¶

Click Visibility in the diagram footer (bottom-right area). A dropdown opens with two sections: Node Types and Schema Groups.

Changes apply immediately — nodes that are toggled off disappear from the canvas and the layout reflows. Toggle them back on to restore them.

Node Types¶

The Node Types section lets you show or hide each category of PostgreSQL object:

Node Type Default What is hidden when off
Views ✅ On Regular CREATE VIEW nodes and the edges representing view dependencies
Materialized Views ✅ On CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW nodes
Functions ✅ On CREATE FUNCTION and CREATE PROCEDURE nodes
Enums ✅ On CREATE TYPE … AS ENUM nodes
Domains ✅ On CREATE DOMAIN nodes
Roles ✅ On CREATE ROLE nodes
Sequences ✅ On CREATE SEQUENCE nodes
Extensions ✅ On CREATE EXTENSION nodes
Policies ✅ On CREATE POLICY (RLS policy) nodes

All types are on by default. For most schema review workflows, you'll want to keep Views and Enums on (they often have FK-like relationships with tables) and consider turning off Roles, Sequences, Extensions, and Policies to reduce clutter.

Recommended Visibility Profiles¶

For a clean table-focused view:

  • Turn off: Functions, Roles, Sequences, Extensions, Policies
  • Keep on: Views, Materialized Views, Enums, Domains

For security review:

  • Turn off: Functions, Sequences, Extensions
  • Keep on: Tables, Views, Policies, Roles

For maximum density (large schemas):

  • Turn off: Functions, Roles, Sequences, Extensions, Policies, Domains
  • Use TITLE view mode on table nodes
  • Use schema grouping to cluster by namespace

For migration review:

  • Keep everything on
  • Use Focus mode on the changed table instead of hiding types

Schema Groups¶

The Schema Groups section lists every PostgreSQL schema namespace present in your project. Each schema has its own toggle:

State Effect
✅ Checked All objects in that schema are visible
☐ Unchecked All objects in that schema are hidden — tables, views, functions, enums, everything

Common Use Cases¶

Hide system schemas: If your schema includes pg_catalog or information_schema objects, hide them here to keep the diagram focused on your application schema.

Focus on one domain: In a multi-schema project with auth, billing, inventory, and public, you can hide auth and inventory to focus the diagram on billing only.

Temporary hide during layout: Hiding a large schema temporarily can speed up ELK layout if you're working on a different part of the diagram.

Active Filter Badge¶

When any schema is hidden, an amber badge appears in the diagram footer showing how many schemas are currently filtered:

● 2 schemas hidden    ×

Click the × on this badge to instantly unhide all schemas and restore the full diagram.

Column View Mode¶

In addition to showing/hiding entire node types, you can control how much detail is shown inside each table node. Switch between view modes using the selector in the diagram footer:

Mode What columns show Best for
ALL Every column with name, type, constraints, collapsible index/trigger/policy sections Detailed review, documentation
KEYS Only primary key and foreign key columns Understanding relationships at a glance
TITLE Table name only — no columns shown Very large schemas, high-level overviews

View mode applies globally to all table, view, and materialized view nodes at once.

Visibility and Layout¶

Every time you toggle a node type or schema, the visible node count changes and ELK re-runs layout for the remaining nodes. This means:

  • Turning things off makes the diagram denser around the remaining nodes — run Fit to recenter
  • Turning things back on re-adds nodes; their positions are computed from scratch for the new layout unless you have manually dragged them

Tip: Set your visibility profile first, then run auto-layout (Layout → Re-run auto-layout) once to get a clean arrangement. After that, manually drag any nodes you want to reposition and lock groups if you're using schema grouping.

Visibility and Path Analysis¶

Path Analysis respects the current visibility settings. If a schema or node type is hidden, tables in that hidden set are excluded from path calculation. This means:

  • Hiding a schema will make paths that pass through that schema return "No path found"
  • Hiding object types like Views removes view dependency edges from path calculation
  • Use Exclude inferred relationships in the path analysis settings instead of hiding schemas if you want to keep all tables visible but ignore certain edge types

Next Steps¶

  • ER Diagram Overview — full feature overview including navigation, export, and all node type details
  • Grouping & Schema Clusters — cluster visible nodes by schema namespace
  • Path Analysis — find shortest join paths through visible tables
  • Resona AI in the ER Diagram — AI analysis scoped to visible tables and schemas

# Visibility & Node Types

The ER Diagram supports every PostgreSQL object type — tables, views, materialized views, functions, enums, domains, roles, sequences, extensions, and RLS policies. On a real production schema this can mean dozens or hundreds of extra nodes beyond your core tables. The **Visibility** controls let you show only what you need, making large diagrams readable and layout faster.

> Quick links: [Open SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [ER Diagram Overview](/sql-editor/er-diagram)

![The Visibility dropdown panel showing Node Types and Schema Groups toggles for the ER Diagram](/images/sql-editor/er-diagram-visibilty.png)

---

## Opening Visibility Controls

Click **Visibility** in the diagram footer (bottom-right area). A dropdown opens with two sections: **Node Types** and **Schema Groups**.

Changes apply immediately — nodes that are toggled off disappear from the canvas and the layout reflows. Toggle them back on to restore them.

---

## Node Types

The **Node Types** section lets you show or hide each category of PostgreSQL object:

| Node Type | Default | What is hidden when off |
|---|---|---|
| **Views** | ✅ On | Regular `CREATE VIEW` nodes and the edges representing view dependencies |
| **Materialized Views** | ✅ On | `CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW` nodes |
| **Functions** | ✅ On | `CREATE FUNCTION` and `CREATE PROCEDURE` nodes |
| **Enums** | ✅ On | `CREATE TYPE … AS ENUM` nodes |
| **Domains** | ✅ On | `CREATE DOMAIN` nodes |
| **Roles** | ✅ On | `CREATE ROLE` nodes |
| **Sequences** | ✅ On | `CREATE SEQUENCE` nodes |
| **Extensions** | ✅ On | `CREATE EXTENSION` nodes |
| **Policies** | ✅ On | `CREATE POLICY` (RLS policy) nodes |

All types are on by default. For most schema review workflows, you'll want to keep **Views** and **Enums** on (they often have FK-like relationships with tables) and consider turning off **Roles**, **Sequences**, **Extensions**, and **Policies** to reduce clutter.

### Recommended Visibility Profiles

**For a clean table-focused view:**
- Turn off: Functions, Roles, Sequences, Extensions, Policies
- Keep on: Views, Materialized Views, Enums, Domains

**For security review:**
- Turn off: Functions, Sequences, Extensions
- Keep on: Tables, Views, Policies, Roles

**For maximum density (large schemas):**
- Turn off: Functions, Roles, Sequences, Extensions, Policies, Domains
- Use **TITLE** view mode on table nodes
- Use [schema grouping](/sql-editor/er-diagram-grouping) to cluster by namespace

**For migration review:**
- Keep everything on
- Use [Focus mode](/sql-editor/er-diagram#focus-mode-subgraph) on the changed table instead of hiding types

---

## Schema Groups

The **Schema Groups** section lists every PostgreSQL schema namespace present in your project. Each schema has its own toggle:

| State | Effect |
|---|---|
| ✅ Checked | All objects in that schema are visible |
| ☐ Unchecked | All objects in that schema are hidden — tables, views, functions, enums, everything |

### Common Use Cases

**Hide system schemas**: If your schema includes `pg_catalog` or `information_schema` objects, hide them here to keep the diagram focused on your application schema.

**Focus on one domain**: In a multi-schema project with `auth`, `billing`, `inventory`, and `public`, you can hide `auth` and `inventory` to focus the diagram on `billing` only.

**Temporary hide during layout**: Hiding a large schema temporarily can speed up ELK layout if you're working on a different part of the diagram.

### Active Filter Badge

When any schema is hidden, an amber badge appears in the diagram footer showing how many schemas are currently filtered:

```
● 2 schemas hidden    ×
```

Click the × on this badge to instantly unhide all schemas and restore the full diagram.

---

## Column View Mode

In addition to showing/hiding entire node types, you can control how much detail is shown inside each table node. Switch between view modes using the selector in the diagram footer:

| Mode | What columns show | Best for |
|---|---|---|
| **ALL** | Every column with name, type, constraints, collapsible index/trigger/policy sections | Detailed review, documentation |
| **KEYS** | Only primary key and foreign key columns | Understanding relationships at a glance |
| **TITLE** | Table name only — no columns shown | Very large schemas, high-level overviews |

View mode applies globally to all table, view, and materialized view nodes at once.

---

## Visibility and Layout

Every time you toggle a node type or schema, the visible node count changes and ELK re-runs layout for the remaining nodes. This means:

- **Turning things off** makes the diagram denser around the remaining nodes — run **Fit** to recenter
- **Turning things back on** re-adds nodes; their positions are computed from scratch for the new layout unless you have manually dragged them

> **Tip:** Set your visibility profile first, then run auto-layout (`Layout → Re-run auto-layout`) once to get a clean arrangement. After that, manually drag any nodes you want to reposition and lock groups if you're using [schema grouping](/sql-editor/er-diagram-grouping).

---

## Visibility and Path Analysis

[Path Analysis](/sql-editor/er-diagram-path-analysis) respects the current visibility settings. If a schema or node type is hidden, tables in that hidden set are excluded from path calculation. This means:

- Hiding a schema will make paths that pass through that schema return "No path found"
- Hiding object types like Views removes view dependency edges from path calculation
- Use **Exclude inferred relationships** in the path analysis settings instead of hiding schemas if you want to keep all tables visible but ignore certain edge types

---

## Next Steps

- [ER Diagram Overview](/sql-editor/er-diagram) — full feature overview including navigation, export, and all node type details
- [Grouping & Schema Clusters](/sql-editor/er-diagram-grouping) — cluster visible nodes by schema namespace
- [Path Analysis](/sql-editor/er-diagram-path-analysis) — find shortest join paths through visible tables
- [Resona AI in the ER Diagram](/sql-editor/er-diagram-ai) — AI analysis scoped to visible tables and schemas

frontmatter.title: ER Diagram
frontmatter.description: Visualize your entire PostgreSQL schema as a live, interactive entity-relationship diagram — all object types supported, updates in real time as you write SQL

related.title: Resona AI in the ER Diagram

sql-editor/er-diagram.content:
ER Diagram¶

The ER Diagram is a live, interactive visualization of your entire PostgreSQL schema. Every object — tables, views, materialized views, functions, enums, domains, sequences, roles, extensions, and RLS policies — renders as a node on a pannable, zoomable canvas. Foreign-key relationships appear as directed edges. The diagram updates in real time as you type SQL with no manual refresh required, and scales to schemas with thousands of tables.

Quick links: Open SQL Editor · Path Analysis · Grouping · AI Features

Opening the Diagram¶

The ER Diagram is built into the SQL Editor — there is no separate page to navigate to.

  • In standard mode: Click Graph in the footer status bar — the diagram opens as a full-screen overlay
  • In fullscreen / split mode: The diagram opens as a resizable panel alongside the editor, updating as you type
  • Keyboard shortcut: Ctrl+G / Cmd+G toggles the diagram panel

How the Diagram is Built¶

The diagram is generated entirely in the browser from the same compiled schema that feeds the Schema Compiler. No server round-trip is needed.

All project SQL files
        ↓
  Schema Weaver Custom SQL Parser
  (runs entirely in your browser — no server round-trip)
        ↓
  Schema Compiler (20 layers)
        ↓
  Relationship Graph
  (foreign keys, view deps, trigger targets, inferred)
        ↓
  ELK Auto-Layout Engine
        ↓
  React Flow Canvas
  (re-renders on every SQL change)

Custom parser: Schema Weaver uses its own purpose-built PostgreSQL SQL parser that runs entirely in the browser. This is not a thin wrapper around a generic parser — it is a custom multi-phase parser designed specifically for DDL (schema definitions), handling PostgreSQL-specific syntax for partitions, RLS, identity columns, generated columns, domain types, and more. Because everything runs client-side, there is zero latency between typing SQL and seeing the diagram update.

Because the pipeline is entirely client-side, the diagram is always in sync with your code — add a CREATE TABLE, a new node appears immediately; add a REFERENCES clause, a new edge appears immediately.

Supported PostgreSQL Objects¶

The ER Diagram renders every major PostgreSQL object type as a distinct node:

Node Type Visual What it shows
Table White/gray card All columns with types, constraints, PKs, FKs, indexes, triggers, policies, check constraints
View Dashed border card Column count, source query context, Eye icon
Materialized View Dashed border with refresh icon Column count, REFRESH CONCURRENTLY command tooltip
Function / Procedure Code card Return type, PROC/FUNC badge, argument types
Enum Type card All enum values displayed as colored badges
Domain Type card Base type, NOT NULL constraint, CHECK expression
Sequence Sequence card Start value, increment, min/max bounds
Role Role card Attributes as badges: SUPERUSER, LOGIN, CREATEDB, CREATEROLE, BYPASSRLS, NOINHERIT
Extension Extension card Version, schema, Blocks icon
Policy (RLS) Policy card Command, roles, USING clause, WITH CHECK expression
Schema Group Colored container Groups all objects belonging to a PostgreSQL schema namespace

Every object type can be shown or hidden independently — see Visibility Controls.

Table Nodes in Detail¶

Tables are the most information-dense nodes in the diagram. Each table card shows:

Column Rows¶

Every column row displays the column name, data type, and a set of constraint icons:

Icon Color Meaning
🔑 Key Gold/yellow Primary key column
🔗 Link Indigo Foreign key column (with edge drawn to referenced table)
✦ Sparkles Emerald Unique constraint
≡ List Amber Indexed column
⚡ Zap Indigo Generated column (GENERATED ALWAYS)

Column rows also show nullability (NOT NULL marked with red asterisk) and whether the type is an enum (ENUM badge).

Collapsible Sections¶

Expand any table node to reveal:

  • Indexes — index name, type (B-tree, GiST, GIN, etc.), uniqueness, and column coverage
  • Triggers — trigger name, timing (BEFORE/AFTER), events (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/TRUNCATE), and function name
  • Policies — RLS policy name, command, applicable roles, USING and WITH CHECK expressions
  • Check Constraints — constraint name and the CHECK expression

View Modes¶

You can switch between three column display modes from the footer:

Mode What's shown
ALL Every column with full metadata, all collapsible sections
KEYS Only primary key and foreign key columns — compact overview
TITLE Table name only — maximum density for very large schemas

Cascade Risk Indicator¶

If a table has a chain of ON DELETE CASCADE foreign keys that could affect many rows, the table node shows an amber/red glow border with a ⚡ badge and the count of tables in the cascade chain.

Editing Tables from the Diagram¶

You can edit any table's structure directly from the ER Diagram without switching to the SQL editor. Click the edit icon (pencil) on any table node header to open the Table Edit Panel. It supports two modes, switchable with a toggle:

Visual Editor¶

The visual mode lets you add, remove, or modify columns using a form interface — no SQL required. Each column row has fields for name, type, nullability, default value, and constraint toggles (PRIMARY KEY, UNIQUE, NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY).

Control What it does
Add column Appends a new empty column row to the form
Delete column Removes the column (shows a confirmation for columns with FK dependencies)
Type selector Dropdown of all PostgreSQL types including your project's custom enums and domains
Constraint toggles Toggle PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL, UNIQUE per column
FK target Drop-down of all tables and columns in the project

When you click Save, the edited table definition is serialized back to SQL and written into the active file. The ER Diagram re-renders immediately from the updated SQL.

SQL Editor Mode¶

Switch to the SQL tab in the edit panel to view and edit the raw CREATE TABLE DDL directly. This mode gives you full control — useful for complex constraints, partial indexes on the table, or multi-column primary keys.

Changes you make in SQL mode are validated before saving. If the SQL is invalid, the panel shows the parse error and prevents saving until it is fixed.

Bidirectional sync: Visual mode and SQL mode show the same table. Switching between modes converts the representation in real time — edits made in visual mode are immediately visible in SQL mode and vice versa.

Relationship Edges¶

Each foreign key is drawn as a directed edge from the referencing column to the referenced table:

Edge style Meaning
Solid arrow Standard foreign key
Dashed arrow Nullable FK (optional relationship)
Orange edge View dependency (view references another object)
Red edge Trigger target relationship
Low opacity Low-confidence inferred relationship (confidence < 0.8)

Click any edge to see a side panel with the FK column names, ON DELETE rule (CASCADE, SET NULL, RESTRICT, NO ACTION), and deferrability.

Navigation¶

Action How
Pan Click and drag the canvas background
Zoom in / out Scroll wheel, or use + / − buttons in the toolbar
Fit all nodes Click Fit in the toolbar — centers and scales to show every node
Select a table Click any node — highlights it and dims unrelated nodes
Move a node Drag a node to reposition — positions are saved per session
Reset layout Click Layout → Re-run auto-layout to restore ELK positions
Search Type in the search box in the toolbar to filter visible nodes by name

Search¶

The toolbar search box filters the diagram in real time (300 ms debounce). Nodes whose names match the query stay visible; all others are dimmed. Matching is case-insensitive and partial.

Auto-Layout¶

The diagram uses ELK (Eclipse Layout Kernel) to position nodes automatically. ELK respects FK relationships and arranges parent tables above (or to the left of) their dependent tables.

Layout Direction¶

Switch layout direction from the toolbar dropdown:

Direction Description
Left → Right (LR) Parent tables on the left, child tables flow rightward — good for wide schemas
Top → Bottom (TB) Parent tables at the top, dependencies flow downward — good for deep hierarchies
Right → Left (RL) Mirrored LR
Bottom → Top (BT) Mirrored TB

Position Persistence¶

After ELK places nodes, you can drag any node to fine-tune its position. Positions are stored per schema (via Zustand) and restored when you return to the diagram. Click Reset layout to discard manual positions and re-run ELK from scratch.

Performance & Scale¶

The ELK engine has a built-in timeout system:

  • Hierarchical layout (with schema groups): 8-second timeout, then falls back to flat layout
  • Flat layout: 15-second timeout

For schemas with thousands of tables, use KEYS or TITLE view mode and Visibility Controls to hide object types you don't need — this significantly reduces node count and speeds up layout.

Toolbar Controls¶

Control Description
Search box Filter visible nodes by name (real-time, debounced)
Layout ▾ Switch layout direction (LR / TB / RL / BT)
Connect DB Open the database connection panel
Export ▾ Export diagram or schema (see below)
Path Toggle path analysis mode — find shortest FK path between two tables
Resona AI Open the global AI chat panel anchored to the diagram
Fullscreen Toggle fullscreen mode

Footer Controls¶

Control Description
Table / view / relationship counts Live stats of what's currently visible
Schema filter badge Shown in amber when any schemas are hidden — click to clear
View mode Switch between ALL / KEYS / TITLE column display
Group ▾ Toggle "Cluster by Schema" and "Lock Groups"
Visibility ▾ Toggle individual node types and schema groups
Terminal Open the SQL terminal panel (linked to a live database connection)

Export¶

Click Export in the toolbar to save the diagram or schema in multiple formats:

Image Exports¶

Format Description
PNG High-resolution raster image (2× pixel ratio) — good for Notion, docs, Slack
SVG Scalable vector image — good for presentations and print

Schema Code Exports¶

The diagram can also generate framework-ready code from your schema:

Format Output file Use case
Prisma Schema schema.prisma Drop into a Prisma project
Drizzle ORM schema.ts Drop into a Drizzle project
DBML schema.dbml Import into dbdiagram.io
TypeScript Types types.ts Type-safe interfaces matching your schema
Markdown Docs SCHEMA.md Human-readable schema documentation

All exports download as files with auto-generated names based on your project.

Focus Mode (Subgraph)¶

Click the Network icon on any table node to enter focus mode. The diagram zooms in on that table and its direct relationships, hiding the rest. You can control:

  • Depth — how many FK hops to follow from the root (1 = direct neighbors, 2 = two hops, up to 5)
  • Direction — outbound (tables this table references), inbound (tables that reference this table), or both
  • Confidence threshold — exclude low-confidence inferred relationships

A banner at the top of the diagram shows which table is focused. Click × to restore the full diagram.

Real-Time Updates¶

The diagram re-renders automatically every time the SQL parser produces a new result:

SQL change Diagram change
CREATE TABLE New node appears
DROP TABLE Node and all its edges disappear
Add REFERENCES New directed edge appears
Rename a column Column row updates in place
Add an index Index row appears in the table's Indexes section
Add a policy Policy row appears in the table's Policies section
Add an enum New enum node appears with all values as badges

Next Steps¶

  • Path Analysis — find the shortest FK join path between any two tables
  • Grouping & Schema Clusters — cluster tables by PostgreSQL schema with resizable group containers
  • Resona AI in the ER Diagram — AI analysis anchored to individual tables, schema groups, or the whole schema
  • Visibility & Node Types — control which object types and schemas are shown
  • Schema Compiler — static analysis that grades your schema and surfaces issues
  • Schema Diff — compare two schema versions side-by-side
sql-editor/er-diagram.raw:
---
title: ER Diagram
description: Visualize your entire PostgreSQL schema as a live, interactive entity-relationship diagram — all object types supported, updates in real time as you write SQL
tags: [sql-editor, er-diagram, visualization, relationships, tables, schema, postgresql, layout]
order: 4
sidebar_group: "SQL Editor"
related:
  - title: Path Analysis
    path: /sql-editor/er-diagram-path-analysis
  - title: Grouping & Schema Clusters
    path: /sql-editor/er-diagram-grouping
  - title: Resona AI in the ER Diagram
    path: /sql-editor/er-diagram-ai
  - title: Visibility & Node Types
    path: /sql-editor/er-diagram-visibility
---

# ER Diagram

The ER Diagram is a live, interactive visualization of your entire PostgreSQL schema. Every object — tables, views, materialized views, functions, enums, domains, sequences, roles, extensions, and RLS policies — renders as a node on a pannable, zoomable canvas. Foreign-key relationships appear as directed edges. The diagram updates in real time as you type SQL with no manual refresh required, and scales to schemas with thousands of tables.

> Quick links: [Open SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [Path Analysis](/sql-editor/er-diagram-path-analysis) · [Grouping](/sql-editor/er-diagram-grouping) · [AI Features](/sql-editor/er-diagram-ai)

![The ER Diagram showing tables, foreign-key edges, schema groups, and the toolbar controls](/images/sql-editor/er-digram.png)

---

## Opening the Diagram

The ER Diagram is built into the SQL Editor — there is no separate page to navigate to.

- **In standard mode**: Click **Graph** in the footer status bar — the diagram opens as a full-screen overlay
- **In fullscreen / split mode**: The diagram opens as a resizable panel alongside the editor, updating as you type
- **Keyboard shortcut**: `Ctrl+G` / `Cmd+G` toggles the diagram panel

---

## How the Diagram is Built

The diagram is generated entirely in the browser from the same compiled schema that feeds the Schema Compiler. No server round-trip is needed.

```
All project SQL files
        ↓
  Schema Weaver Custom SQL Parser
  (runs entirely in your browser — no server round-trip)
        ↓
  Schema Compiler (20 layers)
        ↓
  Relationship Graph
  (foreign keys, view deps, trigger targets, inferred)
        ↓
  ELK Auto-Layout Engine
        ↓
  React Flow Canvas
  (re-renders on every SQL change)
```

> **Custom parser:** Schema Weaver uses its own purpose-built PostgreSQL SQL parser that runs entirely in the browser. This is not a thin wrapper around a generic parser — it is a custom multi-phase parser designed specifically for DDL (schema definitions), handling PostgreSQL-specific syntax for partitions, RLS, identity columns, generated columns, domain types, and more. Because everything runs client-side, there is zero latency between typing SQL and seeing the diagram update.

Because the pipeline is entirely client-side, the diagram is always in sync with your code — add a `CREATE TABLE`, a new node appears immediately; add a `REFERENCES` clause, a new edge appears immediately.

---

## Supported PostgreSQL Objects

The ER Diagram renders every major PostgreSQL object type as a distinct node:

| Node Type | Visual | What it shows |
|---|---|---|
| **Table** | White/gray card | All columns with types, constraints, PKs, FKs, indexes, triggers, policies, check constraints |
| **View** | Dashed border card | Column count, source query context, Eye icon |
| **Materialized View** | Dashed border with refresh icon | Column count, REFRESH CONCURRENTLY command tooltip |
| **Function / Procedure** | Code card | Return type, PROC/FUNC badge, argument types |
| **Enum** | Type card | All enum values displayed as colored badges |
| **Domain** | Type card | Base type, NOT NULL constraint, CHECK expression |
| **Sequence** | Sequence card | Start value, increment, min/max bounds |
| **Role** | Role card | Attributes as badges: SUPERUSER, LOGIN, CREATEDB, CREATEROLE, BYPASSRLS, NOINHERIT |
| **Extension** | Extension card | Version, schema, Blocks icon |
| **Policy (RLS)** | Policy card | Command, roles, USING clause, WITH CHECK expression |
| **Schema Group** | Colored container | Groups all objects belonging to a PostgreSQL schema namespace |

Every object type can be shown or hidden independently — see [Visibility Controls](/sql-editor/er-diagram-visibility).

---

## Table Nodes in Detail

Tables are the most information-dense nodes in the diagram. Each table card shows:

### Column Rows

Every column row displays the column name, data type, and a set of constraint icons:

| Icon | Color | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 🔑 Key | Gold/yellow | Primary key column |
| 🔗 Link | Indigo | Foreign key column (with edge drawn to referenced table) |
| ✦ Sparkles | Emerald | Unique constraint |
| ≡ List | Amber | Indexed column |
| ⚡ Zap | Indigo | Generated column (`GENERATED ALWAYS`) |

Column rows also show nullability (`NOT NULL` marked with red asterisk) and whether the type is an enum (ENUM badge).

### Collapsible Sections

Expand any table node to reveal:

- **Indexes** — index name, type (B-tree, GiST, GIN, etc.), uniqueness, and column coverage
- **Triggers** — trigger name, timing (BEFORE/AFTER), events (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/TRUNCATE), and function name
- **Policies** — RLS policy name, command, applicable roles, USING and WITH CHECK expressions
- **Check Constraints** — constraint name and the CHECK expression

### View Modes

You can switch between three column display modes from the footer:

| Mode | What's shown |
|---|---|
| **ALL** | Every column with full metadata, all collapsible sections |
| **KEYS** | Only primary key and foreign key columns — compact overview |
| **TITLE** | Table name only — maximum density for very large schemas |

### Cascade Risk Indicator

If a table has a chain of `ON DELETE CASCADE` foreign keys that could affect many rows, the table node shows an amber/red glow border with a ⚡ badge and the count of tables in the cascade chain.

---

## Editing Tables from the Diagram

You can edit any table's structure directly from the ER Diagram without switching to the SQL editor. Click the **edit icon** (pencil) on any table node header to open the **Table Edit Panel**. It supports two modes, switchable with a toggle:

### Visual Editor

The visual mode lets you add, remove, or modify columns using a form interface — no SQL required. Each column row has fields for name, type, nullability, default value, and constraint toggles (PRIMARY KEY, UNIQUE, NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY).

![The ER Diagram table editor in visual mode showing column fields, type selectors, and constraint toggles for a selected table](/images/sql-editor/er-diagram-edittable-visual.png)

| Control | What it does |
|---|---|
| **Add column** | Appends a new empty column row to the form |
| **Delete column** | Removes the column (shows a confirmation for columns with FK dependencies) |
| **Type selector** | Dropdown of all PostgreSQL types including your project's custom enums and domains |
| **Constraint toggles** | Toggle PRIMARY KEY, NOT NULL, UNIQUE per column |
| **FK target** | Drop-down of all tables and columns in the project |

When you click **Save**, the edited table definition is serialized back to SQL and written into the active file. The ER Diagram re-renders immediately from the updated SQL.

### SQL Editor Mode

Switch to the SQL tab in the edit panel to view and edit the raw `CREATE TABLE` DDL directly. This mode gives you full control — useful for complex constraints, partial indexes on the table, or multi-column primary keys.

![The ER Diagram table editor in SQL mode showing the raw CREATE TABLE DDL with syntax highlighting](/images/sql-editor/er-digram-edit-sql.png)

Changes you make in SQL mode are validated before saving. If the SQL is invalid, the panel shows the parse error and prevents saving until it is fixed.

> **Bidirectional sync:** Visual mode and SQL mode show the same table. Switching between modes converts the representation in real time — edits made in visual mode are immediately visible in SQL mode and vice versa.

---

## Relationship Edges

Each foreign key is drawn as a directed edge from the referencing column to the referenced table:

| Edge style | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Solid arrow** | Standard foreign key |
| **Dashed arrow** | Nullable FK (optional relationship) |
| **Orange edge** | View dependency (view references another object) |
| **Red edge** | Trigger target relationship |
| **Low opacity** | Low-confidence inferred relationship (confidence < 0.8) |

Click any edge to see a side panel with the FK column names, `ON DELETE` rule (CASCADE, SET NULL, RESTRICT, NO ACTION), and deferrability.

---

## Navigation

| Action | How |
|---|---|
| **Pan** | Click and drag the canvas background |
| **Zoom in / out** | Scroll wheel, or use `+` / `−` buttons in the toolbar |
| **Fit all nodes** | Click **Fit** in the toolbar — centers and scales to show every node |
| **Select a table** | Click any node — highlights it and dims unrelated nodes |
| **Move a node** | Drag a node to reposition — positions are saved per session |
| **Reset layout** | Click **Layout → Re-run auto-layout** to restore ELK positions |
| **Search** | Type in the search box in the toolbar to filter visible nodes by name |

### Search

The toolbar search box filters the diagram in real time (300 ms debounce). Nodes whose names match the query stay visible; all others are dimmed. Matching is case-insensitive and partial.

---

## Auto-Layout

The diagram uses **ELK** (Eclipse Layout Kernel) to position nodes automatically. ELK respects FK relationships and arranges parent tables above (or to the left of) their dependent tables.

### Layout Direction

Switch layout direction from the toolbar dropdown:

| Direction | Description |
|---|---|
| **Left → Right (LR)** | Parent tables on the left, child tables flow rightward — good for wide schemas |
| **Top → Bottom (TB)** | Parent tables at the top, dependencies flow downward — good for deep hierarchies |
| **Right → Left (RL)** | Mirrored LR |
| **Bottom → Top (BT)** | Mirrored TB |

### Position Persistence

After ELK places nodes, you can drag any node to fine-tune its position. Positions are stored per schema (via Zustand) and restored when you return to the diagram. Click **Reset layout** to discard manual positions and re-run ELK from scratch.

### Performance & Scale

The ELK engine has a built-in timeout system:
- **Hierarchical layout** (with schema groups): 8-second timeout, then falls back to flat layout
- **Flat layout**: 15-second timeout

For schemas with thousands of tables, use **KEYS** or **TITLE** view mode and **Visibility Controls** to hide object types you don't need — this significantly reduces node count and speeds up layout.

---

## Toolbar Controls

| Control | Description |
|---|---|
| **Search box** | Filter visible nodes by name (real-time, debounced) |
| **Layout ▾** | Switch layout direction (LR / TB / RL / BT) |
| **Connect DB** | Open the database connection panel |
| **Export ▾** | Export diagram or schema (see below) |
| **Path** | Toggle path analysis mode — find shortest FK path between two tables |
| **Resona AI** | Open the global AI chat panel anchored to the diagram |
| **Fullscreen** | Toggle fullscreen mode |

---

## Footer Controls

| Control | Description |
|---|---|
| **Table / view / relationship counts** | Live stats of what's currently visible |
| **Schema filter badge** | Shown in amber when any schemas are hidden — click to clear |
| **View mode** | Switch between ALL / KEYS / TITLE column display |
| **Group ▾** | Toggle "Cluster by Schema" and "Lock Groups" |
| **Visibility ▾** | Toggle individual node types and schema groups |
| **Terminal** | Open the SQL terminal panel (linked to a live database connection) |

---

## Export

Click **Export** in the toolbar to save the diagram or schema in multiple formats:

### Image Exports

| Format | Description |
|---|---|
| **PNG** | High-resolution raster image (2× pixel ratio) — good for Notion, docs, Slack |
| **SVG** | Scalable vector image — good for presentations and print |

### Schema Code Exports

The diagram can also generate framework-ready code from your schema:

| Format | Output file | Use case |
|---|---|---|
| **Prisma Schema** | `schema.prisma` | Drop into a Prisma project |
| **Drizzle ORM** | `schema.ts` | Drop into a Drizzle project |
| **DBML** | `schema.dbml` | Import into dbdiagram.io |
| **TypeScript Types** | `types.ts` | Type-safe interfaces matching your schema |
| **Markdown Docs** | `SCHEMA.md` | Human-readable schema documentation |

All exports download as files with auto-generated names based on your project.

---

## Focus Mode (Subgraph)

Click the **Network icon** on any table node to enter focus mode. The diagram zooms in on that table and its direct relationships, hiding the rest. You can control:

- **Depth** — how many FK hops to follow from the root (1 = direct neighbors, 2 = two hops, up to 5)
- **Direction** — outbound (tables this table references), inbound (tables that reference this table), or both
- **Confidence threshold** — exclude low-confidence inferred relationships

A banner at the top of the diagram shows which table is focused. Click × to restore the full diagram.

---

## Real-Time Updates

The diagram re-renders automatically every time the SQL parser produces a new result:

| SQL change | Diagram change |
|---|---|
| `CREATE TABLE` | New node appears |
| `DROP TABLE` | Node and all its edges disappear |
| Add `REFERENCES` | New directed edge appears |
| Rename a column | Column row updates in place |
| Add an index | Index row appears in the table's Indexes section |
| Add a policy | Policy row appears in the table's Policies section |
| Add an enum | New enum node appears with all values as badges |

---

## Next Steps

- [Path Analysis](/sql-editor/er-diagram-path-analysis) — find the shortest FK join path between any two tables
- [Grouping & Schema Clusters](/sql-editor/er-diagram-grouping) — cluster tables by PostgreSQL schema with resizable group containers
- [Resona AI in the ER Diagram](/sql-editor/er-diagram-ai) — AI analysis anchored to individual tables, schema groups, or the whole schema
- [Visibility & Node Types](/sql-editor/er-diagram-visibility) — control which object types and schemas are shown
- [Schema Compiler](/sql-editor/schema-compiler) — static analysis that grades your schema and surfaces issues
- [Schema Diff](/sql-editor/schema-diff) — compare two schema versions side-by-side

frontmatter.title: File Manager
frontmatter.description: Organize your PostgreSQL schemas into cloud projects, folders, and files using the SQL Editor's built-in file manager

sql-editor/file-manager.content:
File Manager¶

The SQL Editor's file manager lets you organize your PostgreSQL schema definitions into projects, folders, and files. It works like a lightweight version of VS Code's Explorer sidebar — with drag-and-drop reordering, instant file search, and full cloud sync for team projects.

Quick links: Open SQL Editor · SQL Editor Overview · Collaboration

Dashboard — Your Project Workspace¶

When you first open the SQL Editor, you land on the Dashboard. This is the home screen for all your projects.

The Dashboard and the full file manager are both available in fullscreen mode — click the expand icon in the editor header to fill your entire browser window with the workspace.

Project List¶

Every project appears as a card showing its name, storage type, and team status. Click a project to see its details on the right panel. The currently opened project is marked with a green OPENED badge.

Badge Meaning
CLOUD Project stored in Schema Weaver's backend — synced across devices
LOCAL Project stored in your browser only — single device, no sharing
TEAM Project belongs to a team workspace
OPENED This project is the active project in the editor

Project Detail Panel¶

Selecting a project on the left reveals its details:

Field Description
FILES Total number of SQL files in the project
FOLDERS Number of folders (excluding the root)
MEMBERS Number of team members with access
DATABASE Name of the linked PostgreSQL connection, if any
STRUCTURE Auto-generated tree preview of folders and files

Click Open in Editor → to load the project into the editor and begin working.

Creating a Project¶

Click New in the dashboard header to open the project creation dialog.

Field Required Description
Name Yes Display name for the project (e.g. payments-service)
Description No Short summary shown in the dashboard
Team No Assign to a team workspace for shared access
Database No Link a PostgreSQL connection at creation time

After creating the project you land directly in the editor with an empty project ready for files.

Explorer Sidebar¶

Once a project is open, the left sidebar switches from the dashboard to the Explorer view. This is your file tree for the active project. The sidebar is fully visible in fullscreen mode, giving you the complete project file tree alongside the editor and diagram without any distractions.

Sections¶

Section Description
Back arrow Returns to the dashboard, closing the active project
Filter files Search box — type to instantly filter file and folder names
OPEN EDITORS Files currently open in tabs, in order of last access
PROJECT FILES Full file tree of the active project

File Tree Actions¶

Hover over any file or folder to reveal context actions:

Action How to trigger
New file Click + in the PROJECT FILES header, or right-click a folder
New folder Click the folder-plus icon in the PROJECT FILES header
Rename Right-click a file/folder → Rename, or press F2 after selecting
Delete Right-click → Delete (shows a confirmation dialog)
Move Drag a file or folder to a new parent location
Collapse all Click the collapse icon in the section header

Creating Files and Folders¶

New File Dialog¶

When you create a new file you'll be prompted for:

Field Description
Name The file name (e.g. users) — the .sql extension is added automatically
Extension File type: sql (default), pgsql, or txt
Location The folder this file belongs to (defaults to the project root)

The file opens immediately in a tab after creation and is auto-named inline so you can start typing right away.

New Folder Dialog¶

Folder names follow the same pattern — enter a name, pick the parent location, and the folder appears expanded in the tree.

Folder Templates¶

For common patterns, use New Folder from Template to create a pre-structured folder hierarchy in one step:

Template Creates
Migration project migrations/, seeds/, schema/
Service schema tables/, views/, functions/, triggers/
Custom Enter your own subfolder names

Fullscreen Split View¶

In fullscreen mode you can open a split panel to view two panes side by side — for example, your SQL file on the left and the live ER diagram on the right. This is the recommended way to work on large or complex schemas.

Click the split icon in the editor toolbar to activate split view:

Once open, the split panel shows the ER diagram updating in real time as you type — every table and relationship renders instantly without leaving the editor:

Drag and Drop¶

Files and folders can be dragged to reorganize the project:

  • Drag a file into any folder to move it there
  • Drag a file to the root area (below all folders) to move it to the top level
  • Drag a folder to nest it inside another folder
  • The tree updates immediately with an optimistic move; the server is updated in the background

File Search¶

The Filter files search box at the top of the Explorer filters the tree as you type. Matching files and their parent folders remain visible; non-matching items are hidden.

  • Searches file and folder names (case-insensitive)
  • Matching parent folders expand automatically to show matched children
  • Press Escape to clear the search and restore the full tree

Cloud vs Local Projects¶

Cloud Projects¶

Cloud projects are stored in Schema Weaver's backend (Supabase) and have these properties:

  • Synced across all your devices
  • Shareable with team members
  • Support version history and snapshots
  • Show a CLOUD badge in the dashboard
  • Require a Schema Weaver account

Local Projects¶

Local projects live entirely in your browser's storage:

  • No account required
  • Not synced between devices
  • Limited to 10 files (guest/local limit)
  • Show a LOCAL badge in the dashboard
  • Can be migrated to a cloud project at any time

Migrating Local to Cloud¶

When you sign in with an existing local project, Schema Weaver offers to migrate it:

  1. Click Migrate to Cloud when prompted
  2. Review the files that will be uploaded
  3. Confirm — files are uploaded and a new cloud project is created
  4. The local copy is cleaned up automatically

Recent Files¶

The dashboard shows your 8 most recently accessed files across all projects. Click any recent file to jump directly to it, reopening the parent project if needed.

Bulk Operations¶

Select multiple files by enabling Selection Mode (checkbox icon in the PROJECT FILES header):

Operation Description
Bulk delete Delete all selected files at once (confirmation required)
Bulk move Drag all selected files to a new folder simultaneously
Click + Shift Select a range of consecutive files

Next Steps¶

  • Writing SQL — editor tabs, syntax highlighting, autosave, split view
  • Collaboration — invite team members, manage roles, share projects
  • Database — link a project to a live PostgreSQL database
  • Version History — browse and restore past snapshots of any file

---
title: File Manager
description: Organize your PostgreSQL schemas into cloud projects, folders, and files using the SQL Editor's built-in file manager
tags: [sql-editor, file-manager, projects, folders, files, cloud, workspace]
order: 1
sidebar_group: "SQL Editor"
related:
  - title: SQL Editor Overview
    path: /sql-editor
  - title: Writing SQL
    path: /sql-editor/writing-sql
  - title: Collaboration
    path: /team-collaboration
---

# File Manager

The SQL Editor's file manager lets you organize your PostgreSQL schema definitions into projects, folders, and files. It works like a lightweight version of VS Code's Explorer sidebar — with drag-and-drop reordering, instant file search, and full cloud sync for team projects.

> Quick links: [Open SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [SQL Editor Overview](/sql-editor) · [Collaboration](/team-collaboration)

![The SQL Editor in fullscreen mode showing the active file open for editing alongside the ER diagram](/images/sql-editor/full-screen-sql-editing.png)

---

## Dashboard — Your Project Workspace

When you first open the SQL Editor, you land on the **Dashboard**. This is the home screen for all your projects.

> The Dashboard and the full file manager are both available in **fullscreen mode** — click the expand icon in the editor header to fill your entire browser window with the workspace.

![The SQL Editor Dashboard showing the project list with CLOUD and LOCAL badges and the project detail panel on the right](/images/sql-editor/workspace.png)

### Project List

Every project appears as a card showing its name, storage type, and team status. Click a project to see its details on the right panel. The currently **opened** project is marked with a green `OPENED` badge.

| Badge | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `CLOUD` | Project stored in Schema Weaver's backend — synced across devices |
| `LOCAL` | Project stored in your browser only — single device, no sharing |
| `TEAM` | Project belongs to a team workspace |
| `OPENED` | This project is the active project in the editor |

### Project Detail Panel

Selecting a project on the left reveals its details:

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| **FILES** | Total number of SQL files in the project |
| **FOLDERS** | Number of folders (excluding the root) |
| **MEMBERS** | Number of team members with access |
| **DATABASE** | Name of the linked PostgreSQL connection, if any |
| **STRUCTURE** | Auto-generated tree preview of folders and files |

Click **Open in Editor →** to load the project into the editor and begin working.

---

## Creating a Project

Click **New** in the dashboard header to open the project creation dialog.

| Field | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Name** | Yes | Display name for the project (e.g. `payments-service`) |
| **Description** | No | Short summary shown in the dashboard |
| **Team** | No | Assign to a team workspace for shared access |
| **Database** | No | Link a PostgreSQL connection at creation time |

After creating the project you land directly in the editor with an empty project ready for files.

---

## Explorer Sidebar

Once a project is open, the **left sidebar** switches from the dashboard to the Explorer view. This is your file tree for the active project. The sidebar is fully visible in **fullscreen mode**, giving you the complete project file tree alongside the editor and diagram without any distractions.

![The Explorer sidebar in fullscreen mode showing the project name, filter box, open editors list, and the full project file tree](/images/sql-editor/full-screen-file-tree.png)

### Sections

| Section | Description |
|---|---|
| **Back arrow** | Returns to the dashboard, closing the active project |
| **Filter files** | Search box — type to instantly filter file and folder names |
| **OPEN EDITORS** | Files currently open in tabs, in order of last access |
| **PROJECT FILES** | Full file tree of the active project |

### File Tree Actions

Hover over any file or folder to reveal context actions:

| Action | How to trigger |
|---|---|
| **New file** | Click `+` in the PROJECT FILES header, or right-click a folder |
| **New folder** | Click the folder-plus icon in the PROJECT FILES header |
| **Rename** | Right-click a file/folder → Rename, or press `F2` after selecting |
| **Delete** | Right-click → Delete (shows a confirmation dialog) |
| **Move** | Drag a file or folder to a new parent location |
| **Collapse all** | Click the collapse icon in the section header |

---

## Creating Files and Folders

### New File Dialog

When you create a new file you'll be prompted for:

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| **Name** | The file name (e.g. `users`) — the `.sql` extension is added automatically |
| **Extension** | File type: `sql` (default), `pgsql`, or `txt` |
| **Location** | The folder this file belongs to (defaults to the project root) |

The file opens immediately in a tab after creation and is auto-named inline so you can start typing right away.

### New Folder Dialog

Folder names follow the same pattern — enter a name, pick the parent location, and the folder appears expanded in the tree.

#### Folder Templates

For common patterns, use **New Folder from Template** to create a pre-structured folder hierarchy in one step:

| Template | Creates |
|---|---|
| **Migration project** | `migrations/`, `seeds/`, `schema/` |
| **Service schema** | `tables/`, `views/`, `functions/`, `triggers/` |
| **Custom** | Enter your own subfolder names |

---

## Fullscreen Split View

In fullscreen mode you can open a **split panel** to view two panes side by side — for example, your SQL file on the left and the live ER diagram on the right. This is the recommended way to work on large or complex schemas.

Click the **split icon** in the editor toolbar to activate split view:

![The split view option in the SQL Editor toolbar showing how to open a second panel alongside the editor](/images/sql-editor/full-screen-split-option.png)

Once open, the split panel shows the ER diagram updating in real time as you type — every table and relationship renders instantly without leaving the editor:

![The SQL Editor in fullscreen split view with the SQL file on the left and the live ER diagram on the right](/images/sql-editor/full-screen-split-view-with-graph.png)

---

## Drag and Drop

Files and folders can be dragged to reorganize the project:

- **Drag a file** into any folder to move it there
- **Drag a file** to the root area (below all folders) to move it to the top level
- **Drag a folder** to nest it inside another folder
- The tree updates immediately with an optimistic move; the server is updated in the background

---

## File Search

The **Filter files** search box at the top of the Explorer filters the tree as you type. Matching files and their parent folders remain visible; non-matching items are hidden.

- Searches file and folder names (case-insensitive)
- Matching parent folders expand automatically to show matched children
- Press `Escape` to clear the search and restore the full tree

---

## Cloud vs Local Projects

### Cloud Projects

Cloud projects are stored in Schema Weaver's backend (Supabase) and have these properties:

- Synced across all your devices
- Shareable with team members
- Support version history and snapshots
- Show a `CLOUD` badge in the dashboard
- Require a Schema Weaver account

### Local Projects

Local projects live entirely in your browser's storage:

- No account required
- Not synced between devices
- Limited to 10 files (guest/local limit)
- Show a `LOCAL` badge in the dashboard
- Can be migrated to a cloud project at any time

#### Migrating Local to Cloud

When you sign in with an existing local project, Schema Weaver offers to migrate it:

1. Click **Migrate to Cloud** when prompted
2. Review the files that will be uploaded
3. Confirm — files are uploaded and a new cloud project is created
4. The local copy is cleaned up automatically

---

## Recent Files

The dashboard shows your **8 most recently accessed files** across all projects. Click any recent file to jump directly to it, reopening the parent project if needed.

---

## Bulk Operations

Select multiple files by enabling **Selection Mode** (checkbox icon in the PROJECT FILES header):

| Operation | Description |
|---|---|
| **Bulk delete** | Delete all selected files at once (confirmation required) |
| **Bulk move** | Drag all selected files to a new folder simultaneously |
| Click + `Shift` | Select a range of consecutive files |

---

## Next Steps

- [Writing SQL](/sql-editor/writing-sql) — editor tabs, syntax highlighting, autosave, split view
- [Collaboration](/team-collaboration) — invite team members, manage roles, share projects
- [Database](/database) — link a project to a live PostgreSQL database
- [Version History](/sql-editor/version-history) — browse and restore past snapshots of any file

frontmatter.description: Write, organize, and analyze PostgreSQL schemas in the Schema Weaver SQL Editor — the primary workspace for schema development

headings.title: What can you do in the SQL Editor?

sql-editor.content:
SQL Editor¶

The SQL Editor is the primary workspace in Schema Weaver. It's where you write PostgreSQL schema definitions, organize them into projects and files, analyze your schema quality with the built-in compiler, and visualize table relationships as an interactive ER diagram — all in one place.

Quick links: Open SQL Editor · Docs Home · Data Explorer · Schema Weaver Landing

What can you do in the SQL Editor?¶

Capability Description
Multi-file projects Organize schemas into cloud projects with folders and files
Syntax-highlighted editing PostgreSQL-aware editor with color coding and auto-indent
Tab-based workflow Open multiple files simultaneously, switch between them instantly
Schema Compiler 20-layer static analysis that grades your schema A–F and surfaces issues
ER Diagram Auto-generated visual diagram of tables and relationships from your SQL
Schema Diff Side-by-side comparison of two schema states with visual highlighting
Version History Browse and restore every saved snapshot of your files
Resona AI Generate SQL with natural language, explain schemas, and review diffs
Database Connections Link your project to a live PostgreSQL database for drift detection and migrations — managed in the Database section
Team Collaboration Invite members to a project with read, write, or admin roles

Interface Layout¶

Left Sidebar — Explorer¶

Region Purpose
Project name Shows the active project with a back arrow to return to the dashboard
PROJECT FILES Collapsible tree of all files and folders in the active project
OPEN EDITORS Quick list of currently open tabs
Filter files Instant search across all file and folder names
Action icons New file, new folder, collapse all, toggle sidebar

Center — Editor¶

The main code editing area with syntax highlighting, tab management, and split/fullscreen controls. Each open file gets its own tab in the header bar.

Bottom — Status Bar¶

Button Opens
Connect Database connection panel — link to a live PostgreSQL database
Compiler Schema analysis panel with grade, issue list, and recommendations
Diff Side-by-side schema diff between two SQL states
Graph ER diagram visualizing all tables and relationships

Core Concepts¶

Projects¶

A project is the top-level container for related schema files. Projects can be:

  • Cloud — Stored in Schema Weaver's backend, accessible from any device, shareable with teams
  • Local — Stored in your browser, single-device only, no sharing

Each project has its own folder structure, file set, and optional database connection.

Files and Folders¶

Inside a project you can create any hierarchy of folders and .sql files. The SQL Editor merges all files in a project when computing the ER diagram, running the Schema Compiler, and preparing migration diffs — so you can split your schema across as many files as makes sense for your team.

The Compilation Pipeline¶

Every time you type SQL, the editor:

  1. Parses the SQL using a multi-phase PostgreSQL parser
  2. Merges all project files into a single schema
  3. Compiles the merged schema through 20 analysis layers
  4. Renders the ER diagram from the compiled output
  5. Grades the schema and surfaces any detected issues

This all happens in the browser — no server round-trips required for the analysis.

Supported PostgreSQL Objects¶

Object Type Support
Tables ✅ Full — columns, constraints, defaults
Indexes ✅ Full — B-tree, GiST, GIN, partial, expression
Views ✅ Full — regular and materialized
Functions & Procedures ✅ Full — PL/pgSQL, SQL, argument types
Triggers ✅ Full — row/statement, BEFORE/AFTER/INSTEAD OF
Row-Level Security ✅ Full — policies, enforcement
Enums & Custom Types ✅ Full — enums, domains, composite, range
Foreign Keys ✅ Full — with cascade rules and relationship visualization
Sequences ✅ Full — identity columns, serial detection
Extensions ✅ Detected — pgcrypto, uuid-ossp, PostGIS, etc.
Partitions ✅ Full — RANGE, LIST, HASH, inheritance trees
Schemas (namespaces) ✅ Supported — multi-schema projects

Quick Start¶

  1. Open sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com and sign in
  2. Click New on the dashboard to create your first project
  3. Create a file (e.g. schema.sql) inside the project
  4. Paste or write your PostgreSQL CREATE TABLE statements
  5. Watch the ER diagram build automatically in the Graph panel
  6. Open Compiler in the footer to see your schema grade and any detected issues
  7. Optionally link a live database with Connect to enable drift detection

Next Steps¶

  • File Manager — projects, cloud storage, folders, drag-and-drop
  • Writing SQL — tabs, syntax highlighting, autosave, split view
  • Schema Compiler — grades, issues, 20-layer analysis
  • ER Diagram — visual schema, layout, subgraphs, path analysis
  • Schema Diff — comparing schema versions side-by-side
  • Version History — browsing and restoring past snapshots
  • Resona AI — SQL generation, explanations, and review
  • Database — connect to a live PostgreSQL database, run migrations, and detect drift
  • Collaboration — team projects, member roles, sharing

### Center — Editor

### Bottom — Status Bar

| Button | Opens |
|---|---|
| **Connect** | Database connection panel — link to a live PostgreSQL database |
| **Compiler** | Schema analysis panel with grade, issue list, and recommendations |
| **Diff** | Side-by-side schema diff between two SQL states |
| **Graph** | ER diagram visualizing all tables and relationships |

---

## Core Concepts

### Projects

A **project** is the top-level container for related schema files. Projects can be:

- **Cloud** — Stored in Schema Weaver's backend, accessible from any device, shareable with teams
- **Local** — Stored in your browser, single-device only, no sharing

Each project has its own folder structure, file set, and optional database connection.

### Files and Folders

Inside a project you can create any hierarchy of folders and `.sql` files. The SQL Editor **merges all files in a project** when computing the ER diagram, running the Schema Compiler, and preparing migration diffs — so you can split your schema across as many files as makes sense for your team.

### The Compilation Pipeline

Every time you type SQL, the editor:

1. **Parses** the SQL using a multi-phase PostgreSQL parser
2. **Merges** all project files into a single schema
3. **Compiles** the merged schema through 20 analysis layers
4. **Renders** the ER diagram from the compiled output
5. **Grades** the schema and surfaces any detected issues

This all happens in the browser — no server round-trips required for the analysis.

---

## Supported PostgreSQL Objects

| Object Type | Support |
|---|---|
| Tables | ✅ Full — columns, constraints, defaults |
| Indexes | ✅ Full — B-tree, GiST, GIN, partial, expression |
| Views | ✅ Full — regular and materialized |
| Functions & Procedures | ✅ Full — PL/pgSQL, SQL, argument types |
| Triggers | ✅ Full — row/statement, BEFORE/AFTER/INSTEAD OF |
| Row-Level Security | ✅ Full — policies, enforcement |
| Enums & Custom Types | ✅ Full — enums, domains, composite, range |
| Foreign Keys | ✅ Full — with cascade rules and relationship visualization |
| Sequences | ✅ Full — identity columns, serial detection |
| Extensions | ✅ Detected — pgcrypto, uuid-ossp, PostGIS, etc. |
| Partitions | ✅ Full — RANGE, LIST, HASH, inheritance trees |
| Schemas (namespaces) | ✅ Supported — multi-schema projects |

---

## Quick Start

1. Open [sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) and sign in
2. Click **New** on the dashboard to create your first project
3. Create a file (e.g. `schema.sql`) inside the project
4. Paste or write your PostgreSQL `CREATE TABLE` statements
5. Watch the ER diagram build automatically in the **Graph** panel
6. Open **Compiler** in the footer to see your schema grade and any detected issues
7. Optionally link a live database with **Connect** to enable drift detection

---

## Next Steps

- [File Manager](/sql-editor/file-manager) — projects, cloud storage, folders, drag-and-drop
