frontmatter.title: Schema Compiler
frontmatter.description: Understand the SQL Editor's 20-layer schema compiler — how it grades your PostgreSQL schema, surfaces issues, and helps you write production-ready DDL

sql-editor/schema-compiler.content:
Schema Compiler¶

The Schema Compiler is a static analysis engine built into the SQL Editor. Every time you edit a file, it silently re-compiles the merged project SQL through 20 analysis layers and produces a health grade with a detailed issue report — all in your browser, with no server round-trip.

Quick links: Open SQL Editor · SQL Editor Overview · Writing SQL

Opening the Compiler Panel¶

Click Compiler in the footer status bar to open the analysis panel. The button also shows a color-coded grade badge so you can see your schema health at a glance without opening the panel.

Badge Grade Meaning
🟢 A 90–100 Excellent — production-ready
🟢 B 80–89 Good — minor issues
🟡 C 70–79 Acceptable — some gaps to address
🟠 D 60–69 Needs work — multiple issues present
🔴 F < 60 Critical — blocking issues detected

What the Compiler Checks¶

The compiler processes your schema through 20 layers in sequence. Each layer analyzes a specific aspect of your PostgreSQL objects.

Layer Overview¶

# Layer What it analyzes
1 Database Encoding, server version, installed extensions
2 Schemas Namespace-level object counts and anomalies
3 Tables Table definitions, missing PKs, naming conventions
4 Columns Types, nullability, default values, text vs bounded types
5 Types Enums, domains, composite types, range types
6 Constraints PKs, FKs, unique, check, exclusion constraints
7 Indexes Redundant indexes, missing indexes on FK columns, expression indexes
8 Partitions Partition hierarchies, orphaned child tables
9 Views Broken references, circular dependencies, materialized view freshness
10 Functions Security definers, mutable functions in indexes, volatile defaults
11 Triggers Event trigger ordering conflicts, duplicate triggers
12 RLS Row-level security coverage, policies without USING clauses
13 Privileges Over-privileged roles, public schema exposure scoring
14 Sequences Orphan sequences, mismatched identity columns
15 Extensions Installed vs available extensions, version mismatches
16 Dependencies Object dependency graph, circular references
17 Storage Table bloat estimates, TOAST configuration
18 Replication Replication slot status, tables without REPLICA IDENTITY
19 Semantic Naming convention consistency, plural/singular table names
20 Metrics Final health score, complexity rating, overall grade

Issue Severity Levels¶

Every detected issue is tagged with a severity:

Severity Color When to act
Critical 🔴 Red Act immediately — blocks production use
Warning 🟠 Orange Address before the next release
Info 🔵 Blue Best-practice suggestion — low urgency
Hint ⚪ Gray Optional improvement — can safely ignore

Common Issues Detected¶

Tables & Columns¶

Issue Severity Description
Missing primary key Warning Table has no PRIMARY KEY — replication and ORMs need one
Unbounded text column Info Use varchar(n) or a domain to document intent
Nullable foreign key without index Warning Can cause sequential scans on joins
Column with no default or constraint Hint Explicit NOT NULL or a DEFAULT documents intent clearly
serial instead of identity Hint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY is the modern alternative to serial

Indexes¶

Issue Severity Description
Redundant index Warning An index is a prefix of another — one can be dropped
Missing FK index Warning Foreign key column has no index — join performance will suffer
Unused expression index Info Expression indexes should be validated against real query plans
Duplicate index Critical Two identical indexes waste storage and slow writes

Foreign Keys & Constraints¶

Issue Severity Description
FK references non-PK column Warning FK target should be a PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE column
Check constraint always true Info Tautological CHECK clauses add noise with no benefit
Missing ON DELETE rule Hint Explicit cascade behavior is safer than implicit NO ACTION

Security¶

Issue Severity Description
RLS enabled but no policies Critical All rows are blocked — queries will return empty results
SECURITY DEFINER function Warning Runs with owner privileges — review for privilege escalation risk
PUBLIC schema accessible Warning All authenticated users can create objects in public — restrict with REVOKE
Table without RLS in a multi-tenant schema Info Consider enabling RLS for tenant isolation

Views & Functions¶

Issue Severity Description
View references dropped table Critical View will fail at query time
Circular view dependency Critical Two views reference each other — this cannot be resolved at runtime
VOLATILE function in index Critical Non-deterministic functions cannot be used in expression indexes
Materialized view with no unique index Warning REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY requires a unique index

Reading the Compiler Panel¶

The panel is split into three sections:

1. Summary Bar¶

Schema Health      Grade: B      Score: 83 / 100
────────────────────────────────────────────────
  🔴 Critical   0    🟠 Warning   3    🔵 Info   7    ⚪ Hint  12

2. Issue List¶

Issues are grouped by severity. Each entry shows:

Element Description
Icon + severity Color-coded severity indicator
Object name The table, column, index, or function the issue relates to
Message A plain-English description of the problem
Category Which layer detected this issue (e.g. indexes, rls, constraints)
Fix suggestion An example SQL statement that would resolve the issue

Click any issue to jump directly to the relevant object in the SQL editor.

3. Metrics Summary¶

  Tables         24     Columns       187     Indexes        31
  Views           3     Functions       8     Triggers        5
  Enums           4     Policies        6     Extensions      3
  Complexity    HIGH     Replication  PARTIAL  Overall   GOOD

How the Grade is Calculated¶

The grade is computed from a weighted score across all 20 layers:

Score = Σ (layer_weight × layer_score) / Σ layer_weights

Layer weights (highest → lowest):
  Constraints (20%) → Tables (15%) → Security/RLS (15%) →
  Indexes (12%) → Functions (10%) → Views (8%) →
  Columns (8%) → Remaining layers (12% combined)

Each critical issue reduces the score by 5–15 points. Warnings reduce by 1–3 points. Info and hints do not affect the numeric score.

Compiler in the Workflow¶

The compiler runs automatically every time you stop typing (debounced at 500 ms). You don't need to trigger it manually.

You type SQL  →  Parser runs  →  Compiler runs  →  Grade updates in footer

The ER diagram also re-renders from the same compilation output, so diagram and analysis are always in sync with your code.

Example: Fixing a Warning¶

Given this schema:

CREATE TABLE orders (
  id   serial,
  user uuid REFERENCES users(id)
);

The compiler would flag:

  • orders — Warning: Missing primary key
  • orders.id — Hint: Use GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY instead of serial
  • orders.user — Warning: FK column user has no index

Fixed version:

CREATE TABLE orders (
  id      integer GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id uuid    NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_id ON orders (user_id);

After applying the fix, the grade improves and all three issues disappear.

Next Steps¶

  • ER Diagram — visualize the compiled schema as an interactive relationship diagram
  • Schema Diff — compare two versions of your schema
  • Database — run migrations against a live database
  • Writing SQL — editor tabs, syntax highlighting, and autosave

# Schema Compiler

The Schema Compiler is a static analysis engine built into the SQL Editor. Every time you edit a file, it silently re-compiles the merged project SQL through 20 analysis layers and produces a health grade with a detailed issue report — all in your browser, with no server round-trip.

> Quick links: [Open SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [SQL Editor Overview](/sql-editor) · [Writing SQL](/sql-editor/writing-sql)

---

## Opening the Compiler Panel

Click **Compiler** in the footer status bar to open the analysis panel. The button also shows a color-coded grade badge so you can see your schema health at a glance without opening the panel.

![The Schema Compiler panel showing the health grade, issue list with severities, and the metrics summary](/images/sql-editor/compiler.png)

| Badge | Grade | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 🟢 **A** | 90–100 | Excellent — production-ready |
| 🟢 **B** | 80–89 | Good — minor issues |
| 🟡 **C** | 70–79 | Acceptable — some gaps to address |
| 🟠 **D** | 60–69 | Needs work — multiple issues present |
| 🔴 **F** | < 60 | Critical — blocking issues detected |

---

## What the Compiler Checks

The compiler processes your schema through **20 layers** in sequence. Each layer analyzes a specific aspect of your PostgreSQL objects.

### Layer Overview

| # | Layer | What it analyzes |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Database** | Encoding, server version, installed extensions |
| 2 | **Schemas** | Namespace-level object counts and anomalies |
| 3 | **Tables** | Table definitions, missing PKs, naming conventions |
| 4 | **Columns** | Types, nullability, default values, `text` vs bounded types |
| 5 | **Types** | Enums, domains, composite types, range types |
| 6 | **Constraints** | PKs, FKs, unique, check, exclusion constraints |
| 7 | **Indexes** | Redundant indexes, missing indexes on FK columns, expression indexes |
| 8 | **Partitions** | Partition hierarchies, orphaned child tables |
| 9 | **Views** | Broken references, circular dependencies, materialized view freshness |
| 10 | **Functions** | Security definers, mutable functions in indexes, volatile defaults |
| 11 | **Triggers** | Event trigger ordering conflicts, duplicate triggers |
| 12 | **RLS** | Row-level security coverage, policies without USING clauses |
| 13 | **Privileges** | Over-privileged roles, public schema exposure scoring |
| 14 | **Sequences** | Orphan sequences, mismatched identity columns |
| 15 | **Extensions** | Installed vs available extensions, version mismatches |
| 16 | **Dependencies** | Object dependency graph, circular references |
| 17 | **Storage** | Table bloat estimates, TOAST configuration |
| 18 | **Replication** | Replication slot status, tables without REPLICA IDENTITY |
| 19 | **Semantic** | Naming convention consistency, plural/singular table names |
| 20 | **Metrics** | Final health score, complexity rating, overall grade |

---

## Issue Severity Levels

Every detected issue is tagged with a severity:

| Severity | Color | When to act |
|---|---|---|
| **Critical** | 🔴 Red | Act immediately — blocks production use |
| **Warning** | 🟠 Orange | Address before the next release |
| **Info** | 🔵 Blue | Best-practice suggestion — low urgency |
| **Hint** | ⚪ Gray | Optional improvement — can safely ignore |

---

## Common Issues Detected

### Tables & Columns

| Issue | Severity | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Missing primary key | Warning | Table has no `PRIMARY KEY` — replication and ORMs need one |
| Unbounded `text` column | Info | Use `varchar(n)` or a domain to document intent |
| Nullable foreign key without index | Warning | Can cause sequential scans on joins |
| Column with no default or constraint | Hint | Explicit `NOT NULL` or a `DEFAULT` documents intent clearly |
| `serial` instead of `identity` | Hint | `GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY` is the modern alternative to `serial` |

### Indexes

| Issue | Severity | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Redundant index | Warning | An index is a prefix of another — one can be dropped |
| Missing FK index | Warning | Foreign key column has no index — join performance will suffer |
| Unused expression index | Info | Expression indexes should be validated against real query plans |
| Duplicate index | Critical | Two identical indexes waste storage and slow writes |

### Foreign Keys & Constraints

| Issue | Severity | Description |
|---|---|---|
| FK references non-PK column | Warning | FK target should be a `PRIMARY KEY` or `UNIQUE` column |
| Check constraint always true | Info | Tautological `CHECK` clauses add noise with no benefit |
| Missing `ON DELETE` rule | Hint | Explicit cascade behavior is safer than implicit `NO ACTION` |

### Security

| Issue | Severity | Description |
|---|---|---|
| RLS enabled but no policies | Critical | All rows are blocked — queries will return empty results |
| `SECURITY DEFINER` function | Warning | Runs with owner privileges — review for privilege escalation risk |
| `PUBLIC` schema accessible | Warning | All authenticated users can create objects in `public` — restrict with `REVOKE` |
| Table without RLS in a multi-tenant schema | Info | Consider enabling RLS for tenant isolation |

### Views & Functions

| Issue | Severity | Description |
|---|---|---|
| View references dropped table | Critical | View will fail at query time |
| Circular view dependency | Critical | Two views reference each other — this cannot be resolved at runtime |
| `VOLATILE` function in index | Critical | Non-deterministic functions cannot be used in expression indexes |
| Materialized view with no unique index | Warning | `REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY` requires a unique index |

---

## Reading the Compiler Panel

The panel is split into three sections:

### 1. Summary Bar

```
Schema Health      Grade: B      Score: 83 / 100
────────────────────────────────────────────────
  🔴 Critical   0    🟠 Warning   3    🔵 Info   7    ⚪ Hint  12
```

### 2. Issue List

Issues are grouped by severity. Each entry shows:

| Element | Description |
|---|---|
| **Icon + severity** | Color-coded severity indicator |
| **Object name** | The table, column, index, or function the issue relates to |
| **Message** | A plain-English description of the problem |
| **Category** | Which layer detected this issue (e.g. `indexes`, `rls`, `constraints`) |
| **Fix suggestion** | An example SQL statement that would resolve the issue |

Click any issue to jump directly to the relevant object in the SQL editor.

### 3. Metrics Summary

```
  Tables         24     Columns       187     Indexes        31
  Views           3     Functions       8     Triggers        5
  Enums           4     Policies        6     Extensions      3
  Complexity    HIGH     Replication  PARTIAL  Overall   GOOD
```

---

## How the Grade is Calculated

The grade is computed from a weighted score across all 20 layers:

```
Score = Σ (layer_weight × layer_score) / Σ layer_weights

Layer weights (highest → lowest):
  Constraints (20%) → Tables (15%) → Security/RLS (15%) →
  Indexes (12%) → Functions (10%) → Views (8%) →
  Columns (8%) → Remaining layers (12% combined)
```

Each critical issue reduces the score by 5–15 points. Warnings reduce by 1–3 points. Info and hints do not affect the numeric score.

---

## Compiler in the Workflow

The compiler runs **automatically** every time you stop typing (debounced at 500 ms). You don't need to trigger it manually.

```
You type SQL  →  Parser runs  →  Compiler runs  →  Grade updates in footer
```

The ER diagram also re-renders from the same compilation output, so diagram and analysis are always in sync with your code.

---

## Example: Fixing a Warning

Given this schema:

```sql
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id   serial,
  user uuid REFERENCES users(id)
);
```

The compiler would flag:

- `orders` — **Warning**: Missing primary key
- `orders.id` — **Hint**: Use `GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY` instead of `serial`
- `orders.user` — **Warning**: FK column `user` has no index

Fixed version:

```sql
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id      integer GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id uuid    NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_id ON orders (user_id);
```

After applying the fix, the grade improves and all three issues disappear.

---

## Next Steps

- [ER Diagram](/sql-editor/er-diagram) — visualize the compiled schema as an interactive relationship diagram
- [Schema Diff](/sql-editor/schema-diff) — compare two versions of your schema
- [Database](/database) — run migrations against a live database
- [Writing SQL](/sql-editor/writing-sql) — editor tabs, syntax highlighting, and autosave

frontmatter.title: Schema Diff
frontmatter.description: Compare two versions of a PostgreSQL schema side-by-side with the SQL Editor's built-in diff tool — highlight additions, removals, and modifications

sql-editor/schema-diff.content:
Schema Diff¶

The Schema Diff tool lets you compare two PostgreSQL schema states side-by-side. It highlights every added, removed, and modified object — tables, columns, indexes, constraints, and more — so you can review changes before applying a migration or audit what drifted in production.

Quick links: Open SQL Editor · SQL Editor Overview · Version History

Opening the Diff Panel¶

Click Diff in the footer status bar to open the diff panel. You can also access it from the Schema Diff option in the top toolbar.

Choosing What to Diff¶

The diff panel has two selectors — Left (Base) and Right (Head) — which define what you are comparing.

Source Description
Current file The SQL currently in the editor for the selected file
Version snapshot A saved historical snapshot of a file (from version history)
Live database The current schema pulled from a connected PostgreSQL database
Clipboard Paste any SQL directly into the diff input

Common diff scenarios:

Scenario Left Right
Review uncommitted edits Saved snapshot Current file
Preview a migration Current project Live database
Audit production drift Live database Last deployed snapshot
Review a teammate's PR Main branch snapshot PR branch snapshot

The Diff View¶

Once both sides are selected, the diff renders in three ways depending on your preference:

Unified Diff (default)¶

A single-column view with additions and removals interleaved:

  CREATE TABLE users (
    id          uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    email       text NOT NULL UNIQUE,
+   username    text,
-   full_name   text NOT NULL,
+   display_name text,
    created_at  timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
  );

+ CREATE INDEX idx_users_username ON users (username);
Color Meaning
🟢 Green line (prefix +) Added — present in Right but not Left
🔴 Red line (prefix -) Removed — present in Left but not Right
⚪ White line Unchanged context

Side-by-side Diff¶

A two-column view with Left on the left and Right on the right. Changed lines are highlighted in their respective columns. Useful when reviewing large blocks of SQL where you want to read both versions simultaneously.

Semantic Diff¶

Rather than raw line-by-line comparison, the semantic diff understands SQL structure and reports changes at the object level:

MODIFIED  TABLE users
  ADD COLUMN    username text
  DROP COLUMN   full_name
  ADD COLUMN    display_name text

ADDED  INDEX idx_users_username
  ON users (username)

Semantic diff is noise-free — reformatting, reindentation, and comment changes are ignored. Only structural DDL changes appear.

Diff Summary Bar¶

At the top of the diff panel:

Changes:  + 3 added   ~ 2 modified   - 1 removed
Objects:    tables: 0   columns: 4   indexes: 1   constraints: 0
Counter Description
Added Objects present in Right but not Left
Modified Objects present in both but changed
Removed Objects present in Left but not Right

Navigating the Diff¶

Action How
Jump to next change Click ↓ in the diff toolbar or press Alt+↓
Jump to previous change Click ↑ or press Alt+↑
Filter by change type Use the + / ~ / - toggle buttons to show only adds, modifications, or removals
Filter by object type Use the Tables / Columns / Indexes / Constraints chips to focus
Expand context Click … between hunks to show more surrounding lines

Inline Diff Overlay¶

When you have a database connection active, an inline diff overlay can be shown directly inside the SQL editor (not just in the diff panel). Changed lines are highlighted with a colored gutter marker:

Gutter color Meaning
🟢 Green bar Line added vs the live database version
🔴 Red bar Line present in the database but removed in your editor
🟡 Yellow bar Line modified relative to the database version

This overlay makes it easy to see exactly what your current edits would change in production without switching away from the editor.

Toggle the overlay with Diff → Show inline diff in the footer.

Exporting a Diff¶

Click Export in the diff panel header to download the diff:

Format Use case
Unified patch (.diff) Standard diff format — can be attached to a PR or stored in git
Migration SQL The additions and modifications expressed as executable DDL statements
Markdown report Human-readable summary with a change table — useful for PR descriptions

Diff and Migrations¶

The diff between your current schema file and the live database is the foundation for Schema Weaver's migration engine. When you click Apply Migration from the connections panel, Schema Weaver:

  1. Runs a semantic diff between your project SQL and the live database schema
  2. Generates a safe migration plan (handling type casts, FK order, enum additions)
  3. Shows you the migration SQL for review before applying

See Database for details on connecting to a live database and running migrations.

Next Steps¶

  • Version History — browse past snapshots to use as diff sources
  • Database — diff your SQL against a live database
  • Schema Compiler — analyze your schema for issues before diffing
  • ER Diagram — visualize the structural changes alongside the diff

# Schema Diff

The Schema Diff tool lets you compare two PostgreSQL schema states side-by-side. It highlights every added, removed, and modified object — tables, columns, indexes, constraints, and more — so you can review changes before applying a migration or audit what drifted in production.

> Quick links: [Open SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [SQL Editor Overview](/sql-editor) · [Version History](/sql-editor/version-history)

---

## Opening the Diff Panel

Click **Diff** in the footer status bar to open the diff panel. You can also access it from the **Schema Diff** option in the top toolbar.

---

## Choosing What to Diff

The diff panel has two selectors — **Left (Base)** and **Right (Head)** — which define what you are comparing.

| Source | Description |
|---|---|
| **Current file** | The SQL currently in the editor for the selected file |
| **Version snapshot** | A saved historical snapshot of a file (from version history) |
| **Live database** | The current schema pulled from a connected PostgreSQL database |
| **Clipboard** | Paste any SQL directly into the diff input |

Common diff scenarios:

| Scenario | Left | Right |
|---|---|---|
| Review uncommitted edits | Saved snapshot | Current file |
| Preview a migration | Current project | Live database |
| Audit production drift | Live database | Last deployed snapshot |
| Review a teammate's PR | Main branch snapshot | PR branch snapshot |

---

## The Diff View

Once both sides are selected, the diff renders in three ways depending on your preference:

### Unified Diff (default)

A single-column view with additions and removals interleaved:

```diff
  CREATE TABLE users (
    id          uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    email       text NOT NULL UNIQUE,
+   username    text,
-   full_name   text NOT NULL,
+   display_name text,
    created_at  timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
  );

+ CREATE INDEX idx_users_username ON users (username);
```

| Color | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 🟢 Green line (prefix `+`) | Added — present in Right but not Left |
| 🔴 Red line (prefix `-`) | Removed — present in Left but not Right |
| ⚪ White line | Unchanged context |

### Side-by-side Diff

A two-column view with Left on the left and Right on the right. Changed lines are highlighted in their respective columns. Useful when reviewing large blocks of SQL where you want to read both versions simultaneously.

### Semantic Diff

Rather than raw line-by-line comparison, the semantic diff understands SQL structure and reports changes at the **object level**:

```
MODIFIED  TABLE users
  ADD COLUMN    username text
  DROP COLUMN   full_name
  ADD COLUMN    display_name text

ADDED  INDEX idx_users_username
  ON users (username)
```

Semantic diff is noise-free — reformatting, reindentation, and comment changes are ignored. Only structural DDL changes appear.

---

## Diff Summary Bar

At the top of the diff panel:

```
Changes:  + 3 added   ~ 2 modified   - 1 removed
Objects:    tables: 0   columns: 4   indexes: 1   constraints: 0
```

| Counter | Description |
|---|---|
| **Added** | Objects present in Right but not Left |
| **Modified** | Objects present in both but changed |
| **Removed** | Objects present in Left but not Right |

---

## Navigating the Diff

| Action | How |
|---|---|
| Jump to next change | Click `↓` in the diff toolbar or press `Alt+↓` |
| Jump to previous change | Click `↑` or press `Alt+↑` |
| Filter by change type | Use the **+** / **~** / **-** toggle buttons to show only adds, modifications, or removals |
| Filter by object type | Use the **Tables / Columns / Indexes / Constraints** chips to focus |
| Expand context | Click `…` between hunks to show more surrounding lines |

---

## Inline Diff Overlay

When you have a database connection active, an **inline diff overlay** can be shown directly inside the SQL editor (not just in the diff panel). Changed lines are highlighted with a colored gutter marker:

| Gutter color | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 🟢 Green bar | Line added vs the live database version |
| 🔴 Red bar | Line present in the database but removed in your editor |
| 🟡 Yellow bar | Line modified relative to the database version |

This overlay makes it easy to see exactly what your current edits would change in production without switching away from the editor.

Toggle the overlay with **Diff → Show inline diff** in the footer.

---

## Exporting a Diff

Click **Export** in the diff panel header to download the diff:

| Format | Use case |
|---|---|
| **Unified patch (.diff)** | Standard diff format — can be attached to a PR or stored in git |
| **Migration SQL** | The additions and modifications expressed as executable DDL statements |
| **Markdown report** | Human-readable summary with a change table — useful for PR descriptions |

---

## Diff and Migrations

The diff between your current schema file and the live database is the foundation for Schema Weaver's migration engine. When you click **Apply Migration** from the connections panel, Schema Weaver:

1. Runs a semantic diff between your project SQL and the live database schema
2. Generates a safe migration plan (handling type casts, FK order, enum additions)
3. Shows you the migration SQL for review before applying

See [Database](/database) for details on connecting to a live database and running migrations.

---

## Next Steps

- [Version History](/sql-editor/version-history) — browse past snapshots to use as diff sources
- [Database](/database) — diff your SQL against a live database
- [Schema Compiler](/sql-editor/schema-compiler) — analyze your schema for issues before diffing
- [ER Diagram](/sql-editor/er-diagram) — visualize the structural changes alongside the diff

frontmatter.title: Version History
frontmatter.description: Browse and restore every saved snapshot of your SQL files in the Schema Weaver SQL Editor — never lose a working schema state

sql-editor/version-history.content:
Version History¶

The SQL Editor keeps a complete snapshot history of every file in your cloud projects. Every save creates an immutable version entry — you can browse the timeline, compare any two snapshots, or restore a previous state with a single click.

Quick links: Open SQL Editor · SQL Editor Overview · Schema Diff

Opening Version History¶

There are two ways to open the version history panel:

  1. Right-click any file in the Explorer sidebar → Version History
  2. Header menu → History when a file is active in the editor

The panel slides in from the right, showing the full timeline for the selected file.

The Version Timeline¶

The history panel lists every saved version of the file, newest first:

Version History — auth.sql
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
 ● Today, 14:32    "Added sessions table"          You
 ● Today, 11:07    "Auto-save"                     You
 ● Yesterday 18:44 "Moved RLS policies to auth"    teammate@company.com
 ● Yesterday 09:12 "Initial schema"                You
 ● Apr 12, 22:30   "Auto-save"                     You
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Each entry shows:

Field Description
Timestamp When the version was saved (relative for recent, absolute for older)
Label A short description — auto-generated from the save context or manually set
Author The team member who saved this version
Indicator ● for the current version, ○ for historical

Viewing a Past Version¶

Click any version in the timeline to load it into a read-only preview in the main editor. The file tab shows auth.sql (v2024-04-12) to make it clear you're viewing a historical state.

While previewing a past version you can:

  • Read the SQL — full syntax highlighting, no editing
  • Copy any text
  • Diff against current — click Compare with current to open the diff panel with this snapshot as the Left side
  • Restore — click Restore this version to make this snapshot the active content of the file

Restoring a Version¶

Click Restore this version to replace the current file content with the selected snapshot.

This creates a new version entry at the top of the timeline with the label Restored from [timestamp] — the restore is non-destructive. If you restore by mistake, you can immediately restore the previous version.

 ● Just now        "Restored from Apr 12, 22:30"   You   ← new entry
 ● Today, 14:32    "Added sessions table"           You
 ● Today, 11:07    "Auto-save"                      You
   ...
 ● Apr 12, 22:30   "Initial schema"                 You   ← source

Labeling a Version¶

By default, save events are labeled Auto-save. You can add a meaningful label:

  1. Right-click any version → Edit label
  2. Type a description (e.g. Before adding audit triggers)
  3. Press Enter to save

Labels are visible to all team members with access to the project and are useful for milestone tracking (v1.0 launch schema, post-incident RLS fix, etc.).

Comparing Two Historical Versions¶

To compare any two snapshots (not just against current):

  1. Hover a version → click Pin as Left
  2. Click a second version → it becomes the Right side
  3. Click Compare — the diff panel opens with both snapshots loaded

This lets you answer questions like "What changed between last Tuesday and the version we deployed Friday?"

Version Retention¶

Project type Retention
Cloud project (free tier) 30 days of history
Cloud project (pro tier) Unlimited history
Local project No version history (local storage only)

How Versions Are Created¶

A new version is created whenever:

Trigger Label
Autosave fires (after 3 seconds of inactivity) Auto-save
Manual save (Ctrl+S / Cmd+S) Manual save
File restored from a previous snapshot Restored from [timestamp]
Migration applied (snapshot before and after) Pre-migration snapshot / Post-migration snapshot
AI change accepted AI-generated: [description]

Version History and Migrations¶

Every time a migration is applied through Schema Weaver's migration engine, two version entries are created automatically:

  • Pre-migration — a snapshot of the file immediately before the migration was applied
  • Post-migration — a snapshot reflecting the new state, with the migration ID as the label

This gives you a reliable rollback point for every migration event. If a migration causes unexpected issues, restore to the pre-migration snapshot, fix your SQL, and re-apply.

Next Steps¶

  • Schema Diff — compare any two version snapshots in the diff panel
  • Database — migrations create automatic version snapshots
  • Writing SQL — autosave behavior and manual save
  • Collaboration — version history is shared with all team members on a project

# Version History

The SQL Editor keeps a complete snapshot history of every file in your cloud projects. Every save creates an immutable version entry — you can browse the timeline, compare any two snapshots, or restore a previous state with a single click.

> Quick links: [Open SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [SQL Editor Overview](/sql-editor) · [Schema Diff](/sql-editor/schema-diff)

---

## Opening Version History

There are two ways to open the version history panel:

1. **Right-click** any file in the Explorer sidebar → **Version History**
2. **Header menu** → **History** when a file is active in the editor

The panel slides in from the right, showing the full timeline for the selected file.

---

## The Version Timeline

The history panel lists every saved version of the file, newest first:

```
Version History — auth.sql
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
 ● Today, 14:32    "Added sessions table"          You
 ● Today, 11:07    "Auto-save"                     You
 ● Yesterday 18:44 "Moved RLS policies to auth"    teammate@company.com
 ● Yesterday 09:12 "Initial schema"                You
 ● Apr 12, 22:30   "Auto-save"                     You
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
```

Each entry shows:

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| **Timestamp** | When the version was saved (relative for recent, absolute for older) |
| **Label** | A short description — auto-generated from the save context or manually set |
| **Author** | The team member who saved this version |
| **Indicator** | `●` for the current version, `○` for historical |

---

## Viewing a Past Version

Click any version in the timeline to load it into a **read-only preview** in the main editor. The file tab shows `auth.sql (v2024-04-12)` to make it clear you're viewing a historical state.

While previewing a past version you can:

- **Read** the SQL — full syntax highlighting, no editing
- **Copy** any text
- **Diff against current** — click **Compare with current** to open the diff panel with this snapshot as the Left side
- **Restore** — click **Restore this version** to make this snapshot the active content of the file

---

## Restoring a Version

Click **Restore this version** to replace the current file content with the selected snapshot.

This creates a **new version entry** at the top of the timeline with the label `Restored from [timestamp]` — the restore is non-destructive. If you restore by mistake, you can immediately restore the previous version.

```
 ● Just now        "Restored from Apr 12, 22:30"   You   ← new entry
 ● Today, 14:32    "Added sessions table"           You
 ● Today, 11:07    "Auto-save"                      You
   ...
 ● Apr 12, 22:30   "Initial schema"                 You   ← source
```

---

## Labeling a Version

By default, save events are labeled `Auto-save`. You can add a meaningful label:

1. Right-click any version → **Edit label**
2. Type a description (e.g. `Before adding audit triggers`)
3. Press `Enter` to save

Labels are visible to all team members with access to the project and are useful for milestone tracking (`v1.0 launch schema`, `post-incident RLS fix`, etc.).

---

## Comparing Two Historical Versions

To compare any two snapshots (not just against current):

1. Hover a version → click **Pin as Left**
2. Click a second version → it becomes the Right side
3. Click **Compare** — the diff panel opens with both snapshots loaded

This lets you answer questions like "What changed between last Tuesday and the version we deployed Friday?"

---

## Version Retention

| Project type | Retention |
|---|---|
| **Cloud project (free tier)** | 30 days of history |
| **Cloud project (pro tier)** | Unlimited history |
| **Local project** | No version history (local storage only) |

---

## How Versions Are Created

A new version is created whenever:

| Trigger | Label |
|---|---|
| **Autosave fires** (after 3 seconds of inactivity) | `Auto-save` |
| **Manual save** (`Ctrl+S` / `Cmd+S`) | `Manual save` |
| **File restored** from a previous snapshot | `Restored from [timestamp]` |
| **Migration applied** (snapshot before and after) | `Pre-migration snapshot` / `Post-migration snapshot` |
| **AI change accepted** | `AI-generated: [description]` |

---

## Version History and Migrations

Every time a migration is applied through Schema Weaver's migration engine, two version entries are created automatically:

- **Pre-migration** — a snapshot of the file immediately before the migration was applied
- **Post-migration** — a snapshot reflecting the new state, with the migration ID as the label

This gives you a reliable rollback point for every migration event. If a migration causes unexpected issues, restore to the pre-migration snapshot, fix your SQL, and re-apply.

---

## Next Steps

- [Schema Diff](/sql-editor/schema-diff) — compare any two version snapshots in the diff panel
- [Database](/database) — migrations create automatic version snapshots
- [Writing SQL](/sql-editor/writing-sql) — autosave behavior and manual save
- [Collaboration](/team-collaboration) — version history is shared with all team members on a project

frontmatter.title: Writing SQL
frontmatter.description: Use the SQL Editor's tabs, syntax highlighting, autosave, split view, and fullscreen mode to write PostgreSQL schema definitions efficiently

sql-editor/writing-sql.content:
Writing SQL¶

The SQL Editor gives you a PostgreSQL-aware code editor with multi-file tabs, syntax highlighting, autosave, and a fullscreen mode. This page covers everything about the editing experience itself.

Quick links: Open SQL Editor · SQL Editor Overview · Schema Compiler

The Editor¶

The center panel is a fully featured code editor optimized for PostgreSQL schema definitions. It handles large files gracefully and keeps the interface responsive even with hundreds of tables.

The left sidebar is your file manager — project name, folder tree, open editors list, and file search all live here. Use it to navigate between files, create new ones, and organize your schema across folders.

Syntax Highlighting¶

The editor colors your SQL to make it immediately readable:

Color Element
Keyword CREATE, TABLE, PRIMARY KEY, REFERENCES, NOT NULL, etc.
Type text, uuid, timestamptz, integer, jsonb, boolean, etc.
String String literals and quoted identifiers
Comment -- single line and /* block */ comments
Number Numeric literals and constants
Operator =, ::, ->, ->>, @>, etc.

Auto-Indentation¶

The editor automatically indents new lines inside CREATE TABLE, BEGIN ... END, and function bodies. Pressing Enter after an opening ( indents the next line by one level.

Line Numbers¶

Line numbers are displayed in the left gutter. Clicking a line number selects the entire line.

Tabs — Working with Multiple Files¶

Each file you open gets its own tab in the header bar at the top of the editor.

│  auth.sql  ×  │  billing.sql  ×  │  schema.sql  ●  │  +  │
Element Meaning
File name The name of the open file
× Close this tab
● (dot) This tab has unsaved changes
Italic name Preview tab — not yet pinned (single-click opens in preview, double-click pins)
+ Open a new file or create a file

Tab Behavior¶

Action Result
Single-click a file in the Explorer Opens in a preview tab (italic) — replaces any existing preview
Double-click a file in the Explorer Opens in a permanent tab
Double-click a preview tab Pins the tab permanently
Close all tabs Returns to the welcome/dashboard view
Middle-click a tab Closes the tab

Tab Context Menu¶

Right-click any tab to access:

Option Description
Close Close this tab
Close Others Close all tabs except the current one
Close All Close every open tab
Reveal in Explorer Scrolls and highlights the file in the left sidebar tree

Autosave¶

The editor autosaves your changes automatically. The footer status bar shows:

Status Meaning
Autosave ✓ Autosave is on — changes are saved a few seconds after you stop typing
Saving... A save is in progress
Saved + timestamp The last successful save time
Unsaved changes Autosave is off and there are pending changes

Toggle Autosave¶

Click the autosave indicator in the footer to toggle it on or off. When autosave is off, use Ctrl+S / Cmd+S to save manually.

Tip: Autosave uses optimistic conflict detection. If the server copy was updated by a team member since your last load, a conflict toast appears and lets you choose to overwrite or reload.

Fullscreen Mode¶

Click the fullscreen icon (↗) in the top-right of the editor header to expand the editor to fill the entire browser window.

In fullscreen mode:

  • The left Explorer sidebar appears, giving you the full file tree
  • The bottom status bar remains accessible
  • The ER diagram, compiler, and diff panels open as overlays
  • Press Escape or click the collapse icon to exit fullscreen

Tip: Fullscreen mode is the recommended way to work on large schemas. The sidebar and status bar give you everything you need without context-switching to other pages.

Split View¶

Click the split view icon in the header to open a second panel alongside the editor. The right panel shows the live ER diagram — every table and relationship updates instantly as you type SQL, giving you a real-time visual of your schema without switching views.

Split view is also used for:

  • Comparing two files side by side
  • Keeping the Resona AI chat panel open while you edit
  • Viewing the diff panel while reviewing changes

Drag the divider between panels to adjust the relative widths.

Keyboard Shortcuts¶

Shortcut Action
Ctrl+S / Cmd+S Save current file
Ctrl+Z / Cmd+Z Undo
Ctrl+Shift+Z / Cmd+Shift+Z Redo
Ctrl+/ / Cmd+/ Toggle line comment (--)
Ctrl+D / Cmd+D Select next occurrence of current word
Ctrl+F / Cmd+F Find in current file
Ctrl+H / Cmd+H Find and replace
Alt+↑ / Alt+↓ Move current line up or down
Ctrl+Shift+K / Cmd+Shift+K Delete current line
Ctrl+Enter Insert line below without breaking the current line
Tab Indent selected lines
Shift+Tab Unindent selected lines

The Merge Model¶

The SQL Editor maintains a merged view of all files in the active project. This merged SQL is what feeds the parser, schema compiler, and ER diagram.

auth.sql     + billing.sql   + schema.sql
     ↓               ↓               ↓
           Merged SQL (in memory)
                    ↓
     SQL Parser → Schema Compiler → ER Diagram

This means:

  • You can split your schema across as many files as you like — the analysis always sees the full picture
  • A REFERENCES in billing.sql pointing to a table defined in auth.sql will resolve correctly
  • The order files are merged follows the folder structure (folders first, then files, alphabetically within each group)
  • Circular or missing references are caught and reported in the Compiler

Format Button¶

The Format button in the footer toolbar reformats the SQL in the current file:

  • Normalizes indentation and spacing
  • Uppercases SQL keywords
  • Wraps long lines at 100 characters
  • Preserves comments

Formatting is non-destructive — it only affects whitespace and casing, never the schema logic.

Generate Button¶

The Generate button opens the Resona AI SQL generation dialog. See Resona AI for full details.

Downloading Files¶

Right-click any file in the Explorer → Download to save it as a .sql file to your local machine. The download contains the raw SQL content of that file only (not the merged project SQL).

To export the full merged schema, use the Copy button in the footer toolbar — this copies the entire merged SQL to your clipboard.

File Extensions¶

The editor supports three file extensions:

Extension Use
.sql Standard SQL — the default for all schema files
.pgsql PostgreSQL-specific — same parsing and highlighting as .sql
.txt Plain text — useful for notes, changelogs, or documentation inside a project

Next Steps¶

  • Schema Compiler — analyze your SQL for issues, grades, and best practices
  • ER Diagram — visualize tables and relationships from your SQL
  • Schema Diff — compare two versions of your schema side-by-side
  • Version History — browse and restore past snapshots of any file
  • Resona AI — generate SQL from natural language descriptions
sql-editor/writing-sql.raw:
---
title: Writing SQL
description: Use the SQL Editor's tabs, syntax highlighting, autosave, split view, and fullscreen mode to write PostgreSQL schema definitions efficiently
tags: [sql-editor, writing, syntax-highlighting, tabs, autosave, editor]
order: 2
sidebar_group: "SQL Editor"
related:
  - title: SQL Editor Overview
    path: /sql-editor
  - title: File Manager
    path: /sql-editor/file-manager
  - title: Schema Compiler
    path: /sql-editor/schema-compiler
---

# Writing SQL

The SQL Editor gives you a PostgreSQL-aware code editor with multi-file tabs, syntax highlighting, autosave, and a fullscreen mode. This page covers everything about the editing experience itself.

> Quick links: [Open SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [SQL Editor Overview](/sql-editor) · [Schema Compiler](/sql-editor/schema-compiler)

![The Schema Weaver SQL Editor showing the file tabs, PostgreSQL syntax highlighting, Explorer sidebar, and status bar](/images/sql-editor/sql-editor.png)

---

## The Editor

The center panel is a fully featured code editor optimized for PostgreSQL schema definitions. It handles large files gracefully and keeps the interface responsive even with hundreds of tables.

The **left sidebar** is your file manager — project name, folder tree, open editors list, and file search all live here. Use it to navigate between files, create new ones, and organize your schema across folders.

![The SQL Editor left sidebar showing the project name, Explorer file tree, open editors section, and filter search box](/images/sql-editor/sql-editor-leftside-bar.png)

### Syntax Highlighting

The editor colors your SQL to make it immediately readable:

| Color | Element |
|---|---|
| **Keyword** | `CREATE`, `TABLE`, `PRIMARY KEY`, `REFERENCES`, `NOT NULL`, etc. |
| **Type** | `text`, `uuid`, `timestamptz`, `integer`, `jsonb`, `boolean`, etc. |
| **String** | String literals and quoted identifiers |
| **Comment** | `-- single line` and `/* block */` comments |
| **Number** | Numeric literals and constants |
| **Operator** | `=`, `::`, `->`, `->>`, `@>`, etc. |

### Auto-Indentation

The editor automatically indents new lines inside `CREATE TABLE`, `BEGIN ... END`, and function bodies. Pressing `Enter` after an opening `(` indents the next line by one level.

### Line Numbers

Line numbers are displayed in the left gutter. Clicking a line number selects the entire line.

---

## Tabs — Working with Multiple Files

Each file you open gets its own **tab** in the header bar at the top of the editor.

```
│  auth.sql  ×  │  billing.sql  ×  │  schema.sql  ●  │  +  │
```

| Element | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **File name** | The name of the open file |
| **×** | Close this tab |
| **●** (dot) | This tab has unsaved changes |
| **Italic name** | Preview tab — not yet pinned (single-click opens in preview, double-click pins) |
| **+** | Open a new file or create a file |

### Tab Behavior

| Action | Result |
|---|---|
| Single-click a file in the Explorer | Opens in a preview tab (italic) — replaces any existing preview |
| Double-click a file in the Explorer | Opens in a permanent tab |
| Double-click a preview tab | Pins the tab permanently |
| Close all tabs | Returns to the welcome/dashboard view |
| Middle-click a tab | Closes the tab |

### Tab Context Menu

Right-click any tab to access:

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| **Close** | Close this tab |
| **Close Others** | Close all tabs except the current one |
| **Close All** | Close every open tab |
| **Reveal in Explorer** | Scrolls and highlights the file in the left sidebar tree |

---

## Autosave

The editor autosaves your changes automatically. The footer status bar shows:

| Status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `Autosave ✓` | Autosave is on — changes are saved a few seconds after you stop typing |
| `Saving...` | A save is in progress |
| `Saved` + timestamp | The last successful save time |
| `Unsaved changes` | Autosave is off and there are pending changes |

### Toggle Autosave

Click the autosave indicator in the footer to toggle it on or off. When autosave is off, use `Ctrl+S` / `Cmd+S` to save manually.

> **Tip:** Autosave uses optimistic conflict detection. If the server copy was updated by a team member since your last load, a conflict toast appears and lets you choose to overwrite or reload.

---

## Fullscreen Mode

Click the **fullscreen icon** (↗) in the top-right of the editor header to expand the editor to fill the entire browser window.

In fullscreen mode:

- The **left Explorer sidebar** appears, giving you the full file tree
- The **bottom status bar** remains accessible
- The ER diagram, compiler, and diff panels open as overlays
- Press `Escape` or click the collapse icon to exit fullscreen

> **Tip:** Fullscreen mode is the recommended way to work on large schemas. The sidebar and status bar give you everything you need without context-switching to other pages.

---

## Split View

Click the **split view icon** in the header to open a second panel alongside the editor. The right panel shows the **live ER diagram** — every table and relationship updates instantly as you type SQL, giving you a real-time visual of your schema without switching views.

![The SQL Editor right panel showing the ER diagram updating in real time as SQL is written in the left editor panel](/images/sql-editor/sql-edtior-rightside-bar.png)

Split view is also used for:

- Comparing two files side by side
- Keeping the Resona AI chat panel open while you edit
- Viewing the diff panel while reviewing changes

Drag the divider between panels to adjust the relative widths.

---

## Keyboard Shortcuts

| Shortcut | Action |
|---|---|
| `Ctrl+S` / `Cmd+S` | Save current file |
| `Ctrl+Z` / `Cmd+Z` | Undo |
| `Ctrl+Shift+Z` / `Cmd+Shift+Z` | Redo |
| `Ctrl+/` / `Cmd+/` | Toggle line comment (`--`) |
| `Ctrl+D` / `Cmd+D` | Select next occurrence of current word |
| `Ctrl+F` / `Cmd+F` | Find in current file |
| `Ctrl+H` / `Cmd+H` | Find and replace |
| `Alt+↑` / `Alt+↓` | Move current line up or down |
| `Ctrl+Shift+K` / `Cmd+Shift+K` | Delete current line |
| `Ctrl+Enter` | Insert line below without breaking the current line |
| `Tab` | Indent selected lines |
| `Shift+Tab` | Unindent selected lines |

---

## The Merge Model

The SQL Editor maintains a **merged view** of all files in the active project. This merged SQL is what feeds the parser, schema compiler, and ER diagram.

```
auth.sql     + billing.sql   + schema.sql
     ↓               ↓               ↓
           Merged SQL (in memory)
                    ↓
     SQL Parser → Schema Compiler → ER Diagram
```

This means:

- You can split your schema across as many files as you like — the analysis always sees the full picture
- A `REFERENCES` in `billing.sql` pointing to a table defined in `auth.sql` will resolve correctly
- The order files are merged follows the folder structure (folders first, then files, alphabetically within each group)
- Circular or missing references are caught and reported in the Compiler

---

## Format Button

The **Format** button in the footer toolbar reformats the SQL in the current file:

- Normalizes indentation and spacing
- Uppercases SQL keywords
- Wraps long lines at 100 characters
- Preserves comments

Formatting is non-destructive — it only affects whitespace and casing, never the schema logic.

---

## Generate Button

The **Generate** button opens the **Resona AI SQL generation** dialog. See [Resona AI](/sql-editor/ai-assistant) for full details.

---

## Downloading Files

Right-click any file in the Explorer → **Download** to save it as a `.sql` file to your local machine. The download contains the raw SQL content of that file only (not the merged project SQL).

To export the full merged schema, use the **Copy** button in the footer toolbar — this copies the entire merged SQL to your clipboard.

---

## File Extensions

The editor supports three file extensions:

| Extension | Use |
|---|---|
| `.sql` | Standard SQL — the default for all schema files |
| `.pgsql` | PostgreSQL-specific — same parsing and highlighting as `.sql` |
| `.txt` | Plain text — useful for notes, changelogs, or documentation inside a project |

---

## Next Steps

- [Schema Compiler](/sql-editor/schema-compiler) — analyze your SQL for issues, grades, and best practices
- [ER Diagram](/sql-editor/er-diagram) — visualize tables and relationships from your SQL
- [Schema Diff](/sql-editor/schema-diff) — compare two versions of your schema side-by-side
- [Version History](/sql-editor/version-history) — browse and restore past snapshots of any file
- [Resona AI](/sql-editor/ai-assistant) — generate SQL from natural language descriptions

frontmatter.title: How Resona AI Works
frontmatter.description: Understand the agentic loop, tool execution pipeline, and the full set of analysis tools Resona uses to answer your data questions

data-explorer/agentic-workflows.content:
How Resona AI Works¶

Resona AI is not a simple chatbot. It is an agentic system — when you ask a question, it breaks the problem down into steps, calls specialized tools to gather and analyze data, and synthesizes the results into a complete answer. You can watch every step happen in real time.

Quick links: Open Data Explorer · AI Chat Interface · Charts & Analysis Results

The Three Phases¶

Every Resona response goes through three phases, visible in the Agent Status Bar above the response.

Phase 1 — Planning¶

Resona reads your message and classifies the intent (trend analysis, anomaly detection, segmentation, etc.). It then generates a step-by-step plan: which tables to inspect, which tools to call, and in what order.

The plan appears as a numbered list of steps in the chat. Each step shows its status — pending, running, or complete. Complex requests may have 5–10 steps.

Phase 2 — Tool Execution¶

Resona executes the plan by calling tools one at a time (or in parallel when steps are independent). Each tool call is shown live: the tool name, the parameters being passed, and the result once it returns.

If a tool fails — for example, a SQL query has a syntax error — Resona reads the error message and automatically retries with a corrected query. This self-healing happens transparently up to 3 attempts per tool.

Phase 3 — Synthesis¶

Once all tools have run, Resona drafts its final answer using the collected data. This is where charts, tables, and stat blocks are assembled and streamed back to you alongside the text explanation.

Tools Resona Can Use¶

Resona has access to a library of 40+ specialized tools organized into categories. When you ask a question, it picks the right combination automatically based on your intent.

Data Access¶

These tools let Resona discover and read your database. They are always available regardless of what you ask.

Tool What it does
list_tables Lists all tables with estimated row counts and sizes
get_schema Returns full column definitions, types, and FK relationships for a table
sample_rows Fetches a representative sample of rows from a table
run_query Executes a SQL SELECT against your database
count_rows Counts rows with an optional filter condition
search_rows Full-text search across a table's text columns
get_column_stats Returns null %, distinct count, min, max for every column

Statistics & Distributions¶

Tool What it does Output
compute_statistics Mean, median, std dev, percentiles (p25/p75/p95), skewness, kurtosis Stat block
compute_distribution Frequency histogram — how values are distributed across buckets Bar chart
compute_correlation Pearson correlation matrix between all numeric columns Heatmap

Aggregation & Ranking¶

Tool What it does Output
compute_aggregation GROUP BY with SUM, COUNT, AVG, MIN, MAX Bar chart or table
compute_top_n Top N rows ranked by a chosen metric Ranked table or chart
compute_ranking Full ranking with percentile positions Table

Time Series & Trends¶

Tool What it does Output
compute_trend Trend line over a date/time column Line chart
compare_periods Period-over-period comparison — MoM, QoQ, YoY Grouped bar chart
compute_moving_average Rolling average with configurable window Line chart
detect_seasonality Identifies recurring periodic patterns in time series data Seasonality block

Segmentation & Cohorts¶

Tool What it does Output
segment_data Groups rows into behavioral or value-based segments Segment chart
compute_cohort Cohort analysis — groups users by signup period and tracks behavior over time Cohort heatmap
compute_funnel Conversion funnel — drop-off at each step of a multi-stage process Funnel chart
compute_retention User/customer retention curves over time Retention heatmap

Anomaly & Data Quality¶

Tool What it does Output
detect_anomalies Statistical outlier detection — flags rows or values that deviate significantly Anomaly block
check_quality Scans for completeness gaps, nulls, type mismatches, and integrity violations Quality report
detect_outliers Extreme value detection using IQR and z-score methods Outlier block

Text Analysis¶

Tool What it does Output
analyze_text_column Length distribution, top tokens, pattern detection for string columns Text analysis block
compute_cardinality Distinct value count and entropy — how "unique" a column is Cardinality block
detect_duplicates Finds exact and near-duplicate rows across a table Duplicate report

Comparison¶

Tool What it does Output
compare_tables Side-by-side comparison of schema and row-level differences between two tables Diff block
compare_segments Behavioral differences between two user or customer groups Comparison chart
compute_pareto Pareto analysis — which 20% of items drive 80% of a metric Pareto chart

Predictive¶

Tool What it does Output
forecast_trend Projects a time series forward using trend extrapolation Forecast chart
compute_regression Linear regression — feature coefficients, p-values, R² Regression block
classify_risk Scores rows by risk level based on configurable rules Risk table

Relationship Discovery¶

Tool What it does Output
find_join_paths Discovers foreign key paths between tables so Resona can JOIN them Join map
infer_metrics Identifies likely business metrics (revenue, churn, conversion) in your schema Metric suggestions

Reports & Exports¶

Tool What it does Output
generate_summary_report Structured markdown/HTML summary combining multiple analyses Report artifact
generate_ppt Professional PowerPoint slide deck with charts and summaries .pptx file
generate_pdf PDF version of the analysis report .pdf file

Watching Tool Execution¶

The Tool History section appears inside each assistant message (click "Tools Used" to expand it). It shows:

  • Every tool that was called, in order
  • The exact parameters passed to each tool
  • The result summary returned
  • Latency per tool call

This gives you full transparency into how Resona arrived at its answer. If a result looks unexpected, the tool history is the first place to check.

Parallel Execution¶

When a plan has independent steps — for example, fetching schema for two different tables simultaneously — Resona runs those tool calls in parallel. This significantly reduces response time for multi-table analyses.

Steps that depend on each other (e.g., "run this query first, then chart the result") are kept sequential automatically.

Iteration Limit¶

Resona caps each response at 25 tool calls and 120 seconds of wall-clock time to protect against runaway costs. If a very complex request approaches these limits, Resona will synthesize a partial answer with what it has and note what it was unable to complete.

For very large analyses, breaking your question into smaller follow-up messages produces better results than a single very broad request.

![Resona AI agentic interface showing the planning and execution phases](/images/data-explorer/resona-ai.png)

## The Three Phases

Every Resona response goes through three phases, visible in the **Agent Status Bar** above the response.

### Phase 1 — Planning

### Time Series & Trends

### Segmentation & Cohorts

### Anomaly & Data Quality

## Watching Tool Execution

The **Tool History** section appears inside each assistant message (click "Tools Used" to expand it). It shows:

frontmatter.title: Charts & Analysis Results
frontmatter.description: Understand the artifact types Resona AI generates — charts, stat blocks, and data tables — and how to interact with them

data-explorer/ai-artifacts.content:
Charts & Analysis Results¶

When Resona AI finishes an analysis, it outputs artifacts — rich interactive components embedded directly in the chat. These are not static images. You can expand them, sort them, pin them to a dashboard, and export their underlying SQL.

Quick links: Open Data Explorer · AI Chat Interface · Workspace & Projects

Data Tables¶

When Resona runs a SQL query or returns row-level results, it renders them as an interactive data table.

  • Smart formatting — dates, numbers, and null values are formatted automatically for readability
  • Sortable columns — click any column header to sort ascending or descending
  • Inline filtering — type to filter visible rows without re-running the query
  • Export — download the table as CSV or Excel directly from the artifact toolbar

Data tables are produced by the run_query, sample_rows, search_rows, compute_top_n, and compute_ranking tools.

Charts¶

Resona generates interactive charts using Recharts. The chart type is chosen automatically based on the analysis being performed.

Bar Chart¶

Used for comparisons — aggregations, top-N rankings, period-over-period comparisons, segment breakdowns.

Produced by: compute_aggregation, compute_top_n, compare_periods, compare_segments, compute_distribution

Line Chart¶

Used for trends over time — revenue over months, user signups over weeks, moving averages.

Produced by: compute_trend, compute_moving_average, forecast_trend

Area Chart¶

Line chart with a filled area — used for cumulative metrics or stacked time series.

Pie / Donut Chart¶

Used for proportional breakdowns where parts must sum to a whole.

Scatter Chart¶

Used for correlation analysis — plots two numeric columns against each other to show relationships.

Produced by: compute_correlation (individual pairs)

Heatmap¶

Used for correlation matrices and cohort / retention grids. Cell color intensity represents the value magnitude.

Produced by: compute_correlation, compute_cohort, compute_retention

Funnel Chart¶

Used for conversion analysis — each stage shows absolute count and drop-off percentage to the next stage.

Produced by: compute_funnel

Forecast Chart¶

A line chart extended with a projected future segment and a confidence interval band.

Produced by: forecast_trend

Stat Blocks¶

Stat blocks are structured analytical outputs for computations that don't fit a standard chart. Each type has its own layout.

Statistics Block¶

Summary statistics for one or more numeric columns: mean, median, standard deviation, min, max, 25th/75th/95th percentiles, skewness, kurtosis.

Produced by: compute_statistics

Distribution Block¶

Frequency histogram showing how values are spread across value buckets.

Produced by: compute_distribution

Seasonality Block¶

Identifies repeating patterns in time series data. Shows the dominant period (e.g., weekly, monthly), the strength of the pattern, and a visual of the cycle.

Produced by: detect_seasonality

Anomaly Block¶

Highlights rows or values that deviate significantly from the expected distribution. Each anomaly entry shows the column, the anomalous value, and the expected range.

Produced by: detect_anomalies

Outlier Block¶

Extreme value detection using IQR and z-score. Separates high and low outliers with their actual values.

Produced by: detect_outliers

Quality Report¶

Scans a table for data integrity problems: null rates per column, type inconsistencies, duplicate primary keys, and referential integrity violations.

Produced by: check_quality

Text Analysis Block¶

For string columns: character length distribution, top token frequency, detected pattern types (email, phone, URL, etc.), and example values.

Produced by: analyze_text_column

Cardinality Block¶

Shows the distinct value count and entropy of a column — useful for understanding how "unique" text or categorical columns are.

Produced by: compute_cardinality

Duplicate Report¶

Lists groups of rows that are exact or near-duplicate matches with their duplicate counts.

Produced by: detect_duplicates

Regression Block¶

Linear regression output: feature coefficients with standard errors, p-values, R² score, and a significance assessment per feature.

Produced by: compute_regression

Risk Table¶

Scores each row against a configurable risk model. Rows are bucketed into risk tiers with a score and the factors that drove it.

Produced by: classify_risk

Table Comparison Block¶

Side-by-side diff between two tables: schema differences (column adds/removes/type changes) and row-level count and value differences.

Produced by: compare_tables

Pareto Chart¶

Shows which subset of items (typically 20%) drives the majority of a metric (typically 80%). Combines a bar chart for individual contribution with a cumulative line.

Produced by: compute_pareto

Artifact Interactions¶

Every artifact — chart, table, or stat block — has a consistent set of actions in its toolbar.

Expand to Full Panel¶

Click the expand icon to open the artifact in a full-screen overlay. This gives you more room for detailed chart inspection, scrolling large tables, or reading a long report. The expanded view has the same export and action options.

Pin to Dashboard¶

Click Pin to add the artifact to your Dashboard. The Dashboard is a persistent, grid-based layout where you collect your most important charts and metrics. Pinned artifacts stay on the dashboard across sessions and chat resets.

Access the Dashboard at any time with the D shortcut key or the dashboard icon in the toolbar.

View Underlying SQL¶

Every data-driven artifact (chart, table, stat block) has a View SQL button. This shows the exact SQL query Resona executed to produce the result. You can:

  • Copy the SQL to use in the SQL Editor
  • Understand exactly what data was fetched and how it was aggregated
  • Verify the logic before sharing the result with stakeholders

Export from Artifact¶

Individual artifacts can be exported directly:

  • Charts → Download as PNG image
  • Tables → Download as CSV or Excel
  • Reports → Save to Workspace as PDF or PowerPoint (see Workspace & Projects)

Artifact Placeholder (Streaming)¶

When Resona is still computing a result, a skeleton placeholder appears in the chat in the artifact's final position. This signals that a result is incoming so the layout doesn't jump when the data arrives. Charts and tables stream their data progressively — you often see rows or data points appear as they are computed.
data-explorer/ai-artifacts.raw:
---
title: Charts & Analysis Results
description: Understand the artifact types Resona AI generates — charts, stat blocks, and data tables — and how to interact with them
tags: [data-explorer, resona-ai, artifacts, charts, visualizations, stat-blocks]
order: 6
sidebar_group: "Data Explorer"
related:
  - title: Resona AI
    path: /data-explorer/ai-overview
  - title: How Resona AI Works
    path: /data-explorer/agentic-workflows
  - title: Workspace & Projects
    path: /data-explorer/ai-workspace
---

# Charts & Analysis Results

When Resona AI finishes an analysis, it outputs **artifacts** — rich interactive components embedded directly in the chat. These are not static images. You can expand them, sort them, pin them to a dashboard, and export their underlying SQL.

> Quick links: [Open Data Explorer](https://data.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [AI Chat Interface](/data-explorer/ai-chat-interface) · [Workspace & Projects](/data-explorer/ai-workspace)

![Resona AI chat response with an expanded interactive data table artifact](/images/data-explorer/resoan-ai-chat-expand-table.png)

---

## Data Tables

When Resona runs a SQL query or returns row-level results, it renders them as an interactive data table.

- **Smart formatting** — dates, numbers, and null values are formatted automatically for readability
- **Sortable columns** — click any column header to sort ascending or descending
- **Inline filtering** — type to filter visible rows without re-running the query
- **Export** — download the table as CSV or Excel directly from the artifact toolbar

Data tables are produced by the `run_query`, `sample_rows`, `search_rows`, `compute_top_n`, and `compute_ranking` tools.

![A data table artifact in the chat showing the export button and table data](/images/data-explorer/chat-tables-export.png)

---

## Charts

Resona generates interactive charts using Recharts. The chart type is chosen automatically based on the analysis being performed.

### Bar Chart

Used for comparisons — aggregations, top-N rankings, period-over-period comparisons, segment breakdowns.

Produced by: `compute_aggregation`, `compute_top_n`, `compare_periods`, `compare_segments`, `compute_distribution`

### Line Chart

Used for trends over time — revenue over months, user signups over weeks, moving averages.

Produced by: `compute_trend`, `compute_moving_average`, `forecast_trend`

### Area Chart

Line chart with a filled area — used for cumulative metrics or stacked time series.

### Pie / Donut Chart

Used for proportional breakdowns where parts must sum to a whole.

### Scatter Chart

Used for correlation analysis — plots two numeric columns against each other to show relationships.

Produced by: `compute_correlation` (individual pairs)

### Heatmap

Used for correlation matrices and cohort / retention grids. Cell color intensity represents the value magnitude.

Produced by: `compute_correlation`, `compute_cohort`, `compute_retention`

### Funnel Chart

Used for conversion analysis — each stage shows absolute count and drop-off percentage to the next stage.

Produced by: `compute_funnel`

### Forecast Chart

A line chart extended with a projected future segment and a confidence interval band.

Produced by: `forecast_trend`

![Resona AI chat showing a chart visualization artifact in the response](/images/data-explorer/resona-ai.png)

---

## Stat Blocks

Stat blocks are structured analytical outputs for computations that don't fit a standard chart. Each type has its own layout.

### Statistics Block

Summary statistics for one or more numeric columns: mean, median, standard deviation, min, max, 25th/75th/95th percentiles, skewness, kurtosis.

Produced by: `compute_statistics`

### Distribution Block

Frequency histogram showing how values are spread across value buckets.

Produced by: `compute_distribution`

### Seasonality Block

Identifies repeating patterns in time series data. Shows the dominant period (e.g., weekly, monthly), the strength of the pattern, and a visual of the cycle.

Produced by: `detect_seasonality`

### Anomaly Block

Highlights rows or values that deviate significantly from the expected distribution. Each anomaly entry shows the column, the anomalous value, and the expected range.

Produced by: `detect_anomalies`

### Outlier Block

Extreme value detection using IQR and z-score. Separates high and low outliers with their actual values.

Produced by: `detect_outliers`

### Quality Report

Scans a table for data integrity problems: null rates per column, type inconsistencies, duplicate primary keys, and referential integrity violations.

Produced by: `check_quality`

### Text Analysis Block

For string columns: character length distribution, top token frequency, detected pattern types (email, phone, URL, etc.), and example values.

Produced by: `analyze_text_column`

### Cardinality Block

Shows the distinct value count and entropy of a column — useful for understanding how "unique" text or categorical columns are.

Produced by: `compute_cardinality`

### Duplicate Report

Lists groups of rows that are exact or near-duplicate matches with their duplicate counts.

Produced by: `detect_duplicates`

### Regression Block

Linear regression output: feature coefficients with standard errors, p-values, R² score, and a significance assessment per feature.

Produced by: `compute_regression`

### Risk Table

Scores each row against a configurable risk model. Rows are bucketed into risk tiers with a score and the factors that drove it.

Produced by: `classify_risk`

### Table Comparison Block

Side-by-side diff between two tables: schema differences (column adds/removes/type changes) and row-level count and value differences.

Produced by: `compare_tables`

### Pareto Chart

Shows which subset of items (typically 20%) drives the majority of a metric (typically 80%). Combines a bar chart for individual contribution with a cumulative line.

Produced by: `compute_pareto`

![Column statistics panel showing null percentage, distinct count, min/max, and top values — the same data Resona uses for stat blocks](/images/data-explorer/columns-stats.png)

---

## Artifact Interactions

Every artifact — chart, table, or stat block — has a consistent set of actions in its toolbar.

### Expand to Full Panel

Click the **expand icon** to open the artifact in a full-screen overlay. This gives you more room for detailed chart inspection, scrolling large tables, or reading a long report. The expanded view has the same export and action options.

### Pin to Dashboard

Click **Pin** to add the artifact to your Dashboard. The Dashboard is a persistent, grid-based layout where you collect your most important charts and metrics. Pinned artifacts stay on the dashboard across sessions and chat resets.

Access the Dashboard at any time with the `D` shortcut key or the dashboard icon in the toolbar.

![Resona AI interface — pin any chart or table to the Dashboard for persistent access across sessions](/images/data-explorer/resona-ai-@mention-table-overview.png)

### View Underlying SQL

Every data-driven artifact (chart, table, stat block) has a **View SQL** button. This shows the exact SQL query Resona executed to produce the result. You can:

- Copy the SQL to use in the SQL Editor
- Understand exactly what data was fetched and how it was aggregated
- Verify the logic before sharing the result with stakeholders

### Export from Artifact

Individual artifacts can be exported directly:

- **Charts** → Download as PNG image
- **Tables** → Download as CSV or Excel
- **Reports** → Save to Workspace as PDF or PowerPoint (see [Workspace & Projects](/data-explorer/ai-workspace))

---

## Artifact Placeholder (Streaming)

When Resona is still computing a result, a **skeleton placeholder** appears in the chat in the artifact's final position. This signals that a result is incoming so the layout doesn't jump when the data arrives. Charts and tables stream their data progressively — you often see rows or data points appear as they are computed.

data-explorer/ai-chat-interface.content:
AI Chat Interface¶

Resona AI is the agentic assistant built directly into the Data Explorer. You interact with it through a chat panel — type a question in plain English, and Resona queries your database, runs analysis tools, and returns results as interactive charts, tables, and reports.

Quick links: Open Data Explorer · Data Explorer Overview · How Resona AI Works

Opening the AI Panel¶

Click the Resona AI button in the Data Explorer sidebar to open the chat panel. The panel slides in as a right-side drawer alongside the data grid. You can keep the grid and AI panel visible at the same time.

The panel has three sections accessible from the sidebar icons:

Icon Panel Purpose
Chat bubble Chat Active conversation with Resona
Clock History All previous chats and their artifacts
Folder Projects Organize chats into analysis projects
Library Library Saved charts and connection health

Table Mentions (@)¶

The fastest way to give Resona accurate context is to mention a table in your message.

Type @ and a search dropdown appears showing all tables in your connected database. Select a table to attach it to your message. Resona receives the table's full schema, foreign key relationships, and a sample of its data before it starts planning.

@orders show me total revenue by month for the last year
@users @orders what is the 30-day retention rate for users who signed up in Q1?

Why this matters: Without a mention, Resona has to first call list_tables and get_schema to discover your data. With a mention it skips discovery and starts analysis immediately — faster and more accurate SQL on the first attempt.

You can mention multiple tables in one message. Resona will use all of them as context.

Context Window¶

Every model has a finite memory (context window). Resona tracks how much of it is currently in use via the Context Window Bar — a thin progress indicator at the top of the chat input.

  • Green / low usage: Plenty of room. Resona can handle long multi-step analysis.
  • Yellow / mid usage: Getting full. Resona may start compacting older tool results.
  • Red / near limit: Resona will auto-compact the conversation before the next request.

Auto-Compaction¶

When the conversation grows long, Resona automatically summarizes previous turns into a compact memory block. Your message history stays visible in the UI, but Resona's internal context is trimmed. Analysis continuity is preserved — it remembers what tables you were working on and what findings it already made.

You can also manually Reset the context at any time. This wipes Resona's working memory and starts a fresh session. Use this when switching to a completely unrelated analysis topic.

Voice Input¶

If your browser has microphone access, the microphone icon in the chat input lets you dictate your question. Speech is transcribed in real time and inserted into the text field. You can edit the transcription before sending.

This is useful for long, conversational questions where typing is slower than speaking.

Projects¶

Projects let you group related chat sessions together. A project has its own:

  • Dedicated workspace (files and reports generated in that project stay together)
  • Artifact history scoped to that project
  • Visual label (color + icon) you assign when creating it

Create a project for each ongoing analysis topic — for example "Monthly Sales Review", "User Churn Investigation", "Infrastructure Audit". Switch between projects from the sidebar without losing any conversation history.

Chat History¶

Every conversation is saved automatically. The History panel lists all previous chats, newest first, with a preview of the first message. Click any past chat to reopen it and continue where you left off, or to revisit artifacts that were generated.

Deleting a chat removes its message history from the list, but its generated artifacts remain available in the Artifact History panel.

Keyboard Shortcuts¶

Shortcut Action
Enter Send message
Shift + Enter New line in message
@ Open table mention picker
Esc Close mention picker
D Open Dashboard (pinned artifacts)
W Open Workspace (generated files)
