---

## Context Window

Every model has a finite memory (context window). Resona tracks how much of it is currently in use via the **Context Window Bar** — a thin progress indicator at the top of the chat input.

---

## Voice Input

If your browser has microphone access, the **microphone icon** in the chat input lets you dictate your question. Speech is transcribed in real time and inserted into the text field. You can edit the transcription before sending.

This is useful for long, conversational questions where typing is slower than speaking.

---

## Projects

Projects let you group related chat sessions together. A project has its own:

- Dedicated workspace (files and reports generated in that project stay together)
- Artifact history scoped to that project
- Visual label (color + icon) you assign when creating it

Create a project for each ongoing analysis topic — for example "Monthly Sales Review", "User Churn Investigation", "Infrastructure Audit". Switch between projects from the sidebar without losing any conversation history.

![The Projects panel showing analysis projects organized with color labels](/images/data-explorer/project-resona-ai.png)

---

## Chat History

Every conversation is saved automatically. The **History** panel lists all previous chats, newest first, with a preview of the first message. Click any past chat to reopen it and continue where you left off, or to revisit artifacts that were generated.

Deleting a chat removes its message history from the list, but its generated artifacts remain available in the Artifact History panel.

---

## Keyboard Shortcuts

| Shortcut | Action |
|---|---|
| `Enter` | Send message |
| `Shift + Enter` | New line in message |
| `@` | Open table mention picker |
| `Esc` | Close mention picker |
| `D` | Open Dashboard (pinned artifacts) |
| `W` | Open Workspace (generated files) |

data-explorer/ai-overview.content:
Resona AI¶

Resona AI is the agentic data assistant built into the Data Explorer. You describe what you want to know in plain English — Resona figures out which tables to query, runs the analysis, and returns the results as interactive charts, tables, and reports.

Quick links: Open Data Explorer · Data Explorer Overview · SQL Editor

What can Resona do inside Data Explorer?¶

Explore and query your data¶

Ask questions without writing SQL. Resona automatically discovers your schema, constructs the right queries, and returns clean results.

"Show me the top 10 customers by revenue this quarter" "How many orders were placed each day last month?" "@products list all items with fewer than 5 units in stock"

Analyse trends and patterns¶

Resona has dedicated tools for time series, distributions, correlations, and anomaly detection. It picks the right analysis automatically based on what you ask.

"Is there a seasonal pattern in our signups?" "Which columns in @events correlate most with conversion?" "Flag any unusual spikes in the @transactions table"

Segment and compare¶

Break down data by cohorts, segments, or time periods. Compare behaviour between groups or track retention over time.

"What's the 30-day retention for users who signed up in Q1 vs Q2?" "Compare average order value between mobile and desktop customers" "Show a funnel from signup → activation → first purchase"

Generate reports and presentations¶

Resona can produce a full analysis report and export it as a PowerPoint deck or PDF, ready to share with stakeholders — without leaving the Data Explorer.

"Summarise the key findings from today's analysis as a PDF" "Create a PowerPoint with the revenue trend and top product charts"

How do you access Resona AI?¶

Click the Resona AI button in the top-right of the Data Explorer, or the AI icon in the table sidebar. The AI panel opens alongside your data grid — both stay visible at the same time.

Which pages should you read next?¶

Page What you'll learn
AI Chat Interface How to use @mentions, voice input, context management, and chat history
How Resona AI Works The agentic loop — Planning, Tool Execution, Synthesis — and the full tool library
Charts & Analysis Results Every artifact type Resona can produce and how to interact with them
Workspace & Projects Manage exported reports, organise sessions into projects, browse artifact history

> Quick links: [Open Data Explorer](https://data.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [Data Explorer Overview](https://docs.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/data-explorer/) · [SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/)

## What can Resona do inside Data Explorer?

### Explore and query your data

Ask questions without writing SQL. Resona automatically discovers your schema, constructs the right queries, and returns clean results.

> "Show me the top 10 customers by revenue this quarter"
> "How many orders were placed each day last month?"
> "@products list all items with fewer than 5 units in stock"

### Analyse trends and patterns

Resona has dedicated tools for time series, distributions, correlations, and anomaly detection. It picks the right analysis automatically based on what you ask.

> "Is there a seasonal pattern in our signups?"
> "Which columns in @events correlate most with conversion?"
> "Flag any unusual spikes in the @transactions table"

### Segment and compare

Break down data by cohorts, segments, or time periods. Compare behaviour between groups or track retention over time.

> "What's the 30-day retention for users who signed up in Q1 vs Q2?"
> "Compare average order value between mobile and desktop customers"
> "Show a funnel from signup → activation → first purchase"

### Generate reports and presentations

Resona can produce a full analysis report and export it as a PowerPoint deck or PDF, ready to share with stakeholders — without leaving the Data Explorer.

frontmatter.title: Workspace & Projects
frontmatter.description: Manage generated reports, organize analysis into projects, and browse your full artifact history in the Data Explorer

data-explorer/ai-workspace.content:
Workspace & Projects¶

Resona AI stores everything it generates. The Workspace holds exported files, Projects organize your analysis sessions, and Artifact History gives you a searchable archive of every chart and result ever produced.

Quick links: Open Data Explorer · AI Chat Interface · Data Explorer Overview

Workspace¶

The Workspace is a persistent file store for reports and exports generated by Resona. It is accessible from the W shortcut or the workspace icon in the AI panel toolbar.

File Types¶

Resona generates the following file types and saves them to the Workspace automatically:

Format How it's generated Best for
PowerPoint (.pptx) generate_ppt tool Stakeholder presentations, executive summaries
PDF generate_pdf tool Printed reports, email attachments
HTML Exported interactive dashboards Browser-based sharing, embedded reports
Markdown generate_summary_report tool Developer-readable summaries, internal wikis

Managing Files¶

Each file in the Workspace can be:

  • Downloaded — save to your local machine
  • Renamed — give it a meaningful name for future reference
  • Deleted — permanently removes the file
  • Versioned — if Resona generates an updated version of a report in the same session, both versions are kept

Files are scoped to the Project that was active when they were created. If you are not using Projects, all files go into the default workspace.

Projects¶

Projects let you group related chat sessions and their outputs into a named workspace. When you are investigating a specific question — "Why did revenue drop in March?" or "Analyse user activation rates" — you create a Project for that investigation.

What a Project Contains¶

  • All chat sessions you assigned to it
  • All Workspace files generated during those sessions
  • All artifacts from those sessions (accessible in Artifact History filtered to that project)
  • A user-defined color and icon for quick visual identification

Creating and Switching Projects¶

Open the Projects panel from the sidebar folder icon. Click New Project and give it a name. To assign a chat to a project, select the project before or after starting the chat.

You can move a chat to a different project at any time from the chat options menu.

Artifact History¶

Every artifact Resona has ever generated — across all sessions and projects — is stored in the Artifact History. This includes charts, tables, stat blocks, reports, and files.

Searching and Filtering¶

The Artifact History panel has a search bar and filter controls:

  • Search — full-text search across artifact titles and the questions that generated them
  • Filter by type — show only Charts, Tables, Stat Blocks, Reports, or Files
  • Filter by date — narrow to a specific time range
  • Filter by project — show only artifacts from a specific project

Reusing Artifacts¶

From Artifact History you can:

  • Open — re-display the artifact in its full interactive view
  • Copy SQL — grab the underlying query that generated a data artifact
  • Navigate to chat — jump back to the original conversation that produced this artifact for more context
  • Pin to Dashboard — add a historical chart to the live Dashboard without re-running the analysis

This is particularly useful when you remember a useful analysis from weeks ago but can't recall which chat it was in.

Library¶

The Library is a separate panel for assets you explicitly save for long-term reuse, plus a health overview of your database connections.

Saved Charts¶

Any chart or stat block can be saved to the Library by clicking the bookmark icon on the artifact. Saved charts are displayed as a gallery for quick access — these are the metrics and visualizations you check regularly.

Connection Health¶

The Library shows the current status of all your connected databases:

  • Healthy — connection is live and responding
  • Unhealthy — connection failed the last health check (timeout, auth failure, etc.)
  • Unknown — not yet checked since the last backend restart

Each connection entry also shows the database size, total table count, and the last time it was used for an analysis.

# Workspace & Projects

Resona AI stores everything it generates. The **Workspace** holds exported files, **Projects** organize your analysis sessions, and **Artifact History** gives you a searchable archive of every chart and result ever produced.

> Quick links: [Open Data Explorer](https://data.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [AI Chat Interface](/data-explorer/ai-chat-interface) · [Data Explorer Overview](https://docs.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/data-explorer/)

![Resona AI interface showing the workspace and project organization panel](/images/data-explorer/resona-ai.png)

---

## Workspace

The Workspace is a persistent file store for reports and exports generated by Resona. It is accessible from the `W` shortcut or the workspace icon in the AI panel toolbar.

### File Types

Resona generates the following file types and saves them to the Workspace automatically:

| Format | How it's generated | Best for |
|---|---|---|
| **PowerPoint (.pptx)** | `generate_ppt` tool | Stakeholder presentations, executive summaries |
| **PDF** | `generate_pdf` tool | Printed reports, email attachments |
| **HTML** | Exported interactive dashboards | Browser-based sharing, embedded reports |
| **Markdown** | `generate_summary_report` tool | Developer-readable summaries, internal wikis |

### Managing Files

Each file in the Workspace can be:

- **Downloaded** — save to your local machine
- **Renamed** — give it a meaningful name for future reference
- **Deleted** — permanently removes the file
- **Versioned** — if Resona generates an updated version of a report in the same session, both versions are kept

Files are scoped to the **Project** that was active when they were created. If you are not using Projects, all files go into the default workspace.

![Resona AI chat with table export — the same export options are available for workspace files](/images/data-explorer/chat-tables-export.png)

---

## Projects

Projects let you group related chat sessions and their outputs into a named workspace. When you are investigating a specific question — "Why did revenue drop in March?" or "Analyse user activation rates" — you create a Project for that investigation.

### What a Project Contains

- All chat sessions you assigned to it
- All Workspace files generated during those sessions
- All artifacts from those sessions (accessible in Artifact History filtered to that project)
- A user-defined color and icon for quick visual identification

### Creating and Switching Projects

Open the **Projects** panel from the sidebar folder icon. Click **New Project** and give it a name. To assign a chat to a project, select the project before or after starting the chat.

You can move a chat to a different project at any time from the chat options menu.

![The Projects panel in Resona AI showing analysis projects organized with color labels](/images/data-explorer/project-resona-ai.png)

---

## Artifact History

Every artifact Resona has ever generated — across all sessions and projects — is stored in the **Artifact History**. This includes charts, tables, stat blocks, reports, and files.

### Searching and Filtering

The Artifact History panel has a search bar and filter controls:

- **Search** — full-text search across artifact titles and the questions that generated them
- **Filter by type** — show only Charts, Tables, Stat Blocks, Reports, or Files
- **Filter by date** — narrow to a specific time range
- **Filter by project** — show only artifacts from a specific project

### Reusing Artifacts

From Artifact History you can:

- **Open** — re-display the artifact in its full interactive view
- **Copy SQL** — grab the underlying query that generated a data artifact
- **Navigate to chat** — jump back to the original conversation that produced this artifact for more context
- **Pin to Dashboard** — add a historical chart to the live Dashboard without re-running the analysis

This is particularly useful when you remember a useful analysis from weeks ago but can't recall which chat it was in.

![Artifact History panel showing a searchable archive of all generated charts and results](/images/data-explorer/artifact-history-dark-theme.png)

---

## Library

The **Library** is a separate panel for assets you explicitly save for long-term reuse, plus a health overview of your database connections.

### Saved Charts

Any chart or stat block can be saved to the Library by clicking the bookmark icon on the artifact. Saved charts are displayed as a gallery for quick access — these are the metrics and visualizations you check regularly.

### Connection Health

The Library shows the current status of all your connected databases:

- **Healthy** — connection is live and responding
- **Unhealthy** — connection failed the last health check (timeout, auth failure, etc.)
- **Unknown** — not yet checked since the last backend restart

Each connection entry also shows the database size, total table count, and the last time it was used for an analysis.

![Resona AI showing saved artifacts and connection health across your data sources](/images/data-explorer/resona-ai-@mention-table-overview.png)

frontmatter.title: Exploring Your Data
frontmatter.description: Browse tables, inspect columns, filter rows, and analyze column statistics in the Data Explorer grid

data-explorer/exploring-data.content:
Exploring Your Data¶

Once you've selected a PostgreSQL connection, the interface gives you a two-panel layout for browsing live data — a sidebar of tables on the left and a high-performance data grid on the right. Connections are created in the SQL Editor and are available here automatically — no separate setup needed.

Quick links: Open Data Explorer · Data Explorer Overview · SQL Editor

Interface Layout¶

Region Purpose
Left sidebar Schema selector, table search, table list with row estimates
Right panel Action bar (filter, columns, refresh), data grid, pagination
Column Stats Slide-over panel that appears on the right when you click a column header

Left Sidebar¶

Schema Selector¶

At the top of the sidebar is a schema dropdown that lists every schema in the connected database. Changing the schema reloads the table list and clears the current table selection.

  • Defaults to public for PostgreSQL
  • All schemas visible to the connected user are listed
  • Switching schemas fetches a fresh table list from the server

Table List¶

Below the schema selector, you'll see a searchable list of tables.

Each table row shows:

Element Description
Table icon Standard table indicator
Name Table name (truncated if long)
⚠️ Warning Shown if the table has no primary key
Row estimate Approximate row count (47K, 3.2M, etc.)
AI icon Hover any table to reveal the Resona AI icon — click it to ask AI about that specific table

Search & Filter

Type in the search box at the top to instantly filter the table list. The count bar updates to show how many tables match (e.g., 3 of 24 tables).

Table Selection

Click any table name to load its data in the right-side grid. The selected table is highlighted with a primary-colored left border and highlighted background.

Collapsible Sidebar¶

Click the toggle button in the sidebar header to collapse it to a narrow icon strip (48px), giving the data grid maximum width. Click again to expand.

Data Grid¶

The data grid is the heart of the Data Explorer. It renders your table's rows in a high-performance, scrollable table with fixed column headers.

Column Headers¶

Each column header displays:

Element Description
🔑 Key icon Gold key icon for primary key columns
🔗 Link icon Blue link icon for foreign key columns
Column name Bold for PKs, blue-tinted for FKs
Type badge Monospace type label (e.g., int4, varchar?) — the ? indicates nullable
FK reference On hover, shows → referenced_table for foreign keys
Sort button Click the sort arrows to cycle through ascending → descending → unsorted

Sorting¶

Click the sort icon on any column header to sort:

  1. First click — Sort ascending (↑)
  2. Second click — Sort descending (↓)
  3. Third click — Remove sort (return to default order)

Sorting resets pagination back to page 1. The active sort column is highlighted.

Sort operations are performed server-side, so they work efficiently on tables with millions of rows.

Smart Column Sizing¶

Column widths are automatically calculated to fit your data using a production-grade sizing engine.

How It Works¶

  1. Canvas-Based Measurement — Column widths are calculated using the browser's CanvasRenderingContext2D API with the actual font face and size (13px Inter), giving pixel-perfect proportional measurements
  2. 95th Percentile Sampling — Up to 50 rows are sampled per column, and the 95th percentile width is used. This prevents one abnormally long value from stretching the column far beyond what's useful
  3. Type-Aware Constraints — Different data types have different width ranges:
Column Type Min Width Max Width
Integer / Numeric / Boolean Header width 160px
Timestamp / Date 180px —
UUID 270px —
Text / Varchar 80px 500px

Manual Resizing¶

  • Drag the right border of any column header to resize
  • Double-click the border to auto-fit (reset to calculated width)
  • Manual widths are persisted to localStorage and restored when you return

Persistence & Storage¶

Manual column widths are saved per-connection, per-schema, per-table. The storage layer features:

  • Debounced Saves — Disk writes are debounced at 400ms during drag resize to prevent UI jank
  • LRU Cache — A maximum of 50 saved table configurations are kept. If the limit is exceeded, the least recently used entry is pruned
  • Adaptive Lock Bypass — If new data requires more than 2.5× the saved width to be readable, the saved width is automatically discarded in favor of the auto-calculated size

Filtering Data¶

The filter system lets you narrow down rows using typed conditions.

Adding a Filter¶

  1. Click the Filter button in the action bar
  2. Choose a column from the dropdown
  3. Choose an operator
  4. Enter a value (press Enter or click "Add Filter")

Available Operators¶

Operators adapt based on column type:

Column Type Available Operators
Text =, ≠, ~ Contains
Number =, ≠, >, ≥, <, ≤
Boolean =, ≠
Date / Timestamp =, ≠, >, ≥, <, ≤
All types IN (list), IS NULL, NOT NULL

Filter Chips¶

Active filters appear as tagged chips in the action bar:

[ Filter (2) ] | [ status = active ✕ ] [ role = admin ✕ ] Clear
  • Click the ✕ on any chip to remove that filter
  • Click Clear to remove all filters at once
  • Filter changes reset pagination to page 1

Filters are applied server-side, ensuring accurate results even on large tables.

Toggle Column Visibility¶

Click the Columns button in the action bar to show/hide individual columns:

  • Each column shows an eye icon (visible) or crossed-out eye (hidden)
  • Toggle any column to hide it from the grid
  • The button label updates to show the visible count (e.g., Columns (8/12))
  • Click Show all columns at the bottom to reset

Hidden columns are session-only — they reset when you navigate away.

Refresh Data¶

Click the ↻ refresh button in the action bar to re-fetch the current page of data from the database. The icon animates while the request is in progress.

This is useful when:

  • Another process has modified the data
  • You want to verify that a migration or import succeeded
  • You're monitoring live data changes

Click to Copy¶

Click on any cell in the data grid to copy its value to the clipboard. A brief green "Copied" badge appears to confirm. Special values are handled:

Cell Value Copied As
NULL The string NULL
Boolean true or false
JSON object Pretty-printed JSON string
Date ISO 8601 string
Any other value Plain string

Column Statistics Panel¶

Click a column header (not the sort icon) to open the Column Stats Panel — a slide-over sidebar that shows live statistics for that column.

Statistics Displayed¶

Stat Description
Type Full data type with nullable indicator
Flags Primary Key, Indexed badges
Null % Percentage of null values
Distinct Count Number of unique values
Min / Max Minimum and maximum values (for numbers and dates)
Data Completeness Visual progress bar showing non-null percentage

Top Values¶

For columns with repeated values, the panel shows the most frequent values with percentage distribution bars. This is useful for:

  • Understanding enum-like columns
  • Spotting data skew
  • Identifying the most common categories

Sample Preview¶

A set of sample values is displayed as small tokens, giving you a quick look at what kind of data the column contains without scrolling through the grid.

Close the panel by clicking the X button or clicking the same column header again.

Pagination¶

The bottom bar shows cursor-based pagination:

1–50 of ~47.0K                                    Page 2   ◀ Prev  Next ▶
Element Description
Row range Current visible range (e.g., 1–50)
Estimated total Approximate total row count
Page indicator Shown when not on page 1
Prev / Next Navigate between pages (50 rows per page)

Pagination is cursor-based rather than offset-based, which means:

  • Forward pagination is efficient even on tables with millions of rows
  • The page counter is approximate (indicated by the ~ prefix)

Row Number Column¶

The leftmost # column displays sequential row numbers relative to the current page. Row numbers automatically account for pagination offset — page 2 starts at row 51.

Cell Formatting¶

Different data types are styled for readability:

Data Type Display
NULL Italic gray "NULL"
true / false Colored pill badges (green/red)
Foreign key values Small arrow indicator (→) on hover
JSON objects Serialized to string with truncation
Long text Truncated with ellipsis — click to copy the full value

Empty States¶

The grid handles edge cases gracefully:

State What You See
No connection selected "No connection selected" with guidance to choose one
No table selected "Select a table" with arrow pointing to sidebar
Table has no rows "This table is empty"
Filters return no results "No rows match your filters" with a clear link
Data load failure Error message with a Retry button

Keyboard Shortcuts¶

Action Shortcut
Copy cell value Click on any cell
Sort column Click sort icon in header
Auto-fit column width Double-click column border

Next Steps¶

  • Exporting Data — download tables in CSV, JSON, Excel, SQL, and more
  • AI Chat Interface — use natural language prompts, @mentions, and chat history inside Data Explorer
  • Open Data Explorer — jump back into the live app
data-explorer/exploring-data.raw:
---
title: Exploring Your Data
description: Browse tables, inspect columns, filter rows, and analyze column statistics in the Data Explorer grid
tags: [data-explorer, grid, columns, filters, statistics]
order: 1
sidebar_group: "Data Explorer"
related:
  - title: Data Explorer
    path: /data-explorer
  - title: Exporting Data
    path: /data-explorer/exporting-data
  - title: AI Chat Interface
    path: /data-explorer/ai-chat-interface
---

# Exploring Your Data

Once you've selected a PostgreSQL connection, the interface gives you a two-panel layout for browsing live data — a sidebar of tables on the left and a high-performance data grid on the right. Connections are created in the [SQL Editor](/database/adding-a-connection) and are available here automatically — no separate setup needed.

> Quick links: [Open Data Explorer](https://data.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [Data Explorer Overview](https://docs.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/data-explorer/) · [SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/)

---

## Interface Layout

![The Data Explorer showing the two-column layout with left sidebar and main data grid](/images/data-explorer/data-explorer.png)

| Region | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| **Left sidebar** | Schema selector, table search, table list with row estimates |
| **Right panel** | Action bar (filter, columns, refresh), data grid, pagination |
| **Column Stats** | Slide-over panel that appears on the right when you click a column header |

---

## Left Sidebar

![The left sidebar showing the schema selector, table search, and table list with row counts](/images/data-explorer/left-sidebar.png)

### Schema Selector

At the top of the sidebar is a **schema dropdown** that lists every schema in the connected database. Changing the schema reloads the table list and clears the current table selection.

- Defaults to `public` for PostgreSQL
- All schemas visible to the connected user are listed
- Switching schemas fetches a fresh table list from the server

### Table List

Below the schema selector, you'll see a searchable list of tables.

**Each table row shows:**

| Element | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| Table icon | Standard table indicator |
| Name | Table name (truncated if long) |
| ⚠️ Warning | Shown if the table has **no primary key** |
| Row estimate | Approximate row count (`47K`, `3.2M`, etc.) |
| AI icon | Hover any table to reveal the Resona AI icon — click it to ask AI about that specific table |

**Search & Filter**

Type in the search box at the top to instantly filter the table list. The count bar updates to show how many tables match (e.g., `3 of 24 tables`).

**Table Selection**

Click any table name to load its data in the right-side grid. The selected table is highlighted with a primary-colored left border and highlighted background.

### Collapsible Sidebar

Click the toggle button in the sidebar header to collapse it to a narrow icon strip (48px), giving the data grid maximum width. Click again to expand.

---

## Data Grid

The data grid is the heart of the Data Explorer. It renders your table's rows in a high-performance, scrollable table with fixed column headers.

### Column Headers

Each column header displays:

| Element | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| 🔑 Key icon | Gold key icon for primary key columns |
| 🔗 Link icon | Blue link icon for foreign key columns |
| Column name | Bold for PKs, blue-tinted for FKs |
| Type badge | Monospace type label (e.g., `int4`, `varchar?`) — the `?` indicates nullable |
| FK reference | On hover, shows `→ referenced_table` for foreign keys |
| Sort button | Click the sort arrows to cycle through ascending → descending → unsorted |

### Sorting

Click the sort icon on any column header to sort:

1. **First click** — Sort ascending (↑)
2. **Second click** — Sort descending (↓)
3. **Third click** — Remove sort (return to default order)

Sorting resets pagination back to page 1. The active sort column is highlighted.

> Sort operations are performed server-side, so they work efficiently on tables with millions of rows.

---

## Smart Column Sizing

Column widths are automatically calculated to fit your data using a production-grade sizing engine.

### How It Works

1. **Canvas-Based Measurement** — Column widths are calculated using the browser's `CanvasRenderingContext2D` API with the actual font face and size (`13px Inter`), giving pixel-perfect proportional measurements
2. **95th Percentile Sampling** — Up to 50 rows are sampled per column, and the **95th percentile** width is used. This prevents one abnormally long value from stretching the column far beyond what's useful
3. **Type-Aware Constraints** — Different data types have different width ranges:

| Column Type | Min Width | Max Width |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| Integer / Numeric / Boolean | Header width | 160px |
| Timestamp / Date | 180px | — |
| UUID | 270px | — |
| Text / Varchar | 80px | 500px |

### Manual Resizing

- **Drag** the right border of any column header to resize
- **Double-click** the border to **auto-fit** (reset to calculated width)
- Manual widths are **persisted** to `localStorage` and restored when you return

### Persistence & Storage

Manual column widths are saved per-connection, per-schema, per-table. The storage layer features:

- **Debounced Saves** — Disk writes are debounced at 400ms during drag resize to prevent UI jank
- **LRU Cache** — A maximum of 50 saved table configurations are kept. If the limit is exceeded, the least recently used entry is pruned
- **Adaptive Lock Bypass** — If new data requires more than 2.5× the saved width to be readable, the saved width is automatically discarded in favor of the auto-calculated size

---

## Filtering Data

![The action bar showing the Filter and Columns controls above the data grid](/images/data-explorer/action-bar-filter-colums.png)

The filter system lets you narrow down rows using typed conditions.

### Adding a Filter

1. Click the **Filter** button in the action bar
2. Choose a **column** from the dropdown
3. Choose an **operator**
4. Enter a **value** (press Enter or click "Add Filter")

### Available Operators

Operators adapt based on column type:

| Column Type | Available Operators |
|-------------|-------------------|
| Text | `=`, `≠`, `~ Contains` |
| Number | `=`, `≠`, `>`, `≥`, `<`, `≤` |
| Boolean | `=`, `≠` |
| Date / Timestamp | `=`, `≠`, `>`, `≥`, `<`, `≤` |
| All types | `IN (list)`, `IS NULL`, `NOT NULL` |

### Filter Chips

Active filters appear as tagged chips in the action bar:

```
[ Filter (2) ] | [ status = active ✕ ] [ role = admin ✕ ] Clear
```

- Click the **✕** on any chip to remove that filter
- Click **Clear** to remove all filters at once
- Filter changes reset pagination to page 1

> Filters are applied server-side, ensuring accurate results even on large tables.

---

## Toggle Column Visibility

Click the **Columns** button in the action bar to show/hide individual columns:

- Each column shows an eye icon (visible) or crossed-out eye (hidden)
- Toggle any column to hide it from the grid
- The button label updates to show the visible count (e.g., `Columns (8/12)`)
- Click **Show all columns** at the bottom to reset

Hidden columns are session-only — they reset when you navigate away.

---

## Refresh Data

Click the **↻ refresh** button in the action bar to re-fetch the current page of data from the database. The icon animates while the request is in progress.

This is useful when:
- Another process has modified the data
- You want to verify that a migration or import succeeded
- You're monitoring live data changes

---

## Click to Copy

Click on any cell in the data grid to copy its value to the clipboard. A brief green **"Copied"** badge appears to confirm. Special values are handled:

| Cell Value | Copied As |
|-----------|-----------|
| `NULL` | The string `NULL` |
| Boolean | `true` or `false` |
| JSON object | Pretty-printed JSON string |
| Date | ISO 8601 string |
| Any other value | Plain string |

---

## Column Statistics Panel

![Column Statistics Panel showing null percentage, distinct count, min/max values, and top value distribution](/images/data-explorer/columns-stats.png)

Click a **column header** (not the sort icon) to open the Column Stats Panel — a slide-over sidebar that shows live statistics for that column.

### Statistics Displayed

| Stat | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Type** | Full data type with nullable indicator |
| **Flags** | Primary Key, Indexed badges |
| **Null %** | Percentage of null values |
| **Distinct Count** | Number of unique values |
| **Min / Max** | Minimum and maximum values (for numbers and dates) |
| **Data Completeness** | Visual progress bar showing non-null percentage |

### Top Values

For columns with repeated values, the panel shows the most frequent values with percentage distribution bars. This is useful for:

- Understanding enum-like columns
- Spotting data skew
- Identifying the most common categories

### Sample Preview

A set of sample values is displayed as small tokens, giving you a quick look at what kind of data the column contains without scrolling through the grid.

**Close** the panel by clicking the X button or clicking the same column header again.

---

## Pagination

The bottom bar shows cursor-based pagination:

```
1–50 of ~47.0K                                    Page 2   ◀ Prev  Next ▶
```

| Element | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| Row range | Current visible range (e.g., `1–50`) |
| Estimated total | Approximate total row count |
| Page indicator | Shown when not on page 1 |
| Prev / Next | Navigate between pages (50 rows per page) |

Pagination is **cursor-based** rather than offset-based, which means:
- Forward pagination is efficient even on tables with millions of rows
- The page counter is approximate (indicated by the `~` prefix)

---

## Row Number Column

The leftmost `#` column displays sequential row numbers relative to the current page. Row numbers automatically account for pagination offset — page 2 starts at row 51.

---

## Cell Formatting

Different data types are styled for readability:

| Data Type | Display |
|----------|---------|
| `NULL` | Italic gray "NULL" |
| `true` / `false` | Colored pill badges (green/red) |
| Foreign key values | Small arrow indicator (`→`) on hover |
| JSON objects | Serialized to string with truncation |
| Long text | Truncated with ellipsis — click to copy the full value |

---

## Empty States

The grid handles edge cases gracefully:

| State | What You See |
|-------|-------------|
| No connection selected | "No connection selected" with guidance to choose one |
| No table selected | "Select a table" with arrow pointing to sidebar |
| Table has no rows | "This table is empty" |
| Filters return no results | "No rows match your filters" with a clear link |
| Data load failure | Error message with a **Retry** button |

---

## Keyboard Shortcuts

| Action | Shortcut |
|--------|----------|
| Copy cell value | Click on any cell |
| Sort column | Click sort icon in header |
| Auto-fit column width | Double-click column border |

---

## Next Steps

- [Exporting Data](/data-explorer/exporting-data) — download tables in CSV, JSON, Excel, SQL, and more
- [AI Chat Interface](/data-explorer/ai-chat-interface) — use natural language prompts, @mentions, and chat history inside Data Explorer
- [Open Data Explorer](https://data.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) — jump back into the live app

frontmatter.title: Exporting Data
frontmatter.description: Export individual tables or entire database schemas to CSV, JSON, NDJSON, Excel, and SQL formats

data-explorer/exporting-data.content:
Exporting Data¶

The Data Explorer lets you export data in multiple formats — either a single table or your entire database schema as a ZIP archive. Exports are triggered directly from the browser and download instantly.

Quick links: Open Data Explorer · Data Explorer Overview · SQL Editor

Export Button¶

The Export button appears in the top-right action bar when you have a connection selected. Click it to open a dropdown with two sections:

  1. Export Current Table — downloads the currently selected table
  2. Export Entire Database (ZIP) — bundles all tables in the active schema into a ZIP archive

Single Table Export¶

Select a table in the sidebar, then open the Export dropdown. You'll see the table name with each format option:

Format File Extension Description
CSV .csv Comma-separated values, UTF-8 encoded. Universally compatible with spreadsheets, BI tools, and scripting languages
JSON .json Array of objects. Each row is a JSON object with column names as keys
NDJSON .ndjson Newline-delimited JSON. One JSON object per line — ideal for streaming processors, log tools, and jq
Excel .xlsx Native Excel spreadsheet. Formatted data with proper column types
SQL .sql INSERT INTO statements. Ready to replay into another database

Export Parameters¶

Parameter Default Description
Max rows 10,000 Maximum number of rows exported per table
Schema Current schema The schema of the selected table

File Naming¶

Downloaded files are named with the table name and current date:

users_2026-04-04.csv
orders_2026-04-04.xlsx
products_2026-04-04.sql

Example: CSV Export¶

Clicking users - CSV generates a file like:

id,email,name,role,created_at
1,alice@acme.io,Alice Smith,admin,2025-01-15T08:30:00Z
2,bob@corp.com,Bob Jones,user,2025-02-20T14:22:00Z
3,carol@dev.io,Carol Lee,editor,2025-03-10T09:15:00Z

Example: JSON Export¶

[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "email": "alice@acme.io",
    "name": "Alice Smith",
    "role": "admin",
    "created_at": "2025-01-15T08:30:00Z"
  },
  {
    "id": 2,
    "email": "bob@corp.com",
    "name": "Bob Jones",
    "role": "user",
    "created_at": "2025-02-20T14:22:00Z"
  }
]

Example: NDJSON Export¶

{"id":1,"email":"alice@acme.io","name":"Alice Smith","role":"admin","created_at":"2025-01-15T08:30:00Z"}
{"id":2,"email":"bob@corp.com","name":"Bob Jones","role":"user","created_at":"2025-02-20T14:22:00Z"}

Example: SQL Export¶

INSERT INTO users (id, email, name, role, created_at)
VALUES (1, 'alice@acme.io', 'Alice Smith', 'admin', '2025-01-15T08:30:00Z');

INSERT INTO users (id, email, name, role, created_at)
VALUES (2, 'bob@corp.com', 'Bob Jones', 'user', '2025-02-20T14:22:00Z');

Full Database Export (ZIP)¶

To export all tables in the current schema at once, click the green "Export Entire Database (ZIP)" section in the export dropdown.

How It Works¶

  1. Click the collapsible "Export Entire Database (ZIP)" header to reveal format options
  2. Select a format (CSV, JSON, NDJSON, Excel, or SQL)
  3. A toast notification confirms the export has started:

Exporting schema 'public'... Preparing ZIP archive of CSV files. This may take a moment depending on database size.

  1. The server bundles every table in the schema into a single ZIP file and streams it to your browser

ZIP Structure¶

schema_export_2026-04-04.zip
├── users.csv
├── orders.csv
├── products.csv
├── invoices.csv
├── sessions.csv
└── ...

Each file inside the ZIP follows the same format as a single-table export.

Timing¶

Export time depends on:

  • Number of tables in the schema
  • Total row count across all tables
  • Network speed between the server and database
  • Chosen format (SQL and Excel are slightly slower than CSV)

For large databases, the export button shows a loading spinner and disables re-clicking while the ZIP is being prepared.

Export States & Edge Cases¶

State Behavior
No connection Dropdown shows "Select a connection to export data"
No table selected Table export section shows "Select a table to export rows"
Export in progress Button shows spinning loader, dropdown is disabled
Database export Green-themed options, collapsible section, toast notification

API Reference¶

Exports use direct URL-based downloads. No authentication token is passed in the URL — the browser's session cookie handles auth.

Single Table Export¶

GET /api/data/{connectionId}/tables/{tableName}/export
    ?schemaName=public
    &maxRows=10000
    &format=csv
Parameter Type Default Description
schemaName string public Database schema
maxRows number 10000 Row limit
format string csv One of: csv, json, ndjson, excel, sql

Full Schema Export¶

GET /api/data/{connectionId}/export-all
    ?schemaName=public
    &format=csv
Parameter Type Default Description
schemaName string public Database schema to export
format string csv One of: csv, json, ndjson, excel, sql

Response: ZIP archive streamed as application/zip.

Use Cases¶

Data Migration¶

Export your schema as SQL and replay it in another environment:

# Export from Schema Weaver, then import
psql -h new-host -d newdb -f users_2026-04-04.sql

Reporting & Analysis¶

Export as CSV or Excel for use in:

  • Google Sheets / Microsoft Excel
  • Tableau / Power BI / Metabase
  • Pandas / R / Jupyter notebooks

Backup Snapshots¶

Use the full database export to create periodic data snapshots:

# Automate with curl
curl -o backup.zip \
  "https://data.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/api/data/{connId}/export-all?schemaName=public&format=csv"

Data Processing Pipelines¶

Export as NDJSON for streaming ingestion:

# Process with jq
cat users.ndjson | jq 'select(.role == "admin")' > admins.ndjson

Limits¶

Constraint Value
Max rows per table export 10,000 (configurable)
Max tables per ZIP All tables in selected schema
Supported formats CSV, JSON, NDJSON, Excel (.xlsx), SQL
Max concurrent exports 1 per browser session

Next Steps¶

  • Exploring Your Data — filtering, sorting, and column statistics
  • AI Chat Interface — use natural language to analyze and export data
  • Open Data Explorer — launch the live app
data-explorer/exporting-data.raw:
---
title: Exporting Data
description: Export individual tables or entire database schemas to CSV, JSON, NDJSON, Excel, and SQL formats
tags: [data-explorer, export, csv, json, excel, sql, download]
order: 2
sidebar_group: "Data Explorer"
related:
  - title: Data Explorer
    path: /data-explorer
  - title: Exploring Your Data
    path: /data-explorer/exploring-data
  - title: Workspace & Projects
    path: /data-explorer/ai-workspace
---

# Exporting Data

The Data Explorer lets you export data in multiple formats — either a single table or your entire database schema as a ZIP archive. Exports are triggered directly from the browser and download instantly.

> Quick links: [Open Data Explorer](https://data.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [Data Explorer Overview](https://docs.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/data-explorer/) · [SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/)

---

## Export Button

![The Export button and format selection dropdown in the Data Explorer action bar](/images/data-explorer/export.png)

The **Export** button appears in the top-right action bar when you have a connection selected. Click it to open a dropdown with two sections:

1. **Export Current Table** — downloads the currently selected table
2. **Export Entire Database (ZIP)** — bundles all tables in the active schema into a ZIP archive

---

## Single Table Export

Select a table in the sidebar, then open the Export dropdown. You'll see the table name with each format option:

| Format | File Extension | Description |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| **CSV** | `.csv` | Comma-separated values, UTF-8 encoded. Universally compatible with spreadsheets, BI tools, and scripting languages |
| **JSON** | `.json` | Array of objects. Each row is a JSON object with column names as keys |
| **NDJSON** | `.ndjson` | Newline-delimited JSON. One JSON object per line — ideal for streaming processors, log tools, and `jq` |
| **Excel** | `.xlsx` | Native Excel spreadsheet. Formatted data with proper column types |
| **SQL** | `.sql` | `INSERT INTO` statements. Ready to replay into another database |

### Export Parameters

| Parameter | Default | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| Max rows | 10,000 | Maximum number of rows exported per table |
| Schema | Current schema | The schema of the selected table |

### File Naming

Downloaded files are named with the table name and current date:

```
users_2026-04-04.csv
orders_2026-04-04.xlsx
products_2026-04-04.sql
```

### Example: CSV Export

Clicking `users - CSV` generates a file like:

```csv
id,email,name,role,created_at
1,alice@acme.io,Alice Smith,admin,2025-01-15T08:30:00Z
2,bob@corp.com,Bob Jones,user,2025-02-20T14:22:00Z
3,carol@dev.io,Carol Lee,editor,2025-03-10T09:15:00Z
```

### Example: JSON Export

```json
[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "email": "alice@acme.io",
    "name": "Alice Smith",
    "role": "admin",
    "created_at": "2025-01-15T08:30:00Z"
  },
  {
    "id": 2,
    "email": "bob@corp.com",
    "name": "Bob Jones",
    "role": "user",
    "created_at": "2025-02-20T14:22:00Z"
  }
]
```

### Example: NDJSON Export

```json
{"id":1,"email":"alice@acme.io","name":"Alice Smith","role":"admin","created_at":"2025-01-15T08:30:00Z"}
{"id":2,"email":"bob@corp.com","name":"Bob Jones","role":"user","created_at":"2025-02-20T14:22:00Z"}
```

### Example: SQL Export

```sql
INSERT INTO users (id, email, name, role, created_at)
VALUES (1, 'alice@acme.io', 'Alice Smith', 'admin', '2025-01-15T08:30:00Z');

INSERT INTO users (id, email, name, role, created_at)
VALUES (2, 'bob@corp.com', 'Bob Jones', 'user', '2025-02-20T14:22:00Z');
```

---

## Full Database Export (ZIP)

To export **all tables** in the current schema at once, click the green **"Export Entire Database (ZIP)"** section in the export dropdown.

### How It Works

1. Click the collapsible "Export Entire Database (ZIP)" header to reveal format options
2. Select a format (CSV, JSON, NDJSON, Excel, or SQL)
3. A toast notification confirms the export has started:

> *Exporting schema 'public'... Preparing ZIP archive of CSV files. This may take a moment depending on database size.*

4. The server bundles every table in the schema into a single ZIP file and streams it to your browser

### ZIP Structure

```
schema_export_2026-04-04.zip
├── users.csv
├── orders.csv
├── products.csv
├── invoices.csv
├── sessions.csv
└── ...
```

Each file inside the ZIP follows the same format as a single-table export.

### Timing

Export time depends on:
- Number of tables in the schema
- Total row count across all tables
- Network speed between the server and database
- Chosen format (SQL and Excel are slightly slower than CSV)

> For large databases, the export button shows a loading spinner and disables re-clicking while the ZIP is being prepared.

---

## Export States & Edge Cases

| State | Behavior |
|-------|----------|
| **No connection** | Dropdown shows "Select a connection to export data" |
| **No table selected** | Table export section shows "Select a table to export rows" |
| **Export in progress** | Button shows spinning loader, dropdown is disabled |
| **Database export** | Green-themed options, collapsible section, toast notification |

---

## API Reference

Exports use direct URL-based downloads. No authentication token is passed in the URL — the browser's session cookie handles auth.

### Single Table Export

```
GET /api/data/{connectionId}/tables/{tableName}/export
    ?schemaName=public
    &maxRows=10000
    &format=csv
```

| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |
|-----------|------|---------|-------------|
| `schemaName` | string | `public` | Database schema |
| `maxRows` | number | `10000` | Row limit |
| `format` | string | `csv` | One of: `csv`, `json`, `ndjson`, `excel`, `sql` |

### Full Schema Export

```
GET /api/data/{connectionId}/export-all
    ?schemaName=public
    &format=csv
```

| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |
|-----------|------|---------|-------------|
| `schemaName` | string | `public` | Database schema to export |
| `format` | string | `csv` | One of: `csv`, `json`, `ndjson`, `excel`, `sql` |

Response: ZIP archive streamed as `application/zip`.

---

## Use Cases

### Data Migration

Export your schema as SQL and replay it in another environment:

```bash
# Export from Schema Weaver, then import
psql -h new-host -d newdb -f users_2026-04-04.sql
```

### Reporting & Analysis

Export as CSV or Excel for use in:
- Google Sheets / Microsoft Excel
- Tableau / Power BI / Metabase
- Pandas / R / Jupyter notebooks

### Backup Snapshots

Use the full database export to create periodic data snapshots:

```bash
# Automate with curl
curl -o backup.zip \
  "https://data.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/api/data/{connId}/export-all?schemaName=public&format=csv"
```

### Data Processing Pipelines

Export as NDJSON for streaming ingestion:

```bash
# Process with jq
cat users.ndjson | jq 'select(.role == "admin")' > admins.ndjson
```

---

## Limits

| Constraint | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Max rows per table export | 10,000 (configurable) |
| Max tables per ZIP | All tables in selected schema |
| Supported formats | CSV, JSON, NDJSON, Excel (.xlsx), SQL |
| Max concurrent exports | 1 per browser session |

---

## Next Steps

- [Exploring Your Data](/data-explorer/exploring-data) — filtering, sorting, and column statistics
- [AI Chat Interface](/data-explorer/ai-chat-interface) — use natural language to analyze and export data
- [Open Data Explorer](https://data.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) — launch the live app

headings.title: What can you do in Data Explorer?

How do you access Data Explorer?

data-explorer.content:
Data Explorer¶

The Data Explorer is a built-in SQL workbench that lets you connect to your databases, run queries, and explore your data — all inside Schema Weaver. It integrates with Resona AI so you can ask questions in plain English and get runnable SQL back instantly.

Quick links: Open Data Explorer · Docs Home · Schema Weaver Landing · SQL Editor

What can you do in Data Explorer?¶

  • Browse live data — Two-panel layout with a table sidebar and a high-performance data grid
  • Smart column sizing — Canvas-measured, 95th-percentile column widths that auto-fit your data
  • Filter & sort — Server-side filtering with typed operators and one-click column sorting
  • Toggle columns — Show/hide columns to focus on the data that matters
  • Column statistics — Click any column header to see null %, distinct count, top values, and more
  • Click-to-copy — Click any cell to copy its value to the clipboard
  • Export data — Download single tables or entire schemas as CSV, JSON, NDJSON, Excel, or SQL
  • Full database export — ZIP archive of every table in the current schema
  • Write and run SQL — Full-featured Monaco editor with syntax highlighting and autocomplete
  • AI-assisted analysis — Resona AI writes SQL, analyzes tables, generates visual charts, and compiles PowerPoint reports.
  • Agentic Workflows — Thinking, Tool Execution, and Synthesis phases for deep data exploration.
  • Rich Artifacts — Interactive tables, charts, stat blocks, and Mermaid diagrams embedded in chat.
  • Workspace Storage — Persist generated reports, slide decks, and PDF exports.

Which databases does Data Explorer support today?¶

Database Version Status
PostgreSQL 12+ Fully supported

How do you access Data Explorer?¶

Cloud Platform¶

Open data.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com and click Data Explorer in the left sidebar if you're entering from the main Schema Weaver experience.

Self-hosted / CLI¶

If you're running the Schema Weaver web interface locally:

schema-weaver ui
# Starting UI at http://localhost:4242

Navigate to http://localhost:4242/explorer in your browser.

Overview of the Interface¶

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Connection ▾  │  Filter  Columns  ↻  Export │  ~24.5K rows    │
├──────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Schema ▸ public │  #  │ 🔑 id    │ email          │ role       │
│  ──────────────  │     │ int4     │ varchar        │ varchar?   │
│  🔍 Filter...    │  ── │ ──────── │ ────────────── │ ────────── │
│  ──────────────  │  1  │ 1        │ alice@acme.io  │ admin      │
│  24 tables       │  2  │ 2        │ bob@corp.com   │ user       │
│  ──────────────  │  3  │ 3        │ carol@dev.io   │ editor     │
│  ▸ users    47K  │  4  │ 4        │ dave@org.com   │ [NULL]     │
│  ▸ orders   31K  │  …  │ …        │ …              │ …          │
│  ▸ products 12K  │─────┴──────────┴────────────────┴────────────│
│  ▸ invoices  8K  │  1–50 of ~47.0K                ◀ Prev  Next │
│  ──────────────  │                                              │
│  ⚙ Settings  AI │                                              │
└──────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Left Sidebar¶

  • Schema Selector — Dropdown to switch between database schemas
  • Table Search — Filter the table list instantly as you type
  • Table List — Scrollable list with row estimates, PK warnings, and AI shortcuts
  • Resona AI [AI] — Toggle the integrated agentic chat interface
  • Dashboard [D] — View your pinned charts and metrics
  • Workspace [W] — Manage persisted files and reports

Right Panel — Data Grid¶

  • Action Bar — Filter controls, column visibility toggle, refresh, export button, row count
  • Column Headers — Type badges, PK/FK indicators, sortable, resizable, click for statistics
  • Row Grid — Paginated rows with click-to-copy, NULL formatting, boolean badges, and FK arrows
  • Pagination — Cursor-based, 50 rows per page, with row range and estimated total

Quick Start Workflow¶

  1. Open Data Explorer and select a PostgreSQL connection from the connection selector — connections are added from the SQL Editor
  2. Browse the schema tree on the left to find a table
  3. Click the table name to open a quick preview
  4. Use AI Assist or the chat interface if you need help writing a query
  5. Use @table_name in the AI chat to focus the analysis on specific context
  6. Pin interesting charts to your dashboard for ongoing monitoring

Resona AI Integration¶

Resona AI is more than just a chatbot; it's a co-pilot for your data. It understands your schema and can perform complex multi-step analysis without you writing a single line of SQL.

  • Mentions — Type @ to select tables and provide context to the agent.
  • Live Progress — Watch the agent plan its steps and execute tools against your database in real-time.
  • Rich Visualization — Get bar charts, line graphs, and correlation matrices instantly.
  • Professional Reports — Ask Resona to "generate a slide deck for investors" and download it as a PPTX from your workspace.

Security and Permissions¶

By default the Data Explorer operates in read-only mode, meaning only SELECT statements are allowed. This protects production data from accidental mutations.

To enable write access for a specific connection:

{
  "resona": {
    "readOnly": false,
    "allowedConnections": ["development"]
  }
}

Best practice: Keep readOnly: true for any production connection. Use a read-replica if you need heavy analytical queries.

Row-Level Data Masking¶

For sensitive columns (PII, payment data), configure automatic masking:

{
  "resona": {
    "masking": [
      { "table": "users", "column": "email", "type": "email" },
      { "table": "users", "column": "phone_number", "type": "phone" },
      { "table": "payments", "column": "card_last4", "type": "redact" }
    ]
  }
}

Masked columns display as a***@***.com, ***-***-1234, or [REDACTED].

Next Steps¶

  • Exploring Your Data — filters, column stats, smart sizing, and grid features
  • Exporting Data — CSV, JSON, Excel, SQL, and full-database ZIP export
  • AI Chat Interface — @mentions, model selection, and voice input
  • Agentic Workflows — understanding planning, tools, and synthesis
  • Artifacts & Visualizations — interactive charts, stats, and dashboard pinning
  • Workspace & Organization — projects, file persistence, and artifact history

Related Schema Weaver Links¶

  • Open Data Explorer — launch the live Data Explorer app
  • Open SQL Editor — move into deeper SQL workflows
  • Schema Weaver Landing — product overview and entry point
  • Docs Home — browse the rest of the documentation

> Quick links: [Open Data Explorer](https://data.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [Docs Home](https://docs.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [Schema Weaver Landing](https://schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) · [SQL Editor](https://sql.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/)

- **Browse live data** — Two-panel layout with a table sidebar and a high-performance data grid

# Starting UI at http://localhost:4242

Navigate to `http://localhost:4242/explorer` in your browser.

---

## Overview of the Interface

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Connection ▾  │  Filter  Columns  ↻  Export │  ~24.5K rows    │
├──────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Schema ▸ public │  #  │ 🔑 id    │ email          │ role       │
│  ──────────────  │     │ int4     │ varchar        │ varchar?   │
│  🔍 Filter...    │  ── │ ──────── │ ────────────── │ ────────── │
│  ──────────────  │  1  │ 1        │ alice@acme.io  │ admin      │
│  24 tables       │  2  │ 2        │ bob@corp.com   │ user       │
│  ──────────────  │  3  │ 3        │ carol@dev.io   │ editor     │
│  ▸ users    47K  │  4  │ 4        │ dave@org.com   │ [NULL]     │
│  ▸ orders   31K  │  …  │ …        │ …              │ …          │
│  ▸ products 12K  │─────┴──────────┴────────────────┴────────────│
│  ▸ invoices  8K  │  1–50 of ~47.0K                ◀ Prev  Next │
│  ──────────────  │                                              │
│  ⚙ Settings  AI │                                              │
└──────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Left Sidebar

- **Resona AI [AI]** — Toggle the integrated agentic chat interface

---

## Quick Start Workflow

1. Open [Data Explorer](https://data.schemaweaver.vivekmind.com/) and select a PostgreSQL connection from the connection selector — connections are added from the [SQL Editor](/database/adding-a-connection)
2. Browse the schema tree on the left to find a table
3. Click the table name to open a quick preview
4. Use **AI Assist** or the chat interface if you need help writing a query
5. Use `@table_name` in the AI chat to focus the analysis on specific context
6. Pin interesting charts to your dashboard for ongoing monitoring

---

## Resona AI Integration

Resona AI is more than just a chatbot; it's a co-pilot for your data. It understands your schema and can perform complex multi-step analysis without you writing a single line of SQL.

- **Mentions** — Type `@` to select tables and provide context to the agent.
- **Live Progress** — Watch the agent plan its steps and execute tools against your database in real-time.
- **Rich Visualization** — Get bar charts, line graphs, and correlation matrices instantly.

By default the Data Explorer operates in **read-only mode**, meaning only `SELECT` statements are allowed. This protects production data from accidental mutations.

For sensitive columns (PII, payment data), configure automatic masking:

Masked columns display as `a***@***.com`, `***-***-1234`, or `[REDACTED]`.

frontmatter.title: AI Chat Guide
frontmatter.description: How to write effective prompts, use @mentions, iterate on results, and get the best answers from Resona AI in both the SQL Editor and Data Explorer

resona-ai/ai-chat.content:
AI Chat Guide¶

Resona AI is available in two surfaces — the SQL Editor and the Data Explorer. Both share the same chat mechanics: a streaming conversation with tool execution, model selection, and full context of your schema. This page covers how to use the chat interface effectively in both places.

Opening the Chat¶

SQL Editor¶

Click Resona AI in the left sidebar panel of the SQL Editor. The panel stays open as you navigate files and maintains your full conversation history. You can also open it with Ctrl+K / Cmd+K when a file or table is selected.

Data Explorer¶

Click the Resona AI tab in the Data Explorer, or press Ctrl+Shift+A. The chat panel opens alongside the data grid and query results.

How Context Works¶

Resona automatically reads your schema before every response. You do not need to paste table definitions or describe your schema in the prompt.

SQL Editor AI context includes:

  • Every file in your active project (all merged SQL)
  • The compiled schema graph (tables, views, relationships)
  • The compiler's grade and issue list
  • Your conversation history

Data Explorer AI context includes:

  • Your connected database's schema (tables, columns, types, constraints, FKs)
  • The currently selected table or query
  • Column statistics from recent scans
  • Your conversation history

@ Mentions¶

In both surfaces, type @ followed by a name to reference a specific object. An autocomplete dropdown filters as you type:

What indexes are missing on @orders?
Add a soft-delete column to @users
Explain the relationship between @invoices and @subscriptions
Show me the top 10 rows of @products ordered by price

@ mentions work with table names, file names (SQL Editor), columns, and schema groups. Using them makes Resona focus on exactly the object you mean rather than guessing from context.

Writing Effective Prompts¶

Be Specific About Scope¶

Vague prompts get vague responses. Narrow the scope to get precise, actionable output:

Instead of Try
"Fix my schema" "What constraints are missing from the @orders table?"
"Make this faster" "What indexes would improve queries on @events filtered by user_id and created_at?"
"Analyze my data" "Show me the 5 customers with the highest total spend this month from @invoices"
"Add auth" "Create a sessions table linked to @users with expiry, device info, and IP address"

Give Business Context¶

Resona understands naming conventions, but business context makes the output much better:

✓ "Add a status column to @orders — values are: pending, paid, shipped, refunded, cancelled"
✗ "Add a status column to orders"

✓ "The `amount_cents` column stores values in cents (divide by 100 for dollars)"
✗ "Show revenue" (Resona may not know the unit)

Reference Exact Names¶

When your column or table names are abbreviated or non-obvious, mention them directly:

"The usr_sub_stat column on @users tracks subscription status — what values does it have?"
"Add an index to @evts.ts — that's the timestamp column for the events table"

Iterative Refinement¶

The most productive way to use Resona is to start simple and refine. Each message builds on the previous one — you don't need to repeat context.

SQL Editor Example¶

You:   Create a multi-tenant organizations table with name, slug, and plan

AI:    [generates CREATE TABLE organizations ...]

You:   Add RLS so users can only see their own org — there's a user_org junction table

AI:    [adds RLS + policy referencing user_org]

You:   Run the compiler and show me what's still failing

AI:    [calls compile tool → reports 1 warning: missing index on user_org.user_id]

You:   Fix it

AI:    [patches the file with the missing index]

Data Explorer Example¶

You:   How many orders were placed each day this week?

AI:    [runs query, shows result table with daily counts]

You:   Turn that into a line chart

AI:    [regenerates as chart]

You:   Only show days where order count exceeded 100

AI:    [adds HAVING or WHERE clause, updates chart]

Choosing the Right Model¶

Use the model selector in the chat footer to switch models. The footer shows the active model and its credit multiplier.

Situation Recommended tier
Quick question, simple table FAST — low cost, fast response
Multi-table analysis, 3–5 tables SMART — better reasoning, still efficient
Complex schema design, 50+ tables ADVANCED — GPT-4o or Claude Sonnet
Very large schema, deep refactoring POWERFUL — Claude Opus, Kimi K2
You're iterating quickly on many messages FAST — save ADVANCED credits for harder questions

Start with a FAST or SMART model. If the answer is wrong or incomplete, switch to ADVANCED for that specific question.

See Models and Credits for full tier details and credit costs.

Streaming — Watching Resona Work¶

Resona streams its output in real time. As it processes your message, you see:

  • Tool calls: the name of each tool and a one-line result summary
  • Reasoning: the model's chain of thought between tool calls (where available)
  • Answer: the final response streamed word by word

This transparency helps you catch cases where Resona is heading in the wrong direction — you can interrupt mid-stream and redirect.

When Resona Gets It Wrong¶

AI is not perfect. If a response is incorrect or incomplete:

  1. Tell it directly — "That index is wrong — it should be on user_id, not on email"
  2. Be more specific — mention the exact column or table name
  3. Switch models — some questions are better suited to ADVANCED or POWERFUL tiers
  4. Copy and edit — in the Data Explorer, click Copy to editor to take the query and fix it yourself; in the SQL Editor, you can edit the generated SQL before applying

Conversation History and Memory¶

Within a Conversation¶

Resona keeps the full context of your conversation — you never need to re-explain what table you're working on or what you're trying to do.

Across Sessions (SQL Editor)¶

The SQL Editor AI has persistent memory across sessions. It stores summaries of past conversations (tied to your project and user ID). When you return the next day, Resona remembers the schema work you were doing. This is a four-layer compression model — recent messages verbatim, older messages as summaries, long-term facts extracted from your full history.

Data Explorer¶

The Data Explorer AI maintains conversation history within a session. Start a new conversation with the New Conversation button to clear context. Previous conversations are saved in your history panel for 30 days.

Saving Useful Queries¶

In the Data Explorer, save any AI-generated query to your query library:

  1. Click Save on any query in the chat
  2. Give it a name
  3. Access it from Saved Queries in the Explorer sidebar

Saved queries run directly — they do not consume AI credits when re-run.

In the SQL Editor, AI-applied SQL changes are saved to your project files automatically and tracked in Version History.

Tips for Large Schemas¶

  • Use @ mentions — with 100+ tables, explicit references keep Resona focused
  • Use Visibility filters — in the SQL Editor, hide node types you're not working with to reduce context size
  • Use ADVANCED or POWERFUL models — larger schemas need models with larger context windows
  • Scope your question — instead of "analyze my schema," try "analyze the billing schema for missing indexes"
  • Use subgraph focus — click the Network icon on any table in the ER Diagram to scope to just that table and its neighborhood, then ask AI about it

Limitations¶

Limitation Detail
Row data access SQL Editor AI never sees your row data — only schema. Data Explorer AI runs SELECT queries only.
Write access Data Explorer AI is read-only — it cannot INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE rows.
Cross-database Both surfaces work with one database connection at a time
Schema size Very large schemas (1,000+ tables) may require scoping questions to a specific schema or table group
Real-time streaming data Data Explorer queries run at request time; no continuous streaming

Next Steps¶

  • Models and Credits — tiers, credit costs, and plan limits
  • Resona AI in the SQL Editor — the full agentic schema assistant
  • Resona AI in the Data Explorer — live data analysis and reporting

# AI Chat Guide

Resona AI is available in two surfaces — the SQL Editor and the Data Explorer. Both share the same chat mechanics: a streaming conversation with tool execution, model selection, and full context of your schema. This page covers how to use the chat interface effectively in both places.

---

## Opening the Chat

### SQL Editor

Click **Resona AI** in the left sidebar panel of the SQL Editor. The panel stays open as you navigate files and maintains your full conversation history. You can also open it with `Ctrl+K` / `Cmd+K` when a file or table is selected.

### Data Explorer

Click the **Resona AI** tab in the Data Explorer, or press `Ctrl+Shift+A`. The chat panel opens alongside the data grid and query results.

---

## How Context Works

Resona automatically reads your schema before every response. You do not need to paste table definitions or describe your schema in the prompt.

**SQL Editor AI context includes:**
- Every file in your active project (all merged SQL)
- The compiled schema graph (tables, views, relationships)
- The compiler's grade and issue list
- Your conversation history

**Data Explorer AI context includes:**
- Your connected database's schema (tables, columns, types, constraints, FKs)
- The currently selected table or query
- Column statistics from recent scans
- Your conversation history

### @ Mentions

In both surfaces, type `@` followed by a name to reference a specific object. An autocomplete dropdown filters as you type:

```
What indexes are missing on @orders?
Add a soft-delete column to @users
Explain the relationship between @invoices and @subscriptions
Show me the top 10 rows of @products ordered by price
```

`@` mentions work with table names, file names (SQL Editor), columns, and schema groups. Using them makes Resona focus on exactly the object you mean rather than guessing from context.

---

## Writing Effective Prompts

### Be Specific About Scope

Vague prompts get vague responses. Narrow the scope to get precise, actionable output:

| Instead of | Try |
|---|---|
| "Fix my schema" | "What constraints are missing from the @orders table?" |
| "Make this faster" | "What indexes would improve queries on @events filtered by user_id and created_at?" |
| "Analyze my data" | "Show me the 5 customers with the highest total spend this month from @invoices" |
| "Add auth" | "Create a sessions table linked to @users with expiry, device info, and IP address" |

### Give Business Context

Resona understands naming conventions, but business context makes the output much better:

```
✓ "Add a status column to @orders — values are: pending, paid, shipped, refunded, cancelled"
✗ "Add a status column to orders"

✓ "The `amount_cents` column stores values in cents (divide by 100 for dollars)"
✗ "Show revenue" (Resona may not know the unit)
```

### Reference Exact Names

When your column or table names are abbreviated or non-obvious, mention them directly:

```
"The usr_sub_stat column on @users tracks subscription status — what values does it have?"
"Add an index to @evts.ts — that's the timestamp column for the events table"
```

---

## Iterative Refinement

The most productive way to use Resona is to start simple and refine. Each message builds on the previous one — you don't need to repeat context.

### SQL Editor Example

```
You:   Create a multi-tenant organizations table with name, slug, and plan

AI:    [generates CREATE TABLE organizations ...]

You:   Add RLS so users can only see their own org — there's a user_org junction table

AI:    [adds RLS + policy referencing user_org]

You:   Run the compiler and show me what's still failing

AI:    [calls compile tool → reports 1 warning: missing index on user_org.user_id]

You:   Fix it

AI:    [patches the file with the missing index]
```

### Data Explorer Example

```
You:   How many orders were placed each day this week?

AI:    [runs query, shows result table with daily counts]

You:   Turn that into a line chart

AI:    [regenerates as chart]

You:   Only show days where order count exceeded 100

AI:    [adds HAVING or WHERE clause, updates chart]
```

---

## Choosing the Right Model

Use the model selector in the chat footer to switch models. The footer shows the active model and its credit multiplier.

| Situation | Recommended tier |
|---|---|
| Quick question, simple table | **FAST** — low cost, fast response |
| Multi-table analysis, 3–5 tables | **SMART** — better reasoning, still efficient |
| Complex schema design, 50+ tables | **ADVANCED** — GPT-4o or Claude Sonnet |
| Very large schema, deep refactoring | **POWERFUL** — Claude Opus, Kimi K2 |
| You're iterating quickly on many messages | **FAST** — save ADVANCED credits for harder questions |

Start with a FAST or SMART model. If the answer is wrong or incomplete, switch to ADVANCED for that specific question.

See [Models and Credits](/resona-ai/models) for full tier details and credit costs.

---

## Streaming — Watching Resona Work

Resona streams its output in real time. As it processes your message, you see:

- **Tool calls**: the name of each tool and a one-line result summary
- **Reasoning**: the model's chain of thought between tool calls (where available)
- **Answer**: the final response streamed word by word

This transparency helps you catch cases where Resona is heading in the wrong direction — you can interrupt mid-stream and redirect.

---

## When Resona Gets It Wrong

AI is not perfect. If a response is incorrect or incomplete:

1. **Tell it directly** — *"That index is wrong — it should be on user_id, not on email"*
2. **Be more specific** — mention the exact column or table name
3. **Switch models** — some questions are better suited to ADVANCED or POWERFUL tiers
4. **Copy and edit** — in the Data Explorer, click **Copy to editor** to take the query and fix it yourself; in the SQL Editor, you can edit the generated SQL before applying

---

## Conversation History and Memory

### Within a Conversation

Resona keeps the full context of your conversation — you never need to re-explain what table you're working on or what you're trying to do.

### Across Sessions (SQL Editor)

The SQL Editor AI has **persistent memory** across sessions. It stores summaries of past conversations (tied to your project and user ID). When you return the next day, Resona remembers the schema work you were doing. This is a four-layer compression model — recent messages verbatim, older messages as summaries, long-term facts extracted from your full history.

### Data Explorer

The Data Explorer AI maintains conversation history within a session. Start a new conversation with the **New Conversation** button to clear context. Previous conversations are saved in your history panel for 30 days.

---

## Saving Useful Queries

In the **Data Explorer**, save any AI-generated query to your query library:
1. Click **Save** on any query in the chat
2. Give it a name
3. Access it from **Saved Queries** in the Explorer sidebar

Saved queries run directly — they do not consume AI credits when re-run.

In the **SQL Editor**, AI-applied SQL changes are saved to your project files automatically and tracked in [Version History](/sql-editor/version-history).

---

## Tips for Large Schemas

- **Use @ mentions** — with 100+ tables, explicit references keep Resona focused
- **Use Visibility filters** — in the SQL Editor, hide node types you're not working with to reduce context size
- **Use ADVANCED or POWERFUL models** — larger schemas need models with larger context windows
- **Scope your question** — instead of "analyze my schema," try "analyze the billing schema for missing indexes"
- **Use subgraph focus** — click the Network icon on any table in the ER Diagram to scope to just that table and its neighborhood, then ask AI about it

---

## Limitations

| Limitation | Detail |
|---|---|
| Row data access | SQL Editor AI never sees your row data — only schema. Data Explorer AI runs SELECT queries only. |
| Write access | Data Explorer AI is read-only — it cannot INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE rows. |
| Cross-database | Both surfaces work with one database connection at a time |
| Schema size | Very large schemas (1,000+ tables) may require scoping questions to a specific schema or table group |
| Real-time streaming data | Data Explorer queries run at request time; no continuous streaming |

---

## Next Steps

- [Models and Credits](/resona-ai/models) — tiers, credit costs, and plan limits
- [Resona AI in the SQL Editor](/sql-editor/ai-assistant) — the full agentic schema assistant
- [Resona AI in the Data Explorer](/data-explorer/ai-overview) — live data analysis and reporting
